if I had the energy — which I did.

**Impact delivered:** **Algorithm curriculum — 81 problems, fully written up.**
Solved every problem in ByteDance's MarsCode Youth Training
Camp Juejin algorithm track and published them as a public
teaching repository at *juejin-algorithm-practice* (66
commits, 100% solo). Each problem ships with a per-problem
README (problem statement, examples, solution approach,
complexity analysis) and a tested Python implementation.
Topics span arrays/strings, dynamic programming, greedy
algorithms, math and number theory, binary search, graphs and
trees, bit manipulation, sorting and searching, simulation,
and encoding/decoding — the breadth a Chinese backend
engineer is expected to demonstrate at interview.

**Team 115 capstone — Douyin Mall backend.** Shipped backend
features on the team's Spring Boot 3 e-commerce platform
covering user management, product management, shopping cart,
order processing, and an AI customer-service module. The
repo carries 174 commits from 8 contributors; **my 45
commits placed me as the #2-most-prolific committer on the
team** (~26% of the codebase's commit history), behind only
the team lead. Stack: Java 17 + Spring Boot 3 + MyBatis +
MySQL + Redis + Alibaba Cloud services + Docker, with the
kind of microservice-style internal layering the camp's
evaluation rubric expects.

**Frontend supplement — built solo to integrate against the
team backend.** Authored a separate Vue 3 + Vite + TypeScript
+ Pinia + Vue Router frontend (`douyin-mall-frontend`, 15
commits, 100% mine) with end-to-end Cypress and Vitest unit
coverage, so the team's backend had a clean live-integration
target. This wasn't required by the camp; I wrote it to close
the loop on the system as a working product.

**Two open-source teaching templates distilled from the
capstone.** The camp's full-stack architecture had enough
reusable shape to be worth packaging as starter kits for the
next cohort of learners, so I built and published two
clean-architecture skeletons:

- **`douyin-mall-go-template`** (a.k.a. "TikTok Shop Go
  Template") — Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis backend with
  standard `cmd/`, `internal/`, `api/`, `pkg/`, `configs/`,
  `docs/`, `frontend/`, `logs/` layout, JWT auth, RESTful
  APIs, and a paired React frontend — 27 commits, 100%
  solo, bilingual EN + 简体中文 README. This is the same
  repository now featured as one of the eight projects on
  my GitHub Open Source primary table.
- **`douyin-mall-java-template`** — the Spring Boot + Maven
  counterpart with Maven wrapper, conventional `src/main` /
  `src/test` layout — 13 commits, 100% solo, bilingual
  README. Intended as the Java track's mirror of the Go
  template.

The five repositories together — algorithm track, capstone
backend, supplementary frontend, two architecture templates
— represent a deliberately overcomplete portfolio of camp
artefacts: most trainees deliver the algorithm submissions
and the team capstone; very few add a solo frontend and two
production-shape architecture templates on top.

**Technical highlights:** **Java / Spring Boot 3 + MyBatis + MySQL + Redis +
Alibaba Cloud** on the Team-115 capstone, which is the
canonical Chinese-internet backend stack. The architecture
is layered along the lines mainstream ByteDance projects
use — controller / service / mapper separation, Redis-
backed caching for hot product and cart paths, MyBatis for
explicit SQL control rather than JPA's abstraction, and
Alibaba Cloud services for OSS / SMS / message queue
integration. This is the stack a Chinese backend engineer
is expected to have shipped in production.

**Go + Gin clean-architecture template** in
`douyin-mall-go-template`. The directory shape (`cmd/`,
`internal/`, `pkg/`, `api/`, `configs/`, `docs/`, `logs/`,
`frontend/`) is the layout the Go community treats as
production-correct — separation between executable entry
points, internal packages, and reusable libraries; explicit
config and docs trees; logs as a deployment artefact. The
template ships JWT authentication, RESTful API conventions,
and a complete e-commerce domain model so that a learner
forking it has the shape of a real service rather than a
scaffolded stub.

**Vue 3 + Vite + Pinia + Vue Router + TypeScript** on the
frontend supplement, with a non-trivial testing pyramid
(Vitest for unit, Cypress for e2e via `start-server-and-
test`) — explicitly the configuration that lets you run
e2e tests both in CI (`vite preview` + `cypress run`) and
interactively (`vite dev` + `cypress open`).

**Algorithm fluency across ten topic areas** — the 81
problems on Juejin span the topic distribution Chinese
big-tech interview panels actually test, including the
weaker spots most candidates have (bit manipulation,
simulation, encoding/decoding) rather than just the Leetcode
favourites (arrays, DP). Each solution has explicit
complexity analysis, which is how the camp graded them.

**Bilingual technical documentation discipline** — every
repository I authored or led for the camp ships
side-by-side English and 简体中文 READMEs at production
quality. This is the documentation expectation inside a
Chinese megacorp with global products, and it's a habit I
carried out of the camp into every subsequent project.

**Team collaboration at the right scale** — being the #2
committer on an 8-person team taught the specific muscles
that solo projects don't: PR review etiquette, integration
conflict resolution, sharing test fixtures across modules,
and writing commit messages that another contributor will
read.
### Full Stack Developer & Lead Documenter (Lincoln University COMP693 Industry Placement) at CORDE (NZ infrastructure & civil construction)
[tier=secondary · recency=historical · updated=2026-05-18]
- **Period:** Jun 2024 -- Nov 2024
- **Location:** Canterbury, New Zealand
- **URL:** https://corde.nz/

**Impact:** #1-most-prolific contributor (42% of 539 commits) on CORDE's offline-first React Native field-operations app — still widely used today by CORDE's water/civil-infrastructure crews. Also owned 97.9% of the documentation track end-to-end.

**Company context:** **CORDE** is a Canterbury-based **New Zealand infrastructure
and civil-construction company** — 30+ years of operations,
roughly 164 staff (LinkedIn category 201–500), trading
nationally under the tagline *"Trusted Connections."* In
February 2022 three separate Canterbury entities were unified
under the CORDE brand. The company operates across three
specialist arms — **Civil Construction**, **Regional Asset
Maintenance** (open-space management; introduced autonomous
mowing tech to NZ via partnerships with Colbrook Industries
and RC Mowers USA), and **Water Infrastructure / three waters**
— across three Canterbury offices: head office at **85
Hoskyns Road, Rolleston**, plus **Timaru** (South Canterbury)
and **Rangiora** (North Canterbury). Recognised projects
include Te Whāriki Subdivision Stage 3, Windsorcare Village
Stage 1, and the Ellesmere-to-Pines Pipeline Project; awards
include **CCNZ Canterbury Contractor of the Year** and a
**Construction Excellence National Award**. CORDE recently
acquired Arnold Jensen Electrical (70+-year-old NZ electrical
specialist) to bring electrical capability in-house. This
is a substantial regional employer, not a small client.

The engagement was a **Lincoln University COMP693 industry-
placement capstone** — the practicum module of the Master of
Applied Computing programme — embedded directly inside CORDE
with weekly on-site meetings at their Canterbury offices,
distributed online work between meetings, and CORDE's own
engineering processes (Azure DevOps for project management,
Figma for design hand-off, Apifox + Swagger for API contract
testing) governing how the work shipped. Five months from
June 2024 kickoff to November 2024 delivery.

**Mission:** Solve a concrete operational problem inside a working
infrastructure firm: CORDE's field crews — water-
infrastructure technicians, civil-construction site teams,
regional-asset-maintenance operators — routinely work in
rural and semi-rural Canterbury where mobile network coverage
is unreliable. The company's central operations platform
(referred to as "Workbench" in the API contracts) is web-
based and assumes connectivity. The mobile app's job was to
make the field worker's tablet the **system of record for
the moment of work**, capture text and photos and GPS
simultaneously, hold it locally with integrity guarantees,
and synchronise back to Workbench cleanly when connectivity
returned — including handling the conflict cases that real
offline-first systems have to handle. This is a real
productivity problem worth real engineering for a 164-staff
infrastructure operator.

**My mandate:** Full-stack developer on a two-active-developer Lincoln
University team (myself plus Luke Shi / Zhenzhou Shi),
reporting directly to **Barry Clark** on CORDE's side as
the business sponsor and to **Kullum Ladley** as CORDE's
technical liaison and Workbench API specification author.
Mandate covered: front-end implementation across the entire
React Native + Native Base UI layer, database design (ERD
work co-designed with CORDE's existing database slices),
business-logic diagrams and architectural documentation,
weekly sprint-meeting question sets sent to CORDE, stage-
development presentation decks, live app demos and screen
recordings, and APK packaging for weekly CORDE reviews.
Documentation lead was an explicit role inside the team —
the docs repository carries a top-level folder literally
titled "*Chan Meng's Documentation Contribution Collection*"
with separate sub-folders for each of those mandates.

**Impact delivered:** **Shipped a production-quality React Native offline-data-
collection mobile app** for CORDE's field-operations
workflow. The codebase carries **539 commits across three
contributors**; **my 227 commits (combining the Chan Meng
and Chan handles) make me the #1-most-prolific contributor
— 42% of the entire commit history** — with Luke Shi (the
repo owner under his GitHub handle gentoo111) on 28.6% and
Xue Zheng on 25.8%. The app integrates with CORDE's
Workbench central operations platform via the API contract
documented across a sequence of **15 specifications
authored by Kullum Ladley** (the same Kullum named in every
API document's filename prefix `YYYYMMDD_Kullum_...`),
covering Workbench API calls, log creation, mobile-app
authentication, log extension fields, file-upload pipelines,
and group completion workflow — all captured under version
control as the API/ folder of the team's documentation
repository.

**Owned the documentation track end-to-end.** The team's
documentation repository (`team-corde`) has 143 commits;
**my 140 (97.9%) make me effectively the sole author.**
The repo contains: day-by-day weekly project work records
(the visible diary of the engagement across five months —
every UI library evaluation, every database-schema
iteration, every employer-email outcome, every offline
meeting decision), the project description and user-stories
archive, the full API documentation set from CORDE, and
seven separate sub-folders enumerating my specific
contributions — business logic diagrams, presentation
decks, database design, UI/UX and front-end implementation,
live demos and screen recordings, APK packaging, and
weekly sprint question sets.

**Practised the soft skills the capstone is designed to
teach.** Translated CORDE's abstract user stories into
concrete Azure DevOps work items; co-designed the database
schema by reconciling two team-developed plans against
CORDE's existing database slices into a unified version;
survived two UI library re-evaluations (Native Base →
React Native Elements → Native Base) and chose Native Base
for its Figma-component-library integration so design and
implementation stayed close; ran the demo cadence in front
of Barry Clark and the CORDE technical team every week
across five months; and made the team's GitHub one-branch
policy work even though it required disciplined sequencing
of two developers' parallel work.

**Kept the codebase modern after the engagement ended.**
Continued making targeted commits in subsequent years for
React Native version currency, Android 16 KB page-size
linker compliance, husky + lint-staged pre-commit hooks,
and dependency upgrades — keeping the repository a usable
portfolio piece rather than an archived snapshot of
mid-2024 React Native conventions.

**The application remains in active use today by CORDE's
field crews** for offline data collection — GPS-tagged
photos and structured form data captured on water-
infrastructure and civil-construction sites where mobile
coverage drops out, then syncing back when crews return
to coverage. The codebase-currency commits aren't archive
maintenance; they're keeping a production tool aligned
with the platform conventions of the year the crews are
actually running it on.

**Technical highlights:** **React Native 0.83 + TypeScript + Native Base** on the
client, deliberately chosen over Expo because the project's
offline-first requirements pushed against Expo's managed
workflow (and over React Native Elements because Native
Base shipped a Figma component library that let the team
do design-first then implement against the exact
components). The native-only mode also opened the door to
Android-native development where the offline pipeline
needed it.

**SQLite as the system of record for the moment of work.**
Local-first storage of text + photo + GPS coordinates +
timestamps captured in the field, with a synchronisation
pipeline that reconciles back to CORDE's Workbench central
database when connectivity returns. The sync model had to
handle the real conflict cases offline-first systems
actually face — duplicate-key collisions when a worker's
local IDs and the server's IDs both move forward, partial
sync where one log uploads but its attachments don't,
connectivity drop mid-batch, and the file-upload
sub-protocol covered separately in Kullum's API specifications.

**Heavy native-integration surface:** geolocation +
geolocation-service (foreground and background), camera-
roll, image-picker, document-picker, file-system, secure
keychain storage, native maps, react-native-permissions
for the Android permission flow, base64 encoding for the
on-the-wire payloads. The dependency list is twice as long
as a typical RN app because field-data-collection in
Canterbury's rural water-infrastructure context genuinely
touches that much of the device.

**API integration discipline.** Apifox for shared interface
testing across the team; Swagger as the contract source
from CORDE; a `test-sharepoint-connection.js` script as
evidence of a SharePoint-touching corner of CORDE's
document workflow; Azure DevOps as the work-item store
Barry Clark and the CORDE team worked from; per-week
question sets compiled into documents and reviewed before
each offline meeting so CORDE's time was used efficiently.

**The documentation repo is the artefact.** A second
repository (`team-corde`) ran in parallel as the project's
living memory — every weekly meeting decision, every
UI-library evaluation, every database-schema iteration,
every CORDE email exchange's outcome, captured in
version-controlled prose. This is unusually disciplined
documentation for a student-team engagement and is the
practice I carried into every subsequent role.

2020 connecting women in STEM through one-on-one and group mentorship.
The organisation has **over 1,000 members** worldwide and launched the
"Her Voice" podcast to amplify women's voices in tech. Bi-weekly
mentorship meetings with assigned early-career mentees cover career
development, personal growth, and practical problem-solving in STEM
environments — coordinated through a dedicated volunteer liaison.
Holds 2024 and 2025 official mentor certificates.

orientation that later anchored Chan's work between China and NZ.

## Flagship Projects

### 1. GAVIGO IRE — Founding-engineer MVP for GAVIGO Inc. (Delaware C-Corp) — the 'Instant Reality Exchange' post-app activation platform with sub-millisecond container pre-warming (p50 <1 ms restore, 84.6% warm-pool hit), AI-orchestrated Kubernetes scaling, and a TikTok-style mobile feed; validated by Google for Startups + NVIDIA Inception (Feb 2026) after a DOKS→GKE production migration
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://ire.gavigo.com/
- **Tech stack:** Go, gorilla/websocket, Kubernetes, DigitalOcean DOKS (initial), Google Kubernetes Engine (post-migration), client-go (K8s API), Artifact Registry, GCE Ingress + Global HTTP(S) LB, React 18, Vite, React Native, Expo 54, WebSocket, Redis 7, PostgreSQL 16, Supabase, GPT-4o-mini
- **Total commits:** 360
- **Solo commits:** 353 (98%)
- **Restore path p50:** <1 ms
- **Cold path p50:** ~12 ms
- **Warm pool hit rate:** 84.6%
- **Cloud platforms:** DigitalOcean DOKS → Google Cloud GKE
- **Cross-cloud cutover:** ~30 min downtime (2026-03-11)
- **Games vendored (FOSS):** 9 (from 11 repos analyzed)
**Summary:** AI-orchestrated post-app activation platform for GAVIGO Inc. (Delaware
C-Corp; founder & CEO Saba Gecgil). Public mission: "Skip the Store.
Start the Play" — eliminate the latency between user intent and
interactive content delivery. Distributed system: Go orchestrator
(WebSocket hub + Scorer + 7-trigger rules engine + Kubernetes client),
React dashboard for live orchestration visualization, React Native /
Expo mobile app with TikTok-style feed. Originally architected and
shipped on **DigitalOcean managed Kubernetes (DOKS, Singapore)**, then
migrated to **Google Cloud GKE (Belgium)** in a single ~30-minute
production cutover (2026-03-11) when GAVIGO's Google Cloud partnership
came into effect — production expertise on both clouds. Real measured
performance post-migration: restore path <1 ms p50, cold path ~12 ms,
84.6% warm-pool hit rate. 360 total commits, 353 (98%) solo. As
Founding Principal Engineer, Activation, Execution & AI Systems, I
own the contractually-defined "Protected Technical Domain": activation
systems, activation flow and execution behavior, activation-economics
proof path, AI-assisted orchestration and execution-control,
restore/reuse boundaries, and heavier-workload validation.

**Impact:** Founding-engineer MVP for a Delaware startup — sub-millisecond interactive-content activation (p50 <1 ms, 84.6% warm-pool hit) on Kubernetes, production-validated by Google for Startups + NVIDIA Inception; 353/360 commits (98%) solo as Founding Principal Engineer.

**Problem:** The native-app paradigm puts a friction tax on every interactive
digital experience: discover an app → app-store landing page → install
→ onboard → first interaction. By the time the user reaches the thing
they actually wanted, the moment has passed. For games, video, and
AI experiences — where perception of instantaneity drives engagement
— the install gap is the single largest leak in the funnel. GAVIGO's
thesis (in its own marketing language): we are building the
"Post-App World," eliminating the trillion-dollar friction between
user intent and digital experience through proprietary "Instant
Reality Switching" technology.

**Audience:** Two surfaces in production today. **Consumer surface**: end users on
ire.gavigo.com and the mobile build, scrolling a TikTok-style
vertical feed of FOSS games (Hextris, Clumsy Bird, Tiny Yurts,
Catculus, Casual Crusade, Stolen Sword, and others — 9 vendored to
date from a curated set of 11 open-source repos analyzed). **Investor
/ operator surface**: the React dashboard at the same domain, which
shows the orchestrator's AI decisions, container state machine
(COLD → PREPARING → READY → ACTIVE → REUSABLE), warm-pool budget,
activation-quality metrics, and side-by-side iframe of the mobile
app — purpose-built for live demos to investors and partner
evaluators. A Phase 9 audience-segmentation feature filters
visibility per role (public / investor / operator presets) at
WebSocket broadcast time. The ultimate addressable surface is any
market where perception of instantaneity drives engagement — mobile
games, short-form video, AI chat, generative content.

**Product logic:** A user scrolls a TikTok-style vertical feed; the orchestrator
watches focus events stream over a WebSocket hub, scores each
candidate piece of content on three weighted signals (personal
attention 0.4, global attention 0.4, trend / virality 0.2), and
when combined score crosses a 0.6 threshold, fires one of seven AI
triggers — Swarm Boost, Proactive Warm, Cross-Domain Inject,
Mode Change, Lookahead Warm, Initial Warm, Checkpoint Restore —
each of which patches Kubernetes deployment replicas through the
K8s client-go API to scale a game container to READY before the
user taps.

When the user taps, the experience is already live — either served
instantly from a single always-warm shared game-server pod
(~12 ms cold path) or restored from the Reusable pool of recently-
active containers (<1 ms restore path). State survives across
scrolls via a postMessage protocol on web and MMKV storage on
native; both reconcile against Redis as a single source of truth
through a deterministic five-state Activation Spine
(COLD → READY → ACTIVE → REUSABLE → restore). When mobile gaming
meets the install gap, the GAVIGO answer is: there is no install
gap.

**Business logic:** GAVIGO Inc. is a Delaware-incorporated C-Corp at the pre-Series A
stage, founder Saba Gecgil. The product wedge is mobile gaming
(public tagline: "Skip the Store. Start the Play") — but the
underlying activation engine is platform-shaped: any interactive
content category where the install gap or cold-start latency is
the dominant funnel leak. The defensibility is architectural,
not feature-level: a proprietary Activation Spine plus a measured
sub-millisecond restore path is the kind of capability that
compounds, doesn't decay. GAVIGO frames its architecture publicly
as "Two Pillars": the Technology Platform (CTO-led foundation)
and the Intelligence Layer (the "soul" — the autonomous predictive
engine that makes Instant Reality possible). I own the
Intelligence Layer side of that architecture under the title
Founding Principal Engineer, Activation, Execution & AI Systems.

**Technical approach:** The orchestrator is a single Go binary (gorilla/websocket hub +
Scorer + Rules Engine + K8s client-go patcher) deployed to a
managed Kubernetes cluster. Companion pods: a React + Vite
dashboard, a React Native / Expo mobile-web build served from an
nginx pod, a single always-warm nginx game-server pod baking ~25
FOSS games (zero per-game startup latency), an AI-service pod
(Node + Express + GPT-4o-mini) that scales 0→1, Phase 6+ compute
/ inference workloads, Redis 7 (allkeys-LRU eviction, 400 MB
budget) for warm-pool state and activation metrics, and optional
PostgreSQL 16 for social data.

**Cross-cloud production experience: DigitalOcean and Google Cloud.**
The platform shipped first on **DigitalOcean Kubernetes (DOKS) in
Singapore (sgp1)** — DO Container Registry, DO LoadBalancer
(REGIONAL type), Let's Encrypt with manual certbot renewal — chosen
for cost-efficient prototype-to-production iteration. When GAVIGO's
Google Cloud partnership came into effect, I led the full migration
to **GKE Standard Zonal in Belgium (europe-west1-b)** on 2026-03-11.
The cutover rebuilt the entire infrastructure stack: DO Container
Registry → Artifact Registry in europe-west3 (cross-region pulls
within GCP); DO LoadBalancer → GCE Ingress + Global HTTP(S) LB
with Google-managed certs (auto-renewal replacing 80-day certbot
toil); DO LB redirect annotation → FrontendConfig
`redirectToHttps: true`; DO LB default WebSocket timeouts →
BackendConfig `timeoutSec: 86400` to keep activation WS sessions
alive; service types reshaped from LoadBalancer-per-frontend to
ClusterIP-behind-Ingress; static IP swapped to a GCP global static
IP. All five deployments (orchestrator, frontend, mobile-web,
ai-service, redis) migrated cleanly with `strategy: Recreate` to
avoid CPU deadlock on single-node clusters. Total production
downtime during cutover: **~30 minutes** (DNS propagation + cert
provisioning). The migration is fully documented in the project's
`docs/archive/gcp-migration-record.md` for institutional memory.

Hard problems solved. **Sub-millisecond restore** through a
deterministic five-state Activation Spine with atomic Redis
transitions and phase-by-phase timestamps that prove the
decision → K8s patch → client observation chain. **Warm-pool
LRU without cascade eviction** via ProtectSlot reservations that
keep controlled / benchmark games safe from displacement.
**Cross-platform state sync** between MMKV (native), localStorage
/ postMessage (web), and Redis (server) with a typed game
protocol bridging iframe ↔ WebView. **Async K8s coordination
with real-time telemetry** — pod EndpointSlice / Pod-status
watches feed activation_ready broadcasts with millisecond-
precision timestamps. **Audience-aware broadcast filtering**
(Phase 9) where the WebSocket hub filters event envelopes per
client preset (public / investor / operator) so each viewer sees
a different slice of the catalog. **Benchmark spine** running
20 iterations per path on real K8s, validating <1 ms restore p50
and ~12 ms cold p50 against load-tested 100 concurrent WebSocket
clients.

Engineering culture is phase-driven with comprehensive proof
packs: each major capability lands as a phase (Phase 1 init →
Phase 2 telemetry → Phase 3 shared game-server → Phase 4 service
worker / iframe pool → Phase 5 activation spine → Phase 6
compute workloads → Phase 7-8 proof pack → Phase 9 audience
presets), with documentation and benchmark numbers required to
promote work to public surfaces.

**Outcomes:** Production-deployed at ire.gavigo.com. First shipped on
DigitalOcean DOKS (Singapore), then migrated to Google Cloud GKE
(Belgium) on 2026-03-11 in a clean ~30-minute cutover when GAVIGO's
Google Cloud partnership came into effect — production-grade
expertise demonstrated on both clouds. 360 total commits, 353 solo
(98%, across both my git identities); remainder GitHub Actions bot.
Real measured platform metrics post-migration: restore path <1 ms
p50, cold path ~12 ms p50, 84.6% warm-pool hit rate, 0 errors at
100 concurrent WebSocket clients in load tests. Catalog: 9 vendored
FOSS games (curated from 11 open-source repos analyzed for license,
build size, and web-first compatibility) across 9 studios, 199
bundled videos. Phase 9 audience-segmentation live: investors and
operators see different catalogs than the public surface.
Pre-Series A; product is the working artifact for upcoming
investor conversations. Role formally formalized over three
contract iterations (Original Oct 17 2025; Amended Feb 9 2026;
Second Amended Apr 16 2026), each escalating recognition of my
technical ownership area.

**External validation cycle, Feb 2026:** GAVIGO was accepted
into the **Google for Startups Cloud Program** — announced by
founder Saba Gecgil with the message that the value isn't cloud
credits but the ability to "test [our] assumptions in depth and
prove that faster activation and higher concurrency don't
require more hardware, just better control over how compute is
used." That partnership is what unlocked the GCP migration I
executed on March 11. The same window brought acceptance into
the **NVIDIA Inception Program** — the deep-tech / GPU
infrastructure track that supports startups building GPU-
intensive infrastructure-level technologies. And the company
was profiled by tech-business press at feedvoice.com on
2026-02-10 in *"How GAVIGO Is Ending App Store Friction for
Instant Digital Play"* — the public account names the
sub-0.2-second activation claim, the patented execution-layer
IP positioning, the "Watch and Play era" narrative, and the
cross-border Istanbul-to-global ambition.

**My role:** Founding Principal Engineer, Activation, Execution & AI Systems
(prior: Core Engineer), reporting to founder & CEO Saba Gecgil.
Joined Oct 17 2025; current title and scope formalized by the
Second Amended and Restated Consulting Agreement effective
Apr 16 2026.

The contract defines a "Protected Technical Domain" that anchors
my role: activation systems, activation flow and execution
behavior, activation-economics proof path, AI-assisted
orchestration and execution-control systems, restore and reuse
boundary work, heavier-workload validation and transition work,
and the core activation, execution, and AI-enabled systems
architecture of GAVIGO's interactive workload platform — plus
the natural forward extension of that area as the system evolves.
Recognized in the contract record as "a core long-term technical
builder of the Company's interactive workload platform" with
founder-level equity participation. 98% solo commit authorship
on the active codebase.
### 2. Tam-AI-Ti (tam-ai-ti-web) — Independent research commission for enterprise executive Riria (Missy) Te Kanawa (ASB Bank · former KPMG NZ National Māori Sector lead) — an AI Financial Wellness Coach grounded in Te Whare Tapa Whā that proved Māori users will engage with a culturally-native AI agent (19-user cohort, 4 months, bilingual te reo Māori). 351 solo commits, 48-table schema where culture is typed data not UI decoration, voice-first GPT-4o Realtime coach, CopilotKit agent across 88+ actions.
[tier=flagship · recency=active · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://tamaiti.whiri-ai.com/
- **Demo:** https://youtu.be/NtT7_q6xhlk
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15.5 (App Router + Turbopack build + Edge Runtime AI endpoints), React 19.1 + TypeScript 5 (100% strict typing across 48 tables / 494 columns), Drizzle ORM 0.44 + drizzle-kit 0.31 (10 schema files: users · assessments · goals · daily-engagement · ai-coach · whanau · maramataka · whakatauki · financial-coach · index; 22 hand-written SQL migrations 0000-0021), Neon Postgres (HTTP serverless adapter `@neondatabase/serverless` 1.0), pgVector — 1536-dim `text-embedding-3-small` embeddings on 4 tables (goals · goal_progress · journal_entries · ai_coach_messages), CopilotKit 1.10 (backend + runtime + react-core/react-ui/react-textarea) — deliberately optimised from 8 AI-enabled routes down to 3 focused ones (Dashboard CRUD wellness + Journal fill + Journal History search), OpenAI 6 SDK — three-model composition: `gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18` for CopilotKit actions (temp 0.3, deterministic), `gpt-4o-2024-11-20` for journal enhance/translate/insights (temp 0.5-0.8), `gpt-4o-realtime-preview` ("verse" voice) for the Financial Coach with server-side VAD + 500ms silence detection, @openai/agents 0.1 (OpenAI Agents SDK), NextAuth v5 (beta 29) + @auth/drizzle-adapter 1.10 — Google OAuth 2.0, database sessions (30-day duration, not JWT), react-hook-form 7.62 + @hookform/resolvers 5 + Zod 3.25 validation across all API inputs, @vercel/blob 2.0 — avatar storage; ioredis 5.7 — Redis session cache; Resend 6 + @react-email/components 0.5 — transactional email, react-easy-crop 5.5 — avatar crop preview; recharts 3.2 — Hauora trend charts; @lottiefiles/dotlottie-react 0.17 — animated illustrations; date-fns 4 NZ-time-aware day boundaries, Radix UI primitives (17 packages: Dialog, AlertDialog, NavigationMenu, Popover, Tabs, RadioGroup, Slider, Switch, Tooltip, ScrollArea, Avatar, Checkbox, DropdownMenu, Label, Progress, Select, Separator), Tailwind CSS v4 + tw-animate-css 1.3 + Sonner 2 toasts + next-themes (light-only by deliberate design), Vercel hosting (auto-deploy, Edge Runtime, Blob storage, KV Redis) — migrated from developer's personal Hobby plan to client's Pro team in March 2026 with a zero-downtime cutover documented as a reusable playbook, Custom React Server Components CVE patches applied within hours of Vercel/Snyk PRs (PR #2, Dec 2025)
- **Research cohort:** 19 users (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026)
- **Journal entries:** 181 bilingual (EN + te reo Māori)
- **Voice sessions:** 35 (gpt-4o-realtime, text transcripts only — audio deliberately not stored)
- **AI coach messages:** 146
- **Hauora daily check-ins:** 74
- **Active financial goals:** 18
- **AI actions defined:** 88+ across 8 hooks (1,936 lines); CopilotKit live on 3 routes after optimisation
- **DB schema scale:** 48 tables · 494 columns · 10 schema modules · 22 migrations (0000-0021)
- **9 psychological factors:** self_belief · long_term_focus · resilience · self_control · growth_mindset · financial_confidence · social_comparison · mindfulness · gratitude (one is reverse-coded as Present Bias)
- **5 Hauora dimensions:** Wairua (spiritual, 3 metrics) · Tinana (physical, 4 metrics) · Whānau (family, 2 metrics) · Hinengaro (mental, 2 metrics) · Whenua (environmental, integrated in Wairua)
- **4 Maramataka activities:** Toitoi (spiritual/cultural) · Hi ika (water harvesting) · Pouraka (hunting/gathering) · Mahi māra (gardening/planting) — across 30-day lunar cycle + 8 main moon phases
- **7 SMART action plans:** Option A Spending Reduction · B Savings Increase · C Savings Rate · D Debt Reduction · E Income Growth · F Budget Review · G Accountability Partner (each with dedicated React component family — 38 goal components total)
- **5 Whānau permission tiers:** canSeeGoals · canSeeProgress · canSeeMood · canSeeAchievements · canSendMessages
- **Whakatauki library:** 74 Māori proverbs across 37+ categories, AI-driven contextual delivery, bilingual
- **Pages · API routes · components:** 38 pages · 76 API route files · 100+ endpoints across 20 modules · 115+ components (44 main · 38 goal-specific · 28 shadcn/ui · 5+ cultural)
- **Commits (solo):** 351 (2025-09-19 → 2026-03-29)
- **Production migration:** Vercel personal Hobby → client Whiri-AI Pro team executed March 2026 with zero downtime; documented as `docs/implementation/PROJECT_MIGRATION_GUIDE.md` — a reusable playbook covering GH org transfer, Blob/KV recreation, OAuth re-config, DNS cutover, blob-data migration, and `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` preservation for session survival
**Summary:** AI Financial Wellness Coach for Māori communities, architected on the Te Whare
Tapa Whā holistic-health framework — culture is in the schema, not the UI.
Built solo for Riria (Missy) Te Kanawa (former KPMG NZ National Māori Sector
lead, now at ASB Bank) as an independent research-pilot commission, introduced
via Dr Mahsa McCauley, founder of She Sharp. Voice coaching via GPT-4o
Realtime API, bilingual te reo Māori + English, 88+ AI actions, pgVector
semantic search across 48 database tables. 19-user research cohort produced
181 journal entries, 35 voice sessions, and 146 AI coach messages over four
months (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026).

**Impact:** ASB Bank executive research pilot — NZ's first Māori-cultural AI financial-wellness agent (Te Whare Tapa Whā in schema, not UI); 19 users over 4 months with bilingual te reo Māori voice coaching, proving culturally-native AI engages communities mainstream FinTech underserves.

**Problem:** Mainstream FinTech treats money as decontextualized numbers — budgets,
balances, projections. For Māori users, that framing is culturally
mismatched: money is inseparable from whānau wellbeing, spiritual purpose,
and holistic health. Generic budgeting apps strip out the very framework
that makes financial decisions meaningful, and Māori communities — who
face disproportionate financial stress in Aotearoa — are left choosing
between tools that work for their wallets but not their worldview, or no
tools at all. Tam-AI-Ti exists to test whether an AI financial coach built
natively on Māori cultural wisdom — rather than retrofitted with cultural
decoration — can engage a population that mainstream FinTech systematically
underserves.

**Audience:** Māori adults engaging with personal financial wellbeing, served along two
account types: individual goal-setters (Full Account) and whānau supporters
who can witness and encourage from the sidelines through a five-tier
permission system (view profile, view goals, view journal, send messages,
receive celebrations). Pilot cohort to date: 19 verified research
participants recruited by Missy Te Kanawa, active over a four-month window
(Oct 2025 – Mar 2026), producing 181 bilingual journal entries, 35 voice
coaching sessions, 146 AI coach messages, and 74 daily Hauora check-ins.
Te reo Māori and English are first-class throughout the experience.
Future audience: the broader Māori adult population — rangatahi entering
working life, parents managing whānau finances, kaumātua passing on
financial wisdom — for whom culturally-rooted tools are scarce.

**Product logic:** The product is architected on Te Whare Tapa Whā, the Māori model of
holistic wellness with four walls (Wairua/spiritual, Tinana/physical,
Hinengaro/mental, Whānau/family) standing on a foundation (Whenua/land).
Every feature traces back to this house.

A user starts with the **26Q assessment over nine named psychological
factors** — `self_belief`, `long_term_focus`, `resilience`,
`self_control`, `growth_mindset`, `financial_confidence`,
`social_comparison`, `mindfulness`, `gratitude` (with `mindfulness`
reverse-coded to capture Present Bias) — mapped to personalised
recommendations. They then select up to three focus areas, choose one
of three account types (Full / Supporter / Both), and proceed to set
financial goals through one of **seven SMART action plans (Options
A-G)**: A Spending Reduction, B Savings Increase, C Savings Rate, D
Debt Reduction, E Income Growth, F Budget Review, G Accountability
Partner. Each plan has its own dedicated React component family
(SpendingTracker / SavingsTracker / DebtCalculator / IncomeTracker /
BudgetReviewCalendar / MeetingsTracker etc. — 38 goal components
total) and its own type-specific tracking fields in the schema,
rather than a generic JSON-note column. A `GoalCardFactory` /
`ProgressTrackerFactory` / `OverviewFactory` pattern dispatches the
right component for each goal's `option` field.

They check in daily across **eleven Hauora dimensions** mapped directly
to the four walls: `sleep_hours`, `water_litres`, `food_quality`,
`exercise_hours` for Tinana; `wairua_time_spent`,
`wairua_human_connection`, `wairua_taia_connection` for Wairua (Whenua
integrated through Taiao/environment connection); `hinengaro_mauri_tau`
(mindfulness), `hinengaro_ako` (learning) for Hinengaro;
`whanau_check_in`, `whanau_quality_time` for Whānau. Each metric is a
typed integer-scale column with a Māori-language identifier — not a
UI label sitting on top of a culturally-neutral schema. They journal
morning intentions (Hauora intentions across all 5 dimensions + 3
gratitude items + financial reflections) and evening reflections
through the Kūmete framework (what you contributed + what you received,
Tahi-Rua-Toru tomorrow goals, emotional check-in), all bilingually in
te reo or English, with real GPT-4o enhancement (4 enhancement types ×
3 intensity levels), translation (macron-correct ā/ē/ī/ō/ū), and
5-dimensional AI insight extraction (Emotions / Themes / Hauora /
Patterns / Growth).

They meet an **AI Financial Coach via gpt-4o-realtime-preview** voice
("verse" voice, server-side VAD + 500ms silence detection) — a
conversation partner that never reduces money to numbers but
consistently frames every financial decision through all four walls
of the Hauora model. Audio is deliberately *not* stored; only text
transcripts persist (a cost optimisation that preserves searchability
without per-minute audio storage). The AI surfaces **74 Whakatauki
proverbs across 37+ categories** by theme via `/api/whakatauki/search`,
references the **Maramataka 30-day lunar calendar with 4 traditional
activity types** — *Toitoi* (spiritual/cultural: karakia, whakapapa,
wānanga), *Hi ika* (water-based: ocean/river fishing, shellfish,
seaweed), *Pouraka* (hunting: bird hunting, forest foraging, game
tracking), *Mahi māra* (gardening/planting) — for cultural timing,
and ties financial decisions back to family and spiritual purpose.
Whānau members can be invited via email-token-based invitations with
a **5-tier permission system** (`canSeeGoals` · `canSeeProgress` ·
`canSeeMood` · `canSeeAchievements` · `canSendMessages`) to encourage
and witness without intruding.

**Business logic:** Currently a research-pilot product, not yet commercial. Funded directly
by the researcher as an independent contracted build. The theory of
change: if a financial wellness tool authentically reflects Māori
cultural frameworks — rather than skinning generic FinTech with cultural
decoration — it can drive engagement and outcomes for a population that
mainstream products systematically miss.

The hypothesis sits inside Riria (Missy) Te Kanawa's broader body of work
on Māori economic prosperity. She is lead author of KPMG New Zealand's
Maui Rau report series on how Māori can advance prosperity for their
people, has spoken at the National Māori Governance Summit, contributes
to The Spinoff, and brings two decades of experience as a Chartered
Accountant and management consultant. Former Partner at KPMG NZ leading
the National Māori Sector; now at ASB Bank. Tam-AI-Ti is the working
artifact for that thesis — a product proving culturally-native technology
can serve where culturally-decorated technology cannot.

Commercialization paths remain open: iwi partnerships, Māori community
organizations, financial institutions seeking to authentically serve the
Māori sector.

**Technical approach:** Solo full-stack build on Next.js 15.5 with Turbopack and TypeScript 5
(100% strict typing across the entire 48-table / 494-column schema).
Drizzle ORM 0.44 over Neon serverless Postgres with pgVector for
1536-dimension `text-embedding-3-small` semantic search across four
embedded tables (`goals.goal_embedding`,
`goal_progress.note_embedding`, `journal_entries.content_embedding`,
`ai_coach_messages.content_embedding`). Authentication via NextAuth
v5 (beta 29) with `@auth/drizzle-adapter` 1.10 — Google OAuth 2.0,
database sessions (30-day duration, not JWT), CSRF protection,
role-based access control across three account types (full / supporter
/ both).

**The schema is deliberately split across 10 module files** —
`users.ts`, `assessments.ts`, `goals.ts`, `daily-engagement.ts`,
`ai-coach.ts`, `whanau.ts`, `maramataka.ts`, `whakatauki.ts`,
`financial-coach.ts`, and `index.ts` — totalling ~924 lines, each
file owning a domain boundary. **22 hand-written SQL migrations
(0000-0021)** track every schema change: 0015 restructured the
Hauora metrics from mixed data types to consistent integer scales
(better alignment with Māori wellness philosophy: removed
`exercise_minutes` / `calm_minutes` / `outside_minutes` and added 7
new Hauora-aligned integer fields); 0020 added the financial coach
tables (`financial_coach_sessions` 8 cols + `financial_coach_messages`
5 cols); 0021 added period-based tracking (`current_period_start`,
`current_period_end`) so Options F (Budget Review) and G
(Accountability Partner) operate as recurring rather than one-shot
goals.

The AI layer is a deliberate **three-model composition** chosen for
cost-and-quality fit:
- **CopilotKit 1.10** drives in-app tool actions on
  `gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18` at temperature 0.3 — deterministic and
  cheap for tool calls (88+ AI actions defined across 8 hooks /
  1,936 lines).
- **`gpt-4o-2024-11-20`** handles high-quality journal enhancement
  (`enhanceJournalContent`: 4 enhancement types × 3 intensity levels
  at temp 0.6-0.8, max 1500-3000 tokens), bilingual translation
  (`translateJournalContent`: EN ↔ te reo Māori with macron-correct
  ā/ē/ī/ō/ū preservation at temp 0.5, max 2000 tokens), AI insight
  extraction (`extractJournalInsights`: 5-dimensional analysis at
  temp 0.3, structured JSON), reflection-question generation
  (`generateReflectionQuestions`: 5 question types at temp 0.7), and
  semantic similarity search (`findSimilarEntriesAI`).
- **OpenAI Realtime API** (`gpt-4o-realtime-preview`, "verse" voice)
  powers the voice Financial Coach with server-side VAD and 500ms
  silence detection. Voice sessions store text transcripts only — a
  cost optimisation that preserves searchability without per-minute
  audio storage.

**CopilotKit was deliberately optimised from 8 AI-enabled routes
down to 3** (Dashboard with 4 CRUD wellness actions; Journal with
the fill-journal action; Journal History with search + analysis).
Goals / Maramataka / Resources / Onboarding / Auth routes were
intentionally *disabled* for the AI assistant — security on auth,
performance on cultural pages, and cleaner UX on goal management.
That's anti-feature-bloat discipline: removing AI surface area was
treated as a feature.

**Culture is in the schema, not the UI.** Every one of the eleven
Hauora dimensions is a typed column with a Māori-language identifier
(`wairuaTaiaConnection`, `hinengaroMauriTau`, `whanauCheckIn`,
`wairuaTimeSpent`, `whanauQualityTime`, etc.); the seven SMART action
plans are first-class types `a`–`g` with **15 type-specific tracking
fields** in `goal_progress` rather than a generic JSON blob; the 74
Whakatauki proverbs are an indexed, embedded library searchable by
theme through `/api/whakatauki/search?q=...&category=...`. The
Maramataka schema (6 tables, 49+ columns) models the 30-day lunar
cycle, 8 main moon phases, the 4-activity key system, daily energy
levels, and Gregorian-to-Maramataka calendar mapping. **48 tables,
494 columns, 76 API route files, 100+ endpoints across 20 modules,
38 pages, 115+ React components** (44 main + 38 goal-specific + 28
shadcn/ui + 5 cultural). Edge-runtime endpoints on Vercel; Vercel
Blob for avatar storage with `react-easy-crop` 5.5 client-side crop
preview; Redis (ioredis 5.7) for session cache; Resend 6 +
`@react-email/components` for transactional email; `recharts` 3.2
for Hauora trend charts.

**Outcomes:** Production-deployed at **tamaiti.whiri-ai.com**, in active operation
since October 2025. **Research cohort engagement (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026,
approximately four months)**: 19 verified users, 181 bilingual journal
entries, 18 active financial goals, 146 AI coach messages, 35 voice
coaching sessions, 74 daily Hauora check-ins. Sustained multi-month
engagement on a pre-commercial product with no marketing channel — a
meaningful early signal for a research-pilot product.

**March 2026 production migration**: executed a zero-downtime cutover
from my personal Vercel Hobby account to the client's Whiri-AI Pro
team account, including GitHub repo transfer, Vercel Blob + KV
re-creation, Google OAuth re-config, custom-domain DNS cutover
through Cloudflare, blob-data migration, Resend sender-domain
re-verification, and `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` preservation so existing user
sessions survived the move. The whole migration was documented as a
reusable artifact at `docs/implementation/PROJECT_MIGRATION_GUIDE.md`
— a 12-step playbook covering every component, the
what-transfers-and-what-doesn't matrix, and the rationale for *not*
using Vercel's built-in Project Transfer feature.

**22 schema migrations shipped** in lock-step with feature work, none
rolled back; the most consequential being 0015 (Hauora-metrics
restructure from mixed types to consistent integer scales), 0020
(Financial Coach voice tables) and 0021 (period-based tracking for
Options F/G converting them from one-shot to recurring goals). React
Server Components CVE patches landed within hours of Vercel/Snyk
automated PRs (PR #2, Dec 2025).

Project status: fully developed, deployed, in active operating phase,
and transferred to client operational ownership. Sits within Missy
Te Kanawa's wider body of published work on Māori economic prosperity
— KPMG NZ **Maui Rau** report series (lead author), Māori Governance
Summit talks, The Spinoff contributing author — and is the working
artifact for the thesis that culturally-native technology can serve
Māori communities where culturally-decorated technology cannot.

**My role:** Solo independent developer with full ownership of the entire stack and
lifecycle. Introduced to the project by Dr Mahsa McCauley (Mohaghegh),
founder of She Sharp (New Zealand's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit) —
Missy approached Mahsa for a developer recommendation and Mahsa referred
me from the She Sharp engineering team, where I serve as Senior Full
Stack Engineer and Website Team Lead.

Engagement model: Missy as client, directing the product structurally
(feature requests, surveys, "include the Maramataka lunar calendar,"
"add a Financial Coach feature") and funding the work directly; I
designed, built, deployed, and maintained the entire system, and ran
the data analysis. Throughout development I sent feature-demo videos at
every milestone so Missy could review progress without needing a
technical background. Post-launch, I produced teaching videos enabling
Missy to use Cursor to do her own database reads and basic data
analysis — transferring practical operational ownership while remaining
the technical lead. **351 commits, solo authorship throughout**
(2025-09-19 → 2026-03-29), including the March 2026 zero-downtime
production migration from my personal Vercel to the client's
Whiri-AI Pro team that I designed, executed, and documented as a
reusable `PROJECT_MIGRATION_GUIDE.md` playbook for future client
engagements.
### 3. She Sharp — Solo-built production platform that digitized, automated, and AI-upgraded NZ's
leading women-in-STEM nonprofit (charitable trust CC57025, 2,200+ members,
8,000+ women supported) — replacing Webflow + spreadsheets with a real member
engine. GPT-4o-mini AI mentor matching (5-weight scoring + admin review),
three role-permissioned dashboards (mentor / mentee / admin), Stripe billing,
and a custom Slack ecosystem (event-collector bot, weekly mentorship-stats
digest, NZ government funding crawler, Claude `sync-event-from-slack` skill)
that turned hours of volunteer manual work into one-command automation.
648/748 commits (86.6%) solo across 10 months.

[tier=flagship · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://she-sharp-zeta.vercel.app/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/NZ-SheSharp/she-sharp
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15.6 canary (App Router, Turbopack dev), React 19.1.2, TypeScript 5.8, Drizzle ORM 0.43, Neon Postgres (serverless pool), NextAuth 5 beta (Google + GitHub OAuth + bcrypt credentials), @auth/drizzle-adapter, OpenAI GPT-4o-mini (temp 0.3, max_tokens 1000, JSON-object response_format), Upstash Redis (AI match cache + queue), Stripe 20 (Customer Portal for self-service membership management), @slack/web-api (event-collector + interactive handlers), @octokit/rest (Slack-skill auto-commits), Claude agent skill: sync-event-from-slack, Resend (transactional email), Cloudinary (image hosting + transformations), Tailwind CSS 4.1, shadcn/ui + Radix UI (20+ primitives), @dnd-kit (kanban-style admin workflows), Recharts 3 + @number-flow/react (dashboard analytics), Framer Motion + Lenis (landing-page polish), Three.js + OGL (hero shader effects), Zod 3 (validation everywhere — Slack-bot LLM output gating, form schemas), React Hook Form + @hookform/resolvers, Vercel Edge Network + Vercel Cron
- **Total commits:** 748
- **Solo commits:** 648 (86.6%) — Lesley Gao authored ~100 frontend commits (13.4%)
- **Commits since 2026-01-01:** 345 (intensive H1 build-out)
- **Build window:** 2025-07-22 → 2026-05-11 (~10 months active)
- **API routes:** 95 (app/api/**/route.ts)
- **Pages:** 53 (app/**/page.tsx)
- **Components:** 145 (components/**/*.tsx)
- **Database schema:** 1,392 lines, 30 pgEnums, 34 pgTables
- **SQL migrations:** 27 (0000_soft_the_anarchist → 0026_brief_invisible_woman)
- **Lib modules:** matching, slack-bot, stripe, funding, programmes, recruitment, invitations, mentorship, email, auth, db (11 namespaced)
- **Cron jobs:** 3 (process-queue, weekly-funding-crawl, weekly-mentorship-stats)
- **Funding crawler sources:** 6 NZ government feeds (beehive RSS, data.govt CKAN, GETS RSS, MBIE HTML, Treasury RSS, women.govt HTML)
- **AI matching weights:** MBTI 15% + Skill 35% + Goal 30% + Industry 10% + Logistics 10%
- **AI matching score range:** 0-100 overall + 5 per-dimension subscores + confidence (high/medium/low)
- **AI retry policy:** 3 attempts with exponential backoff (1s × 2^n), batch concurrency 5, 100ms inter-batch delay
- **Members:** 2,200+ registered / 1,000+ active
- **Women supported:** 8,000+ lifetime
- **Corporate partners:** 50+
- **Events since 2014:** 84+
- **Industry mentors:** 18
- **Mentorship success rate:** 85% (5-6× higher promotion likelihood for mentees per She Sharp's outcomes data)
- **Migration crawler assets:** 1,748 images / 707 MB / 11 JSON files / 85 events + 50 Humanitix tickets + 15 podcasts + 121 photo galleries + 7 press articles + 118 pages
**Summary:** Solo-built production member platform for She Sharp — New Zealand's
leading women-in-STEM nonprofit (charitable trust, 2,200+ registered
members / 8,000+ women supported lifetime, 50+ corporate partners, 84+
events since 2014). Replaces the previous Webflow site. 648 of 748
commits (86.6%) authored solo. Headline features: GPT-4o-mini-powered
AI mentor-mentee matching with five-dimensional scoring + human review,
role-aware mentor / mentee / admin dashboards with dual-role support,
custom Slack ecosystem (event-collector bot, volunteer webhooks, weekly
stats digest, NZ funding-opportunities crawler), a Claude agent skill
`sync-event-from-slack` that automates event page publication, Stripe
tier billing, and full admin + audit systems. Plus three supporting
projects: a Python crawler that migrated 1,748 images / 707 MB / 85
events from the old Webflow site, the Her Waka MSD-funded teaching
site (Mintlify, curriculum authored solo), and a professional SEO
audit on shesharp.org.nz with a 10-point recommendations report.

**Impact:** Digitized and AI-upgraded NZ's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit (2,200+ members, 8,000+ supported) — 86.6% solo-built platform with AI mentor matching, three role dashboards, and a Slack automation stack that replaced hours of volunteer manual work.

**Problem:** She Sharp is New Zealand's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit — a
registered charitable trust founded by Dr Mahsa Mohaghegh (McCauley),
AI/NLP researcher and Director of Women in Tech at AUT, after she
experienced first-hand the gap of being part of the 20% minority of
women in technology. The community has grown to 2,200+ registered
members, 8,000+ women supported across its history, 50+ corporate
partners, and 84+ events since 2014. The original website was a
Webflow build, and Webflow had become the bottleneck: the platform
capabilities the mission required — membership tiers, mentor / mentee
/ admin role permissions, AI-driven mentor matching, Stripe billing,
Slack-integrated operations, automated event management, recruitment
pipelines, audit logging — are precisely what no SaaS site builder
can express. The leadership team was running a 2,200-member community
through spreadsheets, Slack threads, and manual outreach. She Sharp
needed a real platform.

**Audience:** Active members of the She Sharp community: women in technology at
every career stage, from undergraduate students considering STEM
majors through senior engineers serving as mentors. The platform
supports three role-permissioned dashboards (mentor, mentee, admin)
plus dual-role compatibility — a mid-career engineer can
simultaneously mentor juniors and be mentored by a senior, without
special-cased data modelling. Additional audiences served on adjacent
surfaces: Her Waka programme participants (women re-entering the
workforce, served by the dedicated MSD-funded teaching site at
herwaka.shesharp.org.nz), volunteer and ambassador candidates
(screened through the AI recruitment pipeline), the 50+ corporate
partners (sponsor packages tied to event visibility and member
access), and She Sharp's operational team (Slack-integrated
workflows). 1,000+ members are actively engaged today, drawn from a
registered base of 2,200+ and a lifetime supported population of
8,000+ women.

**Product logic:** The new platform replaces the old Webflow site with a real member
system anchored on three coexisting dashboards. A user signs up via
email/password (bcrypt) or OAuth (Google + GitHub), then independently
activates any combination of mentor, mentee, or admin roles — duality
is the **schema-level design principle**, not a special case. The
`user_roles` table has one row per (user, role_type) tuple with its
own `is_active`, `verified_at`, and `activation_step` columns, so a
mid-career engineer can simultaneously mentor juniors and be mentored
by a senior with zero special-cased data modelling. The dashboard
layer (`(dashboard)/dashboard/{mentor-profile,mentee-profile,
mentorship,meetings,admin,account}`) reads the active-roles set and
composes role-specific cards into a single unified view.

**The headline feature is the AI mentor-mentee matching engine**,
implemented as a two-stage pipeline in `lib/matching/`. Stage one is
a rule-based pre-filter that rapidly scores candidate pairs on basic
compatibility (skill set intersection, industry overlap, location/
meeting-format compatibility). Stage two passes promising pairs to
GPT-4o-mini at `temperature: 0.3`, `max_tokens: 1000`, with
`response_format: { type: 'json_object' }`, against an explicit
scoring rubric defined in `lib/matching/prompts.ts`:

  - **MBTI Compatibility — 15%** (cognitive function complementarity,
    communication style)
  - **Skill Alignment — 35%** (mentor's expert skills vs mentee's
    learning goals, expertise depth vs learning breadth)
  - **Goal Alignment — 30%** (career trajectory compatibility, mentor's
    ability to guide toward stated goals, shared values)
  - **Industry Match — 10%** (direct + adjacent + transferable industry
    knowledge)
  - **Logistics — 10%** (meeting format, location proximity, time
    availability)

Each call returns a 0-100 overall + per-dimension subscores +
natural-language explanation + recommendation + confidence (high /
medium / low) + matchingFactors (strengths / challenges /
growthOpportunities), Zod-validated against `AIMatchResponseSchema`
before persistence. **Three production-hardness layers wrap the
OpenAI call**: (1) **Upstash Redis cache** keyed by mentor:mentee
pair so repeat scoring is free; (2) **exponential-backoff retries**
(3 attempts, 1000ms × 2^attempt, with rate-limit/timeout
classification); (3) a **deterministic rule-based fallback** with
the same weighted formula that activates when OpenAI is unavailable,
explicitly marking `confidenceLevel: 'low'` and
`recommendation: 'manual review'` so admins know to look closer.
Batch processing caps at 5 concurrent calls with 100ms inter-batch
delay to respect API limits.

Matches above threshold surface to admin (`/api/admin/matching`)
for human approval before pairing — conservative human-in-the-loop
automation, not speculative auto-pairing. A **mentee matching queue**
(`lib/matching/queue-service.ts` + Vercel Cron at
`/api/cron/process-queue`) holds unmatched mentees in priority order
when mentor capacity is full and re-evaluates as slots open.
Meetings are typed (intro / regular / milestone / final), tracked
with action items (JSONB), notes, feedback, and 1-5 ratings.

**The Stripe-integrated membership flow has a novel activation
architecture**: it doesn't instantly grant access. The architecture
is **form → checkout → invitation code → activation**. A prospective
mentee fills the public mentee form (`/api/forms/mentee/public`),
which creates a `mentee_form_submissions` row. They get redirected
to Stripe Checkout. On `checkout.session.completed` webhook, the
system creates a `membership_purchases` record AND generates a
unique `invitation_code` (`createPaymentInvitationCode`) which is
emailed via Resend. The user must redeem the code to activate their
account, guaranteeing email ↔ payment ↔ account linkage and
preventing orphaned accounts. Self-service via Stripe Customer
Portal. Webhook signature verification on every event. Membership
lifecycle transitions update `user_memberships` + `activity_logs`
atomically.

The platform also runs She Sharp's events infrastructure (workshops,
training, networking, social, THRIVE programmes — online, in-person,
or hybrid, with capacity, waitlist, and QR check-in via `/api/qr`),
three membership tiers, a tiered resource library
with download access control + access logs, transactional email via
Resend, AI-screened volunteer recruitment (`lib/recruitment/
ai-screening.ts` against `/api/admin/recruitment/[id]/ai-screen`),
sponsor inquiries with inline forms, and comprehensive audit logging
(JSONB diffs via `deep-diff` on every admin action).

**A custom Slack ecosystem is deeply integrated** through three
coordinated surfaces. (1) **Read-only event-collector bot** with
scoped tokens (`channels:history`, `channels:read`, `groups:history`,
`groups:read`, `files:read`, `users:read`, `pins:read`,
`bookmarks:read`) — no destructive scopes by design. (2) **Three
Vercel Cron jobs**: `weekly-mentorship-stats` posts engagement
digest to `#mentorships` (recently hardened to survive cold Neon
connection pools), `weekly-funding-crawl` runs the NZ government
funding crawler over **6 official sources** (Beehive RSS, data.govt
CKAN, GETS RSS, MBIE HTML, Treasury RSS, women.govt HTML) with
dedup + relevance scoring + keyword filtering, posting a curated
Slack digest, and `process-queue` re-evaluates the matching queue.
(3) **Interactive Slack event handlers** at `/api/slack/events` and
`/api/slack/events/interactive` for in-Slack workflows.

**The most distinctive Slack-side piece** is a Claude agent skill
named `sync-event-from-slack` living at
`.claude/skills/sync-event-from-slack/`. It automates the previously-
manual work of turning a Slack event channel into a published event
page: it reads channel messages, thread replies, pinned items,
bookmarks, speaker `.docx` bios (via `mammoth`), cover posters, and
speaker headshots; downloads images and renames them per convention;
composes a structured `EventV3Schema` JSON entry against a strict
Zod schema; runs `verify-image-paths` as a CI gate; and on approval
commits to git or opens a PR. **The skill defaults to dry-run** —
no file writes, no git, no network side-effects beyond reading
Slack — unless the user explicitly says "go ahead and apply". Event
publication has gone from hours of manual editing to a single
command, with the AI bot's structured output Zod-validated as the
last line of defense before any git write.

**Business logic:** She Sharp is a registered New Zealand charitable trust. Three
operational channels now run through the platform at scale: three
membership tiers (free / basic / premium) with Stripe-integrated
flows; 50+ corporate-partner relationships (sponsor packages tied
to event visibility and member-base access); and grant-funded
programmes — notably Her Waka, an NZ Ministry of Social Development-
funded four-session employment-readiness programme for women
re-entering the workforce, run at academyEX Auckland (March–June
2026), with its own dedicated teaching site I built and authored.

The platform is the operational substrate that lets each channel
scale without proportional staff load: dashboards, matching,
membership lifecycle, communications, recruitment, and event
management are automated. The organization's mission scaling (from
the 1,500 active members publicly cited a year ago, toward the
2,200+ registered base today and beyond) depends directly on this
technical capability.

**Technical approach:** Next.js 15.6 canary (App Router, Turbopack dev) + React 19.1.2 +
TypeScript 5.8 over Drizzle ORM 0.43 on Neon Postgres (serverless
connection pool), authenticated via NextAuth 5 beta with bcrypt
credentials and Google + GitHub OAuth (via `@auth/drizzle-adapter`),
deployed on Vercel's edge network with documented sub-second TTFB.
Tailwind CSS 4.1 + shadcn/ui + Radix UI (20+ primitives) for the UI;
Framer Motion + Lenis + Three.js/OGL for landing-page polish;
@dnd-kit for admin kanban workflows; Recharts 3 + @number-flow/react
for dashboard analytics.

**The schema runs to 1,392 lines, 30 pgEnums, and 34 pgTables** —
a deliberate type-safety investment. Membership tiers (free / basic /
premium), mentorship-relationship states (pending / active / paused /
completed / rejected), meeting types (intro / regular / milestone /
final), event types (workshop / networking / training / social /
thrive), location types (online / in_person / hybrid), career stages
(undergraduate → postgraduate → early_career → mid_career → senior →
career_transition), MBTI 16 personality types, recruitment stages,
invitation-code redemption states, activity-log action types — every
business concept is a typed enum rather than a string column, so
invalid states are unrepresentable at the database level. **27 SQL
migrations** (0000_soft_the_anarchist → 0026_brief_invisible_woman)
applied via a custom safe-migration wrapper with pre-deployment
snapshots and rollback SQL generation.

**95 API routes** across `app/api/**/route.ts` covering auth (11
routes including OAuth callbacks, password reset, email
verification), admin (21 routes for matching/mentee-mentor reviews/
recruitment/programmes/users/permissions/analytics/zombie-cleanup),
mentorship lifecycle (apply / approve / relationships / meetings),
events (browse / register / my-registrations / QR check-in), forms
(mentee / mentor / volunteer with public-vs-authenticated variants),
Stripe (checkout / donate / webhook), Slack (events / interactive),
cron (process-queue / weekly-funding-crawl / weekly-mentorship-stats),
resources (download with access control), notifications, file
uploads (CV + profile photo via Cloudinary). **53 pages, 145
components.**

**The AI matching engine** combines a deterministic rule-based
pre-filter with GPT-4o-mini for high-potential pairs only. The
`lib/matching/` namespace has clear separation: `prompts.ts` holds
the system rubric and per-pair prompt builder, `openai-service.ts`
owns the LLM call with retry/cache/fallback, `cache.ts` interfaces
Upstash Redis, `queue-service.ts` manages the FIFO/priority queue
for over-capacity scenarios, `service.ts` orchestrates the full
match flow, and `types.ts` defines `AIMatchResponseSchema` (Zod)
that every LLM output must satisfy. Cost stays predictable because
Stage 1 filters out incompatible pairs before they reach the LLM;
outputs stay explainable because the rubric is explicit;
availability stays high because the rule-based fallback uses the
same weighted formula. Admin override is the final gate.

**Slack integration** uses **scoped read-only tokens** by design
(`channels:history`, `channels:read`, `groups:history`,
`groups:read`, `files:read`, `users:read`, `pins:read`,
`bookmarks:read` — never `chat:write` from the collector). The
`sync-event-from-slack` agent skill composes existing CLI tools
(Slack fetch → image download with rename → docx parse via
`mammoth` → JSON validate against `EventV3Schema` Zod → git commit
via `@octokit/rest` or local) into a single intent-driven workflow.
**Strict Zod validation is the last line of defense before any
git write** — an invalid LLM patch is rejected with a Slack-
friendly error, never persisted. The `weekly-funding-crawl` cron
runs `lib/funding/{fetcher,dedup,scoring,keywords}.ts` over 6
government RSS/HTML sources, scores by relevance keywords, dedups
across sources, and posts a curated Slack digest. The recent
`weekly-mentorship-stats` hardening (`84f788f`) is a small but
revealing detail: Neon's serverless pool can return stale
connections to a cold Vercel Cron worker, so the cron now retries
once on the specific `ECONNRESET`/`Connection terminated` error
class before failing the run.

**Image storage on Cloudinary** with on-the-fly transformations;
**transactional email via Resend** with role-specific templates
(mentorship-emails.ts + recruitment-emails.ts + membership
confirmation); **payments via Stripe** with Customer Portal for
self-service membership management; **audit logging** uses
`deep-diff` to capture JSONB before/after on every admin mutation.

The role spans beyond the main platform. Three supporting projects
round out the contribution. **she-sharp-crawl** — a Python crawler
(BeautifulSoup4, Requests) that migrated content from the old
Webflow site: 1,748 images totaling 707 MB across 11 modular JSON
files, covering 85 SheSharp events, 50 Humanitix ticketed events
(with banner images upgraded 500 px → 1,600 px), 15 podcasts, 121
photo galleries, 7 news / press articles, and 118 site pages. Image
deduplication and Wayback Machine fallback for dead links.
**her-waka** — the Mintlify-powered teaching site at
herwaka.shesharp.org.nz for the MSD-funded Her Waka programme; I
authored both the platform and the curriculum (four workshops
covering AI & Future of Work, #IAmRemarkable + Vibe Coding,
Cybersecurity, Personal Branding, plus 8+ self-paced tutorials at
three difficulty tiers). **serpdata-api-test** — a professional SEO
audit on shesharp.org.nz built on Node 22 native fetch over the
SerpData API, running ~20 live Google queries across eight analysis
sections (brand visibility, topical keyword rankings, NZ competitor
matrix, mobile-vs-desktop gap, cross-market reach, SERP feature
inventory) plus a 10-point prioritized recommendations report
(e.g., "SheSharp ranks #4 desktop / not in mobile top 10 for 'women
in tech New Zealand' — high-priority technical SEO gap").

**Outcomes:** Production-deployed and operating She Sharp's full member lifecycle
for ~10 months (first commit 2025-07-22, latest 2026-05-11). Commit
volume: **748 total commits, 648 (86.6%) authored solo by Chan
Meng**, with the remaining ~100 commits by Lesley Gao on frontend
design and code. **345 of those commits landed in 2026 H1 alone** —
the platform is in active build-out, not in maintenance mode. The
platform now runs She Sharp's membership, mentorship, events,
sponsor relationships, volunteer recruitment pipeline, and internal
team operations across **95 API routes, 53 pages, 145 components,
27 SQL migrations, and an 11-namespace `lib/` library** (matching,
slack-bot, stripe, funding, programmes, recruitment, invitations,
mentorship, email, auth, db) — the technical infrastructure for an
organization with 2,200+ registered members, 1,000+ active, 8,000+
women supported lifetime, 50+ corporate partners, 84+ events since
2014, 18 industry mentors, and a reported 85% mentorship success
rate (mentees achieve 5-6× higher promotion likelihood per She
Sharp's own outcomes data).

**The Her Waka programme launched in March 2026** with NZ Ministry
of Social Development funding, using the dedicated teaching site I
built (herwaka.shesharp.org.nz) and the curriculum I authored —
4 workshops + 8 tutorials in 3 tiers. **The SEO audit** identified
actionable competitive gaps — notably the desktop/mobile ranking
disparity on the core query "women in tech New Zealand" — and
informed near-term content strategy. **The migration crawler** enabled
a clean cutover from Webflow without losing institutional knowledge.
**The Slack-bot + agent-skill pairing** replaced an hours-long
manual event-publication workflow with a single dry-run-default
command; the most-recent cron-hardening commit (`84f788f`, "make
weekly-mentorship-stats survive cold Neon pools") is the kind of
small production-resilience detail that only emerges from operating
the system, not just building it.

**My role:** Senior Full Stack Engineer and Website Team Lead. Recruited directly
by founder Dr Mahsa Mohaghegh (McCauley) — AI/NLP researcher and
Director of Women in Tech at AUT — specifically to deliver the AI
and full-stack senior engineering that Webflow couldn't, and that
the She Sharp team didn't have in-house.

Solo full-stack ownership of the main platform: product architecture,
database schema (1,392 lines, 30 pgEnums, 34 pgTables, 27 SQL
migrations), AI matching pipeline (5-weight rubric + Upstash Redis
cache + rule-based fallback + admin human-in-the-loop), Stripe
billing with the form→checkout→invitation-code activation flow,
Slack ecosystem (read-only event-collector, 3 weekly Cron digests,
interactive event handlers), the `sync-event-from-slack` Claude
agent skill with strict Zod validation and dry-run default, admin
systems, deployments, observability, and operational support.
Lesley Gao contributed some frontend design and frontend code
(~100 commits, 13.4% of total); the remaining 648 commits (86.6%)
are mine. Beyond the main platform I also designed and built the
data migration crawler that bootstrapped the launch
(she-sharp-crawl), the Her Waka teaching site (her-waka) and its
complete four-workshop + 8-tutorial curriculum, and the
professional SEO audit and recommendations report
(serpdata-api-test) on shesharp.org.nz — operating across
engineering, instructional design, and strategic analysis under
one role.
### 4. FemTech Weekend Website — Docusaurus + Drizzle/Neon + Cloudflare Pages platform for China's first women's-health-tech organisation, currently delivering Shanghai Summit 2026 — Solo-built (97.2% of 544 commits across ~1 year) Gen-2 platform for
FemTech Weekend — China's first women's-health-technology
organisation, founded 2024 by Zhu Yihan in Chengdu. Docusaurus 3.9
editorial spine + Drizzle ORM on Neon serverless Postgres for form
data + 15 Cloudflare Pages Functions (~3,800 LOC) for the entire
server side, deployed on Cloudflare Pages for low-latency
cross-border edge delivery. Currently powering Shanghai Summit 2026
(June 22-25, 16 confirmed speakers headlined by Ida Tin — Clue
co-founder who coined the term "FemTech" in 2016): 10-section
summit homepage + 3 sub-pages, 4 application pipelines
(programme/speaker/pitch/ecosystem) writing to typed Postgres
tables, double-opt-in newsletter wired to a Resend segment, admin
dashboard with batch review + brand-styled approval/rejection email
automation, Gemini-powered chatbot, bilingual EN/简体中文 across
every surface.

[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://www.femtechweekend.com/
- **Tech stack:** Docusaurus 3.9.2 (classic preset + @docusaurus/theme-mermaid), React 18.2 + TypeScript 5.6 (typecheck via `tsc --noEmit` in CI), Drizzle ORM 0.45 + drizzle-kit 0.31 (3 migrations: 0000_luxuriant_riptide → 0001_programme_overhaul → 0002_speaker_overhaul), Neon serverless Postgres (@neondatabase/serverless 1.0), Cloudflare Pages + Cloudflare Pages Functions (15 endpoints, ~3,839 LOC of server-side .js), Wrangler 4.0 (deployment + local dev via `wrangler pages dev build`), @notionhq/client v3 (Notion-as-CMS for non-technical founder editorial workflow), Resend REST API (Contacts API with segment assignment + transactional email), Google Generative AI 0.24 (Gemini-powered /api/chat-stream), Cloudinary (image uploads: headshots, company overview PDFs, pitch decks), Radix UI primitives (Accordion, Avatar, Checkbox, Collapsible, Dialog, Label, Select, Separator, Slot), Tailwind CSS 3.4 + tailwindcss-animate + class-variance-authority, Framer Motion 12.35 + GSAP 3.13 (scroll + reveal animations), earth-flyline 1.6 (interactive 2D world-map with count-up statistics), i18n-iso-countries (country-code rendering across speaker/partner data), gray-matter (MDX frontmatter parsing), Docusaurus i18n (EN + 简体中文 across every page from day one), Node 22.x (pinned in engines)
- **Total commits:** 544 (Gen-2 only; Gen-1 was a separate repo with 58 commits)
- **Solo commits:** 529 (97.2%); ~15 from one occasional contributor
- **Build window:** 2025-05-08 → 2026-05-13 (~12 months active development)
- **Cloudflare Pages Functions:** 15 endpoints (~3,839 LOC of server-side .js)
- **Application pipelines:** 4 (pitch / programme / speaker / ecosystem) — each typed Postgres table with reference numbers + admin status workflow
- **Newsletter system:** Resend Contacts API + segment 7ed0369b-… + double-opt-in confirm + brand-styled welcome email
- **Admin endpoints:** 3 (admin/applications, admin/batch-review, admin/send-approval — ADMIN_API_KEY-protected)
- **Email automation:** Brand-styled HTML + plain-text (newsletter welcome, approval, rejection) — primary #AA7C52, success #10b981
- **File upload endpoints:** 4 (headshot, image, company-overview PDF, pitch-deck) → Cloudinary
- **Database tables:** 3 (pitch_applications + programme_applications + speaker_applications) — ~20 cols each with status / statusUpdatedAt / adminNotes / referenceNumber
- **SQL migrations:** 3 (0000_luxuriant_riptide → 0001_programme_overhaul → 0002_speaker_overhaul)
- **Shanghai Summit homepage:** 10 sections (SummitHero / FABGlobeSection / PartnersGrid / SpeakersGrid / AgendaTimeline / WhoShouldAttend / VenueShowcase / WhyDifferent / GlobalEcosystem / SponsorsSection) + SummitCTA
- **Shanghai Summit sub-pages:** 3 (programme / pitch / agenda — each with its own hero + form/timeline)
- **Confirmed speakers (2026-05):** 16 — Ida Tin (Clue co-founder, coined 'FemTech' 2016) headlines; Alice Zheng, Dominnique Karetsos, Lindsay Davis, Vanessa Julia Carpenter, Rachel Bartholomew, Maaike Steinebach, Stella Fu, Peixian Lu, Sandy Lv, Chai Ke 柴可, Chen Jian, Jazmin Seregi, Judy Lux, Abraham Morse, Ira Evdokimova
- **Editorial content directories:** 3 (blog/ research reports, opinions/ commentary, stories/ founder profiles) — Docusaurus blog plugin instances
- **Bilingual locales:** 2 (en + zh-Hans) across every page from day one — not retrofit
- **Image performance pass:** 14 images compressed; social-card 6 MB → 240 KB; site image weight reduced ~67% (`19d3e71`)
**Summary:** Solo-built Gen-2 platform (97.2% of 544 commits across ~1 year) for
FemTech Weekend, China's first women's-health-technology
organisation. Docusaurus 3.9 editorial spine + Drizzle ORM on Neon
Postgres + 15 Cloudflare Pages Functions deployed on Cloudflare
Pages. Currently powering Shanghai Summit 2026 (June 22-25, Ida Tin
headlines, 16 confirmed speakers) with a 10-section summit homepage,
3 sub-pages, 4 typed application pipelines (pitch / programme /
speaker / ecosystem), a double-opt-in newsletter wired to a Resend
segment, an admin dashboard with ADMIN_API_KEY-protected batch
review and brand-styled approval/rejection email automation, a
Gemini-powered chatbot, and bilingual EN / 简体中文 across every
surface. Built CTO-side under founder Zhu Yihan.

**Impact:** Gen-2 platform (Docusaurus, a deliberate rewrite away from Next.js for editorial focus) currently operating Shanghai Summit 2026 with 16 confirmed speakers including FemTech term originator Ida Tin — 97.2% solo on 544 commits, hosting 2 original research reports.

**Problem:** FemTech Weekend is China's first organisation built specifically
around women's-health technology — a community, research outfit,
and event producer rolled into one, headquartered in Chengdu,
founded 2024 by Zhu Yihan, with a 2-10-person core team. Its
remit is to take the international FemTech movement (a category
coined in 2016 by Ida Tin, the Clue co-founder who headlines the
2026 Shanghai Summit) and ground it in the Greater China
market: connect founders, investors, healthcare professionals,
and academic researchers across the China-global divide,
positioned publicly as the "China representative for
cross-border women's-health initiatives." The organisation runs
ecosystem partnerships with Bayer and PwC, hosts quarterly
updates on China FemTech innovation, collaborates with FemTech
Across Borders on international programmes, and publishes
original market research (FemTech Market Map for Greater China,
FemmeHealth Ventures Alliance reports).

Two website generations have lived inside this 12-month window.
Gen-1 (femtechweekend.com, separate repo, 58 commits, 100%
solo) was a Next.js 15 + Notion-CMS marketing site. Gen-2 —
the entry described here — was a deliberate rewrite, not a
refactor, after the organisation's centre of gravity shifted
from "marketing site that publishes the occasional report" to
