"research publication and multi-event hub with deep editorial
content," and culminating in the **Shanghai Summit 2026** —
a four-day flagship event running June 22-25, 2026 with the
theme *Where Global Women's Health Meets China*. The summit
requires its own first-class digital infrastructure: a
sizeable speaker lineup, multi-track agenda, four distinct
application pipelines, an ecosystem partner directory, a
sponsor programme, and a press-ready editorial surface — all
of it bilingual EN / 简体中文, all of it edge-delivered
across the China-global divide, and all of it operable by a
non-technical founder when I am not in the loop.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. **Founders, investors, and
ecosystem operators** in women's-health technology — the
people who apply to the programme, pitch competition, speaker
pool, or ecosystem directory; subscribe to the newsletter;
and read the research reports. They span both Chinese and
international markets, so every primary surface ships
bilingually. **Shanghai Summit 2026 attendees and prospective
attendees** — 16 confirmed speakers including Ida Tin, plus
partners (AVPN among them), sponsors across multiple tiers,
and a delegate audience drawn from the femtech community
globally. **The FemTech Weekend internal team** — primarily
founder Zhu Yihan, who edits Notion content directly without
developer involvement, and who reviews applications through
the ADMIN_API_KEY-protected admin dashboard and approves
ecosystem applicants via the brand-styled email automation
I built into the platform.

**Product logic:** The platform is organised around **four parallel surfaces**
that the founder operates independently, plus a fifth
founder-only surface (the admin backend).

**Surface 1 — the editorial spine.** Docusaurus 3.9 with three
blog-plugin instances: `blog/` (research reports, currently
2 published — FemTech Market Map for Greater China 2025-05,
and the FemmeHealth Ventures Alliance partnership 2025-06),
`opinions/` (industry commentary), and `stories/` (founder
profiles). Each surface has its own authors and tags. The
choice of Docusaurus over keeping Next.js was deliberate: for
a research-publication-plus-event hub, the gain in
MDX-native editorial ergonomics outweighed the loss of
Next.js's rendering flexibility.

**Surface 2 — the Shanghai Summit 2026 event site.** A 10-
section homepage at `/shanghai-summit/` composed of
SummitHero → FABGlobeSection → PartnersGrid → SpeakersGrid →
AgendaTimeline → WhoShouldAttend → VenueShowcase → WhyDifferent →
GlobalEcosystem → SponsorsSection, with SummitCTA bookending
the page. Three sub-pages: `/shanghai-summit/programme` (8-week
leadership-development programme application), `/shanghai-
summit/pitch` (pitch-competition application with deck upload),
and `/shanghai-summit/agenda` (hour-by-hour DetailedAgendaDay
timeline across the four event days). Speaker data lives in a
typed `src/data/shanghai-summit.ts` module so non-technical
edits go through a single source of truth.

**Surface 3 — the four application pipelines.** Each pipeline
is a typed Drizzle table on Neon Postgres with its own
Cloudflare Pages Function endpoint:

  - **pitch_applications** (~18 cols incl. company_name,
    company_website, role_title, company_type, health_focus,
    work_areas, business_model, annual_revenue,
    pitch_deck_url, market_served, status, status_updated_at,
    admin_notes, reference_number)
  - **programme_applications** (~22 cols including the
    China-engagement nuances: china_objectives,
    target_customer, china_relevance, china_engagement,
    china_concerns, worthwhile_outcome, consent_data)
  - **speaker_applications** (~16 cols including headshot_url,
    bio, speaking_topics, session_format, interested_events,
    previous_experience)
  - **ecosystem submissions** (handled inline through
    `submit-ecosystem.js`)

Every submission gets a `reference_number`, defaults to
`status: 'submitted'`, and is reviewable in the admin
dashboard. **PDFs (pitch decks, company overviews) and images
(headshots) upload to Cloudinary** through four dedicated
`upload-*.js` endpoints — keeping object storage cost
predictable and detached from Postgres row size.

**Surface 4 — the newsletter system.** `subscribe-newsletter.js`
creates a contact in Resend via the Contacts API with
explicit segment assignment (segment ID
`7ed0369b-5209-4a19-a99b-1decde318083`), handling the 409
"contact already exists" path by falling back to the
contact-to-segment join endpoint. The endpoint then sends a
brand-styled HTML welcome email (primary `#AA7C52`, paired
with a plain-text fallback for accessibility) explaining what
the subscriber will receive — research, ecosystem updates,
event invitations, exclusive resources. **A second endpoint
`/api/confirm-subscription` handles the double-opt-in
confirmation flow** so subscribers explicitly verify intent
before getting added to the active segment. All emails carry
XSS-escaped subscriber identity, an unsubscribe foothold, and
the canonical from-address `noreply@femtechweekend.com` (or
whichever address `RESEND_FROM_EMAIL` resolves to in the
Cloudflare environment binding).

**Surface 5 — the admin backend.** `src/pages/admin/
applications.tsx` is the founder-facing dashboard, backed by
three protected Cloudflare Pages Functions:
`admin/applications.js` (list/filter all four pipelines),
`admin/batch-review.js` (batch status updates), and
`admin/send-approval.js` (sends a brand-styled
approval-or-rejection email — success-green `#10b981`
gradient for approvals; the email template enumerates what
the ecosystem member now gets: directory listing, exclusive
networking events, early access to research reports,
investor introductions). **All admin endpoints check
`X-Admin-Key` against the `ADMIN_API_KEY` environment binding
before any database mutation.** XSS-escaping is enforced on
every interpolated field of the email templates as a
defensive default.

Two cross-cutting capabilities: **a Gemini-powered chatbot**
(`/api/chat-stream` over `@google/generative-ai`) for visitor
Q&A about the organisation and summit, and **an interactive
2D world-map** built with `earth-flyline` carrying the
visual weight of the summit's "global meets China" thesis
on the front page, with count-up statistics rendered
responsively across screen sizes and theming-aware
light/dark mode.

**Business logic:** FemTech Weekend is a private organisation; revenue and
sustainability flow through three channels that the platform
directly operationalises: **(1) ecosystem partnerships**
(Bayer, PwC, AVPN, FemTech Across Borders) where the
platform's directory listing + visibility is part of the
partner offer; **(2) the Shanghai Summit 2026 itself** —
ticketed delegate access plus a multi-tier sponsor programme
where the platform's homepage SponsorsSection + dedicated
sponsor logos are part of the sponsor deliverable; and
**(3) research-report distribution + thought leadership** —
the published reports (FemTech Market Map for Greater China,
FemmeHealth Ventures Alliance) function as lead-generation
artefacts that feed the ecosystem and newsletter pipelines.

The platform's job in this economy is to be the
**trust-signal-bearing public surface** that lets a Western
founder, a Chinese investor, a corporate partner, or a
prospective speaker take the organisation seriously inside
15 seconds of arrival — and the **operational substrate** that
lets the 2-10-person core team triage applications, publish
editorial, and run the summit without scaling headcount.
Every architectural choice on Gen-2 is downstream of those
two demands.

**Technical approach:** **Docusaurus 3.9 as editorial spine, Drizzle/Neon for typed
application data, Cloudflare Pages Functions for the entire
server side.** No Node backend, no container, no separate
admin app — the whole platform is one Docusaurus site with
15 colocated `functions/api/*.js` Pages Functions handling
every server-side concern.

**15 Cloudflare Pages Functions, ~3,839 LOC of server-side
JavaScript**, organised by intent:

  - **4 application submitters**: `submit-programme.js`,
    `submit-speaker.js`, `submit-pitch.js`,
    `submit-ecosystem.js` — each validates input, persists to
    its typed Drizzle table, assigns a reference number, and
    triggers a confirmation email.
  - **2 newsletter endpoints**: `subscribe-newsletter.js` and
    `confirm-subscription.js` — Resend Contacts API with
    explicit segment assignment, 409-conflict fallback to
    contact-to-segment join, double-opt-in confirmation,
    brand-styled welcome email.
  - **4 Cloudinary uploaders**: `upload-headshot.js`,
    `upload-image.js`, `upload-company-overview.js`,
    `upload-pitch-deck.js` — each enforces accepted MIME types
    and produces a Cloudinary public URL written back to the
    corresponding Postgres row.
  - **3 admin endpoints**: `admin/applications.js` (filtered
    list), `admin/batch-review.js` (bulk status updates),
    `admin/send-approval.js` (approval / rejection email
    sender) — every one checks `X-Admin-Key` against
    `ADMIN_API_KEY` env binding before touching the DB.
  - **1 chatbot**: `chat-stream.js` over
    `@google/generative-ai` (Gemini), streamed response.
  - **1 PDF intake**: `pdf-form-submit.js`.

**Drizzle schema, 3 tables, ~3 migrations**:
`0000_luxuriant_riptide` (initial pitch + programme +
speaker tables) → `0001_programme_overhaul` (programme-form
restructure including the China-engagement column family) →
`0002_speaker_overhaul` (speaker-form refinement). All
tables share a common pattern: identifying fields → typed
domain fields → uploaded asset URLs → admin lifecycle
(`status` defaulting to `'submitted'`, `status_updated_at`,
`admin_notes`, `reference_number`), with `created_at`
defaulted via Drizzle's `defaultNow()`. Schema lives at
`src/db/schema.ts` (81 lines); CLI access via `drizzle-kit
0.31` (`drizzle.config.ts`).

**Email automation discipline.** Every transactional template
is hand-authored HTML + plain-text twin, with brand colours
baked in (`#AA7C52` primary, `#996F49` dark, `#10b981`
success, `#ef4444` error) and a consistent header / body /
footer table-based layout that renders cleanly in Outlook
and Gmail's quirkier modes. Every interpolated string passes
through an `escapeHtml(str)` helper before reaching the
template — a defensive default against any future
admin-input contamination. Resend REST API is called
directly (no SDK), so the same code runs unchanged on the
Cloudflare Workers runtime.

**Notion-as-CMS for editorial content** in both generations
— articles, ecosystem profiles, event reports edit directly
inside Notion and render live. Implemented in Gen-2 with
the modern `@notionhq/client v3` SDK pulling structured
content into Docusaurus pages. This is the single most
important UX decision in the system: it removes me from the
publishing critical path and gives the founder full
editorial control without needing to learn markdown or any
custom CMS.

**Bilingual everything, from day one.** Both generations
ship in EN and 简体中文 — not as an afterthought, but as a
first-class architectural constraint that shapes routing,
content models, speaker data structures, agenda time-zone
rendering, and editorial copy. Docusaurus's native i18n
plugin handles the routing; the Gen-2 content layer is
built so that every speaker, partner, agenda block, and
editorial piece has parallel locales rather than fall-
through defaults.

**Image performance discipline.** The summit content is
image-heavy (speaker headshots, venue photography, partner
logos), so I ran a deliberate pass (commit `19d3e71`,
2026-05-12) that compressed and optimised 14 large images
and shrank the social-card image from 6 MB to 240 KB —
overall reducing site image weight by roughly 67% without
losing visual fidelity. TypeScript checks (`tsc --noEmit`)
plus Docusaurus's build warnings treated as errors form the
CI safety net.

**Cloudflare Pages + Wrangler 4.0** for global edge
delivery. Local dev composes `docusaurus start` with
`wrangler pages dev build --port 3001` via `concurrently`
so functions are testable against the same edge runtime
they'll run on in production. Node 22.x pinned in
`engines`. Drizzle migrations applied via `drizzle-kit
push` against the Neon serverless connection.

**Outcomes:** Production-deployed at femtechweekend.com and operating the
full organisational pipeline (ecosystem applications,
newsletter, summit registration, research distribution)
across both EN and 简体中文 audiences. **544 commits, 529
(97.2%) authored solo by Chan Meng** across ~12 months
(2025-05-08 → 2026-05-13). **Shanghai Summit 2026 launches
June 22-25, 2026** with the platform as its sole
operational substrate.

Recent commit cadence shows the platform is in active
delivery, not maintenance: the last week of recorded
activity alone includes speaker-lineup rotation (`6b0b780`,
rotating the speaker grid and refreshing Ida Tin's title),
sponsor-tier restructuring (`6f33496`), the image-weight
67%-reduction pass (`19d3e71`), and an ongoing speaker-card
and modal refinement series — all of it solo, all of it on
a live site marketing a flagship event two months out.

The platform demonstrates a design call worth flagging:
**Generation 2 was a deliberate rewrite, not a refactor**.
I admitted that the centre of gravity had shifted from
"marketing site that publishes the occasional report" to
"research-publication and multi-event hub with deep
editorial content," and rebuilt on Docusaurus 3.9 — not
the default choice for a brand website, but the right one
for an editorial platform whose long-term value lives in
its market research, opinion pieces, founder stories, and
event archives. The Gen-1 → Gen-2 cutover happened
without losing institutional knowledge and is now the
primary public-facing surface for the organisation.

Two original research reports have been published through
the platform: **FemTech Market Map for Greater China**
(2025-05) and the **FemmeHealth Ventures Alliance**
partnership announcement (2025-06). Both function as
lead-generation artefacts feeding the ecosystem and
newsletter pipelines, and as thought-leadership
positioning for the organisation in both Chinese and
international markets.

**My role:** Chief Technology Officer, hired 2025-03 by founder Zhu
Yihan with full ownership of every digital surface the
organisation operates. Solo engineering team (with one
occasional contributor of ~15 commits out of 544).
End-to-end authority over architecture, vendor choice,
deployment infrastructure, content management workflow,
application and subscription pipelines, and the live
digital infrastructure for the Shanghai Summit 2026.

Specifically: designed and built both website generations
from scratch (Gen-1 Next.js + Notion at 100% solo across
58 commits in a separate repo; Gen-2 — this entry — at
97.2% solo across 544 commits over ~12 months). Wrote the
entire Drizzle schema, every migration, all 15 Cloudflare
Pages Functions, every email template (HTML + plain-text
twins, brand-coloured, XSS-defended), the admin dashboard,
the four application-pipeline forms, the Shanghai Summit
10-section homepage + 3 sub-pages, the Notion-as-CMS
integration, the bilingual EN/简体中文 routing + content
layer, the Gemini chatbot integration, the earth-flyline
global-network visualisation, and the image-performance
optimisation pass. Operate the production Cloudflare
Pages deployment and every speaker-lineup / sponsor / agenda
update directly on `main`. Reporting line: directly to
founder Zhu Yihan; collaboration with the broader 2-10-
person core team on editorial calendar, summit programming,
and ecosystem partnerships.
### 5. Eatropolis Website — Production-grade Next.js 16 + OpenNext-on-Cloudflare-Workers event platform for Auckland's award-winning culinary festival (2026-10-10, Shed 10) — Solo-built freelance commission under signed
4 May 2026 agreement with Chow Luck Club Ltd; PM Armand Bentigan)
for the official `eatropolis.co.nz` event website — Auckland's
one-day culinary festival on **2026-10-10 at Shed 10, Queens
Wharf, Tāmaki Makaurau**, featuring **30+ award-winning chefs**.
Production-grade in the literal contract sense: 9-day intensive
build with two-phase public reveal schedule (Phase 1 teaser
2026-05-22, Phase 2 full site 2026-06-05), engineered to handle
**1,000 concurrent visitors** under k6 load testing per contract,
hardened for the **NZ Government Web Accessibility Standard 1.2
(WCAG 2.2 AA)** with **Tātaki Auckland Unlimited (TAU)** review
gates, deployed to **Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext** (the
official 2026 Next.js-on-Cloudflare path, not the legacy Pages
adapter), with Resend (DKIM/SPF/DMARC live, mail-tester 10/10),
streaming OpenAI chat + Whisper + TTS gated behind Cloudflare
Turnstile + KV per-IP rate-limit, GA4 + CF Web Analytics behind
a NZ Privacy Act 2020-compliant cookie banner, and a **GitHub-
as-CMS** content workflow with one-click `✏️` Web-Editor links so
the non-technical client edits MDX + JSON directly in the browser.

[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://eatropolis.co.nz/
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 16.2.5 (App Router, React Server Components, ISR), React 19.2.4, TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS 4 with @theme DTCG design tokens → CSS custom properties, @opennextjs/cloudflare 1.19.7 (official 2026 deploy path, Pages adapter retired), Wrangler 4.88 (deploy + local preview), Drizzle ORM 0.45 + drizzle-kit 0.31 (2 SQL migrations + 4-namespace schema split: chat / email / forms / index), Neon serverless Postgres (@neondatabase/serverless 1.1), MDX (@next/mdx + @mdx-js/loader + remark-frontmatter + gray-matter) — 43 MDX + 16 JSON content files, Zod 4.4 (validates every MDX frontmatter + JSON page-chrome blob at build time), Resend 6 + @react-email/components 1.0 + @react-email/render 2.0 (7 hand-authored React Email templates), Vercel AI SDK 6 (@ai-sdk/openai 3 + @ai-sdk/react 3 + ai 6, provider-abstracted via lib/ai-provider.ts), OpenAI gpt-5.4-mini (chat) + Whisper (voice transcribe) + TTS (voice reply), @marsidev/react-turnstile 1.5 (Cloudflare Turnstile Managed mode anti-spam), Cloudflare KV (EATROPOLIS_CHAT_KV per-IP rate limit + session cookie), Svix 1.93 (Resend webhook signature verification), Framer Motion 12.38, Ticketmaster Discovery API (ISR cache 1h, server-only key, UTM-tagged outbound), Cloudflare Zero Trust Access (3 self-hosted apps: pre-launch lockdown + webhook bypass + newsletter bypass), Cloudflare Web Analytics + Google Analytics 4 (manual JS-snippet mode behind cookie consent), Playwright 1.59 + @axe-core/playwright 4.11 (39/39 axe tests pass; mobile-nav + target-size + a11y suites), k6 (load testing → 1,000 concurrent VUs per contract §3.8), GitHub Actions (~55-70s wall-time auto-deploy on every push to main)
- **Engagement type:** Signed commercial commission, three-stage delivery schedule (Phase 1 internal delivery + Phase 2 internal delivery + final acceptance)
- **Build span:** 2026-05-07 → 2026-05-15 (9-day intensive delivery per contract §5)
- **Phase 1 public launch:** 2026-05-22 (teaser landing page with newsletter capture)
- **Phase 2 public launch:** 2026-06-05 (full site + Ticketmaster integration + workshop RSVPs)
- **Event day:** 2026-10-10 at Shed 10, Queens Wharf, Tāmaki Makaurau
- **Free maintenance window:** 5 June 2026 → 5 September 2026 (hard expiry per contract §15.1)
- **Total commits:** 115 (solo; private client-org repo at github.com/Chow-Luck-Club/eatropolis-website)
- **Production routes:** 68 prerendered (per eleventh-pass STATUS doc 2026-05-12; up from 62 in ninth pass)
- **API routes:** 10 (chat + chat/feedback + contact + newsletter + partnership + transcribe + tts + unsubscribe + webhooks/resend + workshop-rsvp)
- **Pages (page.tsx files):** 13 marketing pages
- **React components:** 58 (.tsx) across 14 namespaces (analytics, cards, chat, consent, dishes, faq, forms, hero, legal, mdx, motion, nav, tickets, ui)
- **Content files:** 43 MDX (dishes/workshops/faqs/partners/sponsors/site) + 16 JSON (page-chrome) = 59 total
- **React Email templates:** 7 (ContactAcknowledgment, ContactAdminNotice, PartnershipAcknowledgment, PartnershipAdminNotice, UnsubscribeConfirmation, WelcomeNewsletter, WorkshopRsvpConfirmation)
- **Drizzle schema namespaces:** 4 (chat / email / forms / index) with 4 repositories (chat / email-events / forms / subscribers)
- **SQL migrations:** 2 (0000_elite_molecule_man + 0001_green_hex — the second a partial-index ON CONFLICT mismatch hotfix)
- **Lighthouse Mobile target:** ≥ 90 per contract §3.8; achieved 71 → 84 Performance via font-weight trim + lazy-mount AIAssistant + deferred hero video sources
- **Accessibility verification:** 39/39 axe-core Playwright tests pass; WAI-ARIA APG NavDropdown menu keyboard pattern; conformance statement per WCAG 2.2 AA contract §3.10
- **Email deliverability:** mail-tester 10/10 — SPF + DKIM + DMARC all live on eatropolis.co.nz; Gmail.email-delivered webhook round-trip 5.96s end-to-end
- **Load testing:** k6 to 1,000 concurrent VUs per contract §3.8 (full profile pending Phase 2 Access disable on 2026-06-05)
- **Deploy pipeline:** GitHub Actions push-to-main → opennextjs-cloudflare build + deploy, ~55-70s end-to-end
- **Cloudflare Zero Trust apps:** 3 self-hosted Access apps (pre-launch lockdown `e1ff6f56-…`, webhook bypass, newsletter bypass `96232ff1-…`); newsletter bypass uses 5/5 hostname destination slots (platform limit)
**Summary:** Production-grade event website for Eatropolis — Auckland's
one-day culinary festival (2026-10-10, Shed 10, 30+ award-
winning chefs) — solo-built under signed freelance contract
with Chow Luck Club Ltd. Next.js 16.2 + React 19.2 +
Tailwind 4, deployed to Cloudflare Workers via the official
OpenNext 2026 path. Engineered to the contract's
production-grade requirements: 1,000-concurrent-visitor k6 load
tested, NZ Government Web Accessibility Standard 1.2 (WCAG 2.2
AA) conformance with TAU review gates, mail-tester 10/10 Resend
pipeline, streaming OpenAI chat with Whisper + TTS voice behind
Cloudflare Turnstile + KV rate-limiting, GA4 + Web Analytics
behind a NZ Privacy Act cookie consent gate, ISR-cached
Ticketmaster Discovery API integration, and a GitHub-as-CMS
content workflow with ✏️ Web-Editor links so the non-technical
client edits all MDX + JSON in-browser. Two-phase visibility
reveal on one coherent build (Phase 1 teaser 2026-05-22 → Phase
2 full site 2026-06-05).

**Impact:** Auckland Eatropolis festival (30+ chefs, Shed 10) — signed commission with Tātaki Auckland Unlimited (Council Events) review gates, WCAG 2.2 AA, streaming OpenAI chat + voice assistant, and 1,000-visitor load test; solo-built in 9 days.

**Problem:** Auckland-based **Chow Luck Club Ltd** — production partner
for the **Eatropolis** culinary festival, supported by
**Tātaki Auckland Unlimited on behalf of Auckland Council
Events** — commissioned a production-grade event website for
the one-day festival on **2026-10-10 at Shed 10, Queens
Wharf, Tāmaki Makaurau** featuring **30+ award-winning
chefs**. The brief, formalised in a signed 4 May 2026
commercial agreement, demanded a real public
product: a custom-designed brand site at `eatropolis.co.nz`
with a two-phase public reveal (Phase 1 teaser 2026-05-22,
Phase 2 full site 2026-06-05), accessibility conformance to
the **NZ Government Web Accessibility Standard 1.2 (WCAG 2.2
Level AA)** with TAU review gates before each launch, load-
testing to **1,000 concurrent visitors**, Lighthouse Mobile
≥ 90 across all categories, SEO + GEO discoverability
(sitemap, robots, JSON-LD Event + FAQPage, llms.txt), full
forms infrastructure with branded transactional email, a
conditional AI chat assistant, GA4 + privacy-friendly
analytics, and a content-edit workflow the non-technical
client could operate independently after handoff — all
delivered solo by a single freelance developer in nine
working days, with three months of post-launch maintenance
bundled into the fee.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. **Festival attendees** —
public visitors arriving via search, social, or partner
sites; mobile-first; high traffic spikes around ticket-
release and event-day windows. **The Eatropolis ecosystem**
— 30+ culinary partners with chefs and award-winning
dishes, workshop hosts, multi-tier sponsors (Principal /
Al Fresco / Associate / Community), ticketing partner,
media coverage — each with editorial surfaces on the site
that they review before launch. **The Chow Luck Club
internal team + TAU reviewers** — non-developer client
signatory Bee Keng Koh, day-to-day PM Armand Bentigan, and
the Tātaki Auckland Unlimited review committee that gates
each public launch on accessibility + content compliance.
The content-edit workflow is built around the assumption
that none of these reviewers will ever touch a terminal:
every editable field is reachable through GitHub's browser
Web Editor via a single `✏️` link.

**Product logic:** The product is structured as **one coherent build with a
two-phase visibility schedule**, not two separate websites.
The same Cloudflare Worker serves both phases; what changes
is which routes return content vs. a holding page.

**Phase 1 (2026-05-22):** only `/coming-soon` is publicly
reachable. It carries the "Something Big is Cooking" teaser,
partner attribution, key event statistics, and a newsletter
email-capture form connected to Resend Audiences. All other
routes return the holding page. Implemented via a Cloudflare
Zero Trust Access app `Eatropolis pre-launch` that gates
every non-allowlisted path; the **Newsletter bypass app**
(`96232ff1-10e1-4a58-8a63-953407d45df3`) selectively
publishes 5 essential paths (coming-soon, _next/*, images/*,
api/newsletter, opengraph-image) — exactly the platform
cap of 5 destinations per Access app, discovered the hard
way after the original 14-row plan returned `too many
destinations`.

**Phase 2 (2026-06-05):** the Access lockdown app is
disabled at launch morning; the full **68-route**
prerendered site is revealed simultaneously. The single
launch-announcement broadcast email goes out to all Phase 1
newsletter subscribers via Resend, with the Client
providing copy and the Developer formatting and sending.

**The full-site information architecture** spans the 13
marketing pages: Home (hero video, scroll-card story,
restaurant logo carousel, "Your Day at Eatropolis" steps,
ticketing partner block, sponsor wall, final CTA),
Tickets (two-tier flat ticketing — **General Admission**
and **Family Bundle**, with **workshops included with
admission**, plus a sticky purchase CTA and Ticketmaster
outbound), Dishes (filterable grid of 15 signature plates),
Culinary Partners
(filterable grid of restaurants with featured spotlights),
Workshops (listing + per-workshop RSVP form),
Event Info (date/time/location + schedule timeline +
getting-there guide), Our Partners (4-tier sponsor
showcase: Principal / Al Fresco / Associate / Community),
Contact (general + partnership enquiry forms), plus
legal pages.

**Four form pipelines**, all server-actions over `/api/`:
General Enquiry → `info@eatropolis.co.nz`; Partnership
Enquiry → `partnership@eatropolis.co.nz`; Workshop
Reservation (one per workshop) → client-chosen address;
Newsletter signup → Resend Audiences. **All forms gated
by Cloudflare Turnstile** in Managed mode (better UX than
reCAPTCHA, lower friction). **All transactional email
flows through 7 hand-authored React Email templates** —
ContactAcknowledgment + ContactAdminNotice for general
enquiries (visitor gets a brand-styled acknowledgment, the
client gets the full submission with reply-to set);
PartnershipAcknowledgment + PartnershipAdminNotice for
sponsor enquiries; WorkshopRsvpConfirmation for workshop
RSVPs; WelcomeNewsletter for newsletter signups;
UnsubscribeConfirmation for one-click unsubscribe.

**The conditional AI chat assistant** is a floating widget
that streams responses from OpenAI `gpt-5.4-mini` over the
Vercel AI SDK 6, with **voice input via Whisper
(`/api/transcribe`)** and **voice reply via TTS
(`/api/tts`)** — abstracted behind `lib/ai-provider.ts` so
the entire stack can swap to Anthropic Claude in one line.
It's pre-trained with the event's content (dates, location,
tickets, partners, FAQs, transport) via a build-time-
generated chat-context manifest (`scripts/generate-content-
manifest.mjs` runs as predev/prebuild/predeploy hook).
Visitor cost stays bounded via **Cloudflare KV
(`EATROPOLIS_CHAT_KV`)** per-IP rate limit + session
cookie + Turnstile pre-clearance, with feedback persisted
via `/api/chat/feedback`.

**The content layer is the deliberate UX call of the
project.** The brief required a "CMS structure"; the
contract solution is **GitHub-as-CMS via the browser Web
Editor**, explicitly rejecting Excel/Google Sheets as well
as licensed CMS products (Sanity, Strapi, Payload,
Contentful) since the scope explicitly avoids additional
third-party dependencies. The implementation: every editable
field has a `✏️` direct link in `docs/CONTENT-INVENTORY.md`
pointing at the repo's `edit/main/<path>` GitHub Web Editor
URL — the non-technical client
clicks, edits in GitHub's browser editor, commits, and
GitHub Actions auto-deploys in ~3 minutes. The content
itself is **43 MDX files** (dishes, workshops, FAQs,
partners, sponsors — each with Zod-validated frontmatter)
+ **16 JSON page-chrome blobs** in `content/site/pages/`
(home / tickets / dishes / workshops / event-info / partners
/ sponsors / contact / partnership / coming-soon, plus
site-wide nav / footer / social / tickets / chat / brand
/ announcement). A 9-section `EditorialPageSchema` +
`VideoPageSchema` in `lib/content/site-schema.ts` give
the JSON structural type-safety end-to-end.

**Business logic:** This is a **commercial freelance commission** — not a
personal project, not a passion side-build. The delivery
is anchored to a signed contract with a three-stage
schedule **(Phase 1 internal delivery 2026-05-16, Phase 2
internal delivery 2026-05-29, Phase 3 acceptance)** —
each stage tied to a **Developer deliverable**, not to TAU
approval, so review-cycle delays don't gate progress.

**The contract's scope discipline is the second
architectural axis**: §3 enumerates exactly 14 explicit
items in scope (the site, the design system, the animation
layer, 4 forms, 1 launch broadcast, the conditional AI
widget, SEO + GEO, performance + load testing + security
hardening, GA4 + analytics, WCAG 2.2 AA conformance,
Cloudflare hosting, initial content seeding for 10
partners + 10 dishes + 6 workshops + 15 sponsors, hand-
off + training, and 3 months of maintenance). **Every
category outside §3 — additional pages, custom ticketing,
multilingual versions, blog/news section, marketing-email
campaigns beyond the single launch broadcast, formal
third-party WCAG audit, future-year event refreshes, deep
CRM integrations — sits under §4 Available Add-ons** with
separately-quoted scope, or under §12 hourly engagement
after the maintenance window expires on 2026-09-06. The
warranty (§15) is hard-capped at the 3-month maintenance
window. This isn't a contract I sign for someone else — I
wrote it (in Typst, compiled to PDF), so the scope
discipline is by design: 9 days of intensive solo work
surrounded by a clearly-bounded commercial perimeter that
doesn't let the project bleed into 2027 or 2028 event-year
obligations.

**The client side of the business model** is itself
worth surfacing: Chow Luck Club Ltd produces the
festival as a private event business; **Tātaki Auckland
Unlimited (Auckland Council's culture, business, and
economic-development arm)** sponsors it on behalf of
Auckland Council Events. That sponsor relationship is
why the contract is explicit about WCAG 2.2 AA
conformance with TAU review gates — TAU has organisational
compliance obligations to the New Zealand Government Web
Accessibility Standard 1.2, and the contract draws a
clean line between what the website **technically
conforms to** (in scope: automated audits, manual checks,
a written conformance statement) versus the **formal
third-party accessibility audit + Web Standards Self-
Assessment + Risk Assessment and Management Plan** that
TAU as a public-sector body itself files (out of scope —
organisational compliance, not a website deliverable).

**Technical approach:** **Stack:** Next.js 16.2.5 (App Router, React Server
Components, ISR) + React 19.2.4 + TypeScript 5 +
Tailwind CSS 4 (with `@theme` DTCG design tokens
consumed as CSS custom properties) — deployed to
**Cloudflare Workers via `@opennextjs/cloudflare`
1.19.7**, the official 2026 path now that the Pages
adapter is legacy. Wrangler 4.88 handles local preview
+ deploys. The choice of OpenNext-on-Workers (not
Vercel, not Pages, not Netlify) is contract-driven:
the Client owns the Cloudflare account, and the entire
stack — hosting, DNS, anti-spam, email routing,
analytics, WAF, Zero Trust Access — is operated under
Cloudflare's free tier with the Worker upgraded to
Standard plan only after the CPU constraint of the
Free tier blocked the 1,000-VU k6 profile (Issue #17,
resolved 2026-05-09).

**Data layer:** Drizzle ORM 0.45 + `@neondatabase/
serverless` 1.1 against a Neon serverless Postgres
instance, **4-namespace schema split** at
`lib/db/schema/{chat,email,forms,index}.ts` with
**4 repository modules** at `lib/db/repositories/
{chat,email-events,forms,subscribers}.ts` for
type-safe query boundaries. **2 migrations**:
`0000_elite_molecule_man.sql` (initial schema) +
`0001_green_hex.sql` (a partial-index `ON CONFLICT`
mismatch hotfix on `email_events.resend_event_id` /
`email_outbox.resend_email_id`, caught during the
Resend webhook integration). The persistence
architecture is a **full email lifecycle audit**:
`form_submissions` (visitor input) → `email_outbox`
(Resend send attempts with idempotency keys) →
`email_events` (Resend webhook deliveries / opens /
bounces / complaints). End-to-end newsletter signup
round-trip verified 2026-05-12 at **5.96 seconds**
from form submit to Gmail delivery confirmation in
Neon.

**Content layer:** MDX (`@next/mdx` + `@mdx-js/loader`
+ `remark-frontmatter` + `gray-matter`) for long-form
editorial content + Zod 4.4 schemas for both frontmatter
and page-chrome JSON validation **at build time**. The
prebuild hook `scripts/generate-content-manifest.mjs`
compiles every content file into a typed manifest
(`lib/.generated/chat-context.ts`) that powers the AI
chat assistant's system context — the AI knows about
every dish, workshop, partner, and FAQ without runtime
DB reads. **43 MDX content files + 16 JSON page-chrome
files** total, with the schema split between
`EditorialPageSchema` and `VideoPageSchema` for the
two hero patterns.

**Email pipeline:** Resend 6 with **7 hand-authored
React Email templates** at `emails/*.tsx`, theming
centralised in `emails/_theme.ts`. The domain
`eatropolis.co.nz` has SPF + DKIM (Resend domain
verification) + a DMARC record set up via Cloudflare
DNS, with a DMARC dashboard subscribed for ongoing
deliverability monitoring. **mail-tester scores
10/10**. Resend webhook signatures are verified via
`svix` at `/api/webhooks/resend/route.ts`; webhook
traffic bypasses the Zero Trust lockdown via a
dedicated `Eatropolis webhook bypass` Access app
(path = `eatropolis.co.nz/api/webhooks/*`).

**AI layer:** Vercel AI SDK 6 + `@ai-sdk/openai` 3 +
`@ai-sdk/react` 3, abstracted behind
`lib/ai-provider.ts` so the entire chat stack swaps
to Anthropic Claude in a single line. Model:
OpenAI `gpt-5.4-mini` for streaming chat; **Whisper**
for `/api/transcribe` (voice input); **OpenAI TTS**
for `/api/tts` (voice reply). All three call paths
gated by **(1) Cloudflare Turnstile** pre-clearance,
**(2) Cloudflare KV (`EATROPOLIS_CHAT_KV`)** per-IP
rate limit with a session cookie, and **(3)** the
chat-context manifest as the prompt context so the
AI can never fabricate prices, dates, or partner
names — every answer is grounded in the JSON / MDX
layer the Client edits.

**Analytics + consent:** Cloudflare Web Analytics
(privacy-friendly, cookie-free) + Google Analytics 4
with configured conversion events (ticket-button
clicks, contact-form submits, partnership-form
submits, workshop reservations, newsletter signups,
scroll depth, session duration). Both **render in
manual JS-snippet mode (not auto-injection)** behind
a **NZ Privacy Act 2020-compliant cookie consent
banner** in `components/consent/` — nothing tracks
until the visitor accepts.

**Ticketmaster integration:** Discovery API called
server-side with a Cloudflare-secret API key, response
ISR-cached for **1 hour**, outbound links UTM-tagged
so the Client can attribute ticket revenue back to
the website in the Ticketmaster dashboard.

**Performance discipline:** Lighthouse Mobile
target ≥ 90 across all categories per contract §3.8.
Achieved Performance 71 → 84 (~25% TBT drop) by
trimming Big Shoulders font weights from
`[400,600,700,800,900]` → `[400,700,800]`,
lazy-mounting `<AIAssistant/>` via
`next/dynamic({ssr:false})` gated behind
`requestIdleCallback`, and deferring hero video
`<source>` children with `preload="none"`. Images
run through a custom optimisation pipeline at
`scripts/optimize-images.mjs` producing AVIF + WebP
ladders. **k6 load test** at `tests/load/k6.js`
profiles to **1,000 concurrent VUs** per contract.

**Accessibility:** automated + manual gates. The
automated suite is **39/39 axe-core Playwright
tests passing** across `tests/a11y.spec.ts` +
`tests/mobile-nav.spec.ts` + `tests/target-size.spec.
ts`. The manual layer covers keyboard-only navigation,
focus visibility, heading hierarchy, alt text,
semantic HTML, video captions, screen-reader spot-
checks, and **a WAI-ARIA APG NavDropdown menu
keyboard pattern** with ArrowDown/Up/Home/End + Tab-
close + Esc-no-reopen guard. **WCAG color discipline
worth flagging**: the brand's `--color-harbour` at
`#0866ff` measures 4.13:1 on parchment (failing
1.4.3 AA normal-text 4.5:1) — fixed by adding
`--color-harbour-deep` `#0050cc` at 6.02:1 on
parchment / 7.02:1 on bone / 6.37:1 on cream for
text use; saturated harbour stays in decorative non-
text roles only (focus rings, `aria-hidden` bullet
separators where 3:1 non-text minimum applies). Brand
hierarchy is Black / Red / White per employer brief,
harbour strictly subtle accent. A written **WCAG
2.2 AA conformance statement** delivered at Phase 2
internal handoff documents testing performed,
results, and known limitations.

**Pre-launch lockdown:** all routes sit behind
**Cloudflare Zero Trust Access** (free tier) between
2026-05-09 and 2026-06-05. The `Eatropolis pre-
launch` Access app (`e1ff6f56-7889-4f92-a99d-
6d209cfa051e`) gates everything except allowlisted
reviewers (`@chowluckclub.com`, `@eatropolis.co.nz`,
`chanmeng.career@gmail.com`) who authenticate via
email OTP. Two **bypass apps** carve out the
public-facing paths: `Eatropolis webhook bypass` for
Resend webhooks, and `Eatropolis newsletter bypass`
(`96232ff1-10e1-4a58-8a63-953407d45df3`) for the
Phase 1 coming-soon flow — the latter using all
**5/5 hostname destination slots** (a platform cap
discovered the hard way) to publish `coming-soon`,
`_next/*`, `images/*`, `api/newsletter`, and
`opengraph-image` while keeping the rest locked.

**Deploy pipeline:** GitHub Actions
(`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) triggers on every
push to `main` plus manual `workflow_dispatch`, runs
on `ubuntu-latest` (Linux mandatory — Windows is
explicitly blocked in `AGENTS.md` because the
OpenNext build chain has Windows incompatibilities),
executes `pnpm install` → `lint` → `pnpm run deploy`
(which calls `opennextjs-cloudflare build &&
opennextjs-cloudflare deploy`). **Typical wall time
~55-70 seconds end-to-end**, with `CLOUDFLARE_API_
TOKEN` + `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID` (`3f0d36625c1a1a7b
1f2ee2b211a87791`) stored in GitHub Secrets.

**Outcomes:** Production-deployed at `eatropolis.co.nz` since
2026-05-09, currently in **pre-launch Access lockdown**
with allowlisted reviewers authenticating via Cloudflare
Zero Trust OTP. **Phase 1 coming-soon page publicly
accessible since 2026-05-12** via the dedicated bypass
app — newsletter signup end-to-end verified
(form → Neon `form_submissions` + `email_outbox` rows →
Resend accept → Gmail `email.delivered` webhook → Neon
`email_events` row in **5.96 seconds**), Gmail
Promotions-tab delivery confirmed.

## Notable Open Source Projects

### github-readme-suno-cards — Suno Music Cards for GitHub READMEs — GitHub Marketplace Action + Vercel-hosted SVG service that turns any Suno creator's catalog into auto-updating animated cards in their GitHub README — three id input formats (UUID / suno.com URL / short code), 8 GitHub Action inputs, 2 layouts x 2 presets x 3 themes (12 base combinations), CJK-safe text via SVG foreignObject + CSS keyframe equalizer. Public-extracted from a private monorepo on 2026-04-13 and now shares the @suno-cards/parser kernel with SunoStats.
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/github-readme-suno-cards
- **Tech stack:** pnpm 9.12 monorepo (apps/ + packages/ + action/), Next.js 15.1 (Vercel Edge runtime), TypeScript 5.6, @suno-cards/parser workspace package (Valibot-validated Suno oEmbed parser — shared kernel with SunoStats), @suno-cards/render workspace package (pure-function SVG renderer), Valibot (strict TypeScript schema validation against studio-api-prod.suno.com oEmbed), SVG <foreignObject> + inline HTML <span> (CJK text shaping), CSS @keyframes (equalizer-bar animation driven by HTML spans inside the SVG), GitHub Actions composite action (Node 20 runner with esbuild-bundled dist), Biome 1.9 (lint + format), Vitest (parser tests inside action/test/), Vercel Data Cache (upstream, 3600s TTL for single cards), HTTP Cache-Control headers (downstream, drives GitHub Camo proxy), esbuild (action bundle build via action/build.ts), MIT license

**Summary:** A **GitHub Marketplace Action + Vercel-hosted SVG card service**
that lets any Suno creator surface their music catalog in their
GitHub README — either as a single embedded card (zero-setup
Markdown line) or as an auto-syncing card grid kept in sync by a
scheduled GitHub Action (`uses: ChanMeng666/github-readme-suno-cards@v0.1`).
The current public repo was **extracted from a longer-lived
internal monorepo on 2026-04-13** (commit `73f4fa8`
*"initial commit — github-readme-suno-cards v0.1"* followed
immediately by `295756e` *"clean remaining internal reference"*)
and now ships as a clean, focused, MIT-licensed open-source
package. **Architectural insight**: the `@suno-cards/parser`
workspace package is **shared with SunoStats** (Chan's separate
Suno analytics SaaS) — the parser was distilled out as the common
kernel between the two projects, so a single Valibot-validated
Suno-oEmbed contract underwrites both products. Ships 12 base
card combinations (2 layouts × 2 presets × 3 themes), 11
customization toggles, hex color overrides, 3 different `id`
input formats (raw UUID / `suno.com/song/<uuid>` URL /
`suno.com/s/<shortcode>` short code), and 8 GitHub Action inputs
including tag filtering and duration filtering. Stack: pnpm
monorepo + Next.js 15.1 on Vercel Edge + Valibot schemas + an
SVG renderer that uses `<foreignObject>` + inline HTML `<span>`
for CJK-safe text shaping with CSS `@keyframes` animations
driving the equalizer bars.

**Impact:** GitHub Marketplace Action for Suno music cards — CJK-safe animated SVG with 12 design combinations, parses three ID formats, shares a Valibot kernel with SunoStats analytics, extracted from monorepo as a clean MIT-licensed release.

**Problem:** Suno AI creators have no graceful way to surface their music
catalog on the surfaces where developer audiences actually
congregate: GitHub READMEs, personal portfolio sites, technical
blog footers, project documentation. Three escape hatches all
fail: (1) link out to suno.com — loses the visual hook and
breaks if Suno changes URL shapes; (2) screenshot the player —
stale the moment you publish a new song; (3) hand-edit
Markdown — fine for one song, completely unmanageable beyond
three. Worse: many Suno creators are bilingual or multilingual
and produce songs with CJK (Chinese/Japanese/Korean) titles,
which pure SVG `<text>` doesn't shape properly because SVG
text layout never properly inherited the rich text-shaping
rules of HTML. The gap that needs filling: a service that
renders animated, visually-rich Suno music cards as embeddable
SVG, handles CJK text correctly, supports both zero-setup
single embeds and CI-driven auto-syncing card grids, and
works in both directions (browse-and-build via a hosted UI,
or fully programmatic via a GitHub Action).

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. (1) **Developer-musicians** using
Suno AI who want their GitHub profile README, portfolio site,
or project documentation to display a live, auto-updating
catalog of their music — including CJK-named tracks. (2)
**Music creators** more broadly who maintain a GitHub-shaped
public web presence (devrel folks, indie-game developers,
bedroom electronic producers with side-projects, AI
researchers releasing music under handles) and want a polished
visual surface for their Suno output. (3) **Engineers
studying the project as a worked example** of (a) extracting
a public clean repo from a larger internal monorepo,
(b) sharing a single Valibot-validated parser kernel across
two independent products (SunoStats + this), (c) the
SVG `<foreignObject>` + CSS-keyframes technique for animated,
CJK-safe SVG that pure SVG can't render.

**Product logic:** Two complementary usage paths. **Path 1 — Direct Embed (zero
setup).** A user pastes one Markdown line referencing the
hosted card endpoint:
`[![](https://github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app/api/card?id=YOUR_SONG_ID)](https://suno.com/song/YOUR_SONG_ID)`.
The `id` parameter accepts **three normalized input formats**:
a raw UUID, a full `suno.com/song/<uuid>` URL, or a
`suno.com/s/<shortcode>` short code — the parser canonicalises
all three into a single internal ID. The hosted
[`/builder`](https://github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app/builder)
visual editor lets users preview the 12 base card
combinations (2 layouts × 2 presets × 3 themes), toggle 11
element flags, override individual colors with hex codes, and
copy out the resulting embed URL. **Path 2 — GitHub Action
Auto-Sync.** A user adds `<!-- SUNO-CARDS:START -->` and
`<!-- SUNO-CARDS:END -->` markers to their README, then drops
a workflow file at `.github/workflows/suno-cards.yml` with
`uses: ChanMeng666/github-readme-suno-cards@v0.1` and a `cron`
trigger (typically every 6 hours). The Action takes any of
**8 inputs** — `handle` (auto-discovery of all public songs
for a Suno creator), `manifest_path` (YAML file listing
explicit song IDs), `song_ids` (CSV alternative),
`sort` (`created_at` / `play_count` / `upvote_count` /
`name`), `max` (1–20), `include_tags`, `exclude_tags`,
and `min_duration` / `max_duration` filters — fetches the
matched songs, sorts them, and surgically replaces only the
content between the markers in the README. A companion
`EndBug/add-and-commit@v9` step commits the diff. The two
paths share the same parser + renderer so the cards look
identical regardless of origin path.

**Technical approach:** **pnpm monorepo** with three top-level workspaces: `apps/`
(the Next.js 15.1 frontend), `packages/` (the two reusable
kernels), and `action/` (the GitHub Marketplace Action). The
two reusable packages are the architecturally important ones.
**`@suno-cards/parser`** is a Valibot-validated parser for
Suno's public oEmbed-style API surface
(`studio-api-prod.suno.com`) — anonymous, CORS-enabled,
public-by-design endpoints that Suno advertises via the W3C
oEmbed protocol; no scraping, no auth, no rate-limit
landmines, no headless browsers. **Critical architectural
decision**: the same `@suno-cards/parser` package is **shared
with Chan's other Suno project, SunoStats** — Chan's separate
analytics SaaS for Suno creators
([sunostats.chanmeng.org](https://sunostats.chanmeng.org/)).
Extracting the parser as a workspace package means a single
Valibot schema definition + a single typed-error hierarchy
(`SunoNotFoundError`, `SunoNetworkError`, `SunoSchemaError`,
etc.) underwrites both products — fix a Suno API drift once,
both products benefit. **`@suno-cards/render`** is the pure-
function SVG rendering engine — deterministic, cacheable,
and platform-independent (Vercel Edge can serve it, the
GitHub Action's Node 20 runner can also call it locally).
**The defining rendering technique**: SVG `<foreignObject>`
carries inline HTML `<span>` elements so CJK text shapes
correctly (SVG `<text>` doesn't), with **CSS `@keyframes`
animations** applied to those spans to drive the **animated
equalizer bars** below the song info — a hybrid layout that
pure SVG can't produce cleanly because SVG's animation
primitives can't compose with proper rich-text shaping. The
equalizer animation runs even inside GitHub's Camo proxy
(which strips most interactivity) because CSS keyframes
survive the SVG sanitization pass. **Two-level caching**:
Vercel Data Cache upstream (3600s TTL for single-card
requests) and HTTP `Cache-Control` headers downstream,
letting GitHub's Camo proxy manage the SVG distribution
layer transparently. **GitHub Action build**:
`action/build.ts` runs an esbuild bundle that emits a
`dist/index.js` (Node 20 runtime) plus the `action.yml`
manifest with composite action metadata (icon: `music`,
color: `purple`) — committed alongside the source so the
Action consumes the bundled artifact directly, no install
step needed at workflow runtime. **Quality**: Biome 1.9
for lint + format, Vitest tests inside `action/test/`, CI
via GitHub Actions workflow.

**Outcomes:** Live production service at
[github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app](https://github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app)
with `/gallery` and `/builder` routes. The current public
repository is the **clean-extract** version — first commit
`73f4fa8` *"feat: initial commit — github-readme-suno-cards
v0.1"* on 2026-04-13, immediately followed by `295756e`
*"chore: clean remaining internal reference"* (the
post-extraction sanitization sweep). The actual product is
older inside the private origin monorepo where the parser
was first developed; the v0.1 extraction was the moment Chan
cleaned it up and shipped it as a focused MIT-licensed
open-source release. Continuing iteration through 2026-04-21
(latest commit `2f894c9`). **The shared parser kernel with
SunoStats** (Chan's separate Suno analytics SaaS at
[sunostats.chanmeng.org](https://sunostats.chanmeng.org)) is
the architecturally important outcome: rather than two
independent Suno API integrations drifting apart over time,
the two products are pinned to a single Valibot contract
with a single typed-error hierarchy. **Roadmap**: waveform
layout variants, lyrics-excerpt blocks, playlist cards,
PNG export via `@resvg/resvg` for users who can't embed
SVG directly.

**My role:** Sole author across both the original private monorepo and
the public v0.1 extraction. Designed the pnpm monorepo
split with the parser + renderer extracted as reusable
workspace packages; wrote the Valibot schemas against
Suno's oEmbed API contract; designed the typed Suno error
hierarchy; engineered the `<foreignObject>` + CSS-keyframes
rendering technique that handles CJK text + animated
equalizers in a single SVG; built the GitHub Marketplace
Action with esbuild-bundled dist; designed the 8-input
Action surface with three id-format normalization;
designed the `<!-- SUNO-CARDS:START/END -->` marker-based
surgical README replacement; built the visual `/builder`
UI with live preview over all 12 base combinations + 11
toggle flags + hex color overrides; executed the
2026-04-13 public-extract release sequence (`73f4fa8` +
`295756e`); coordinates parser updates across both this
project and the SunoStats sibling repo.
### echook (Echo + Hook) — AI-operated hooks plugin for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI — install, configure, and use with natural language only; grew from audio notifications into a context-window status line and richer session telemetry
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-07-04]
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/echook
- **Tech stack:** Python 3.6+, Claude Code Plugin SDK, Cursor IDE 3.2.16+ third-party hooks, Codex CLI hooks API, hooks.json native matcher routing, NDJSON structured logging (schema audio-hooks.v1), ElevenLabs TTS API, Slack / Discord / Teams / ntfy webhooks, PowerShell MediaPlayer (Windows), afplay (macOS), mpg123 / ffplay / paplay / aplay (Linux auto-detect), GitHub Actions CI (Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9/3.12/3.13), Remotion 4 (promo video), React 19 (promo video), Suno (background music generation)

**Summary:** AI-operated audio notification system for Claude Code, Cursor IDE, and
Codex CLI. The defining product idea: the **human never learns the
tool**. The user talks to their AI agent in plain English ("install
audio-hooks", "switch to chimes", "snooze for 30 minutes", "send alerts
to my Slack"), and the agent runs every shell command, reads every JSON
output, and reports back. Install reduces to **1 shell command + 2
natural-language prompts + 1 slash command = 4 things**; everything
after that is conversation. Ships 26 hook events, 2 audio themes,
rate-limit alerts (5h/7d quota windows), webhooks (Slack/Discord/Teams/
ntfy), TTS that speaks Claude's actual reply, and a context-window
status line. 63 GitHub stars, 42 releases, 139 unittest cases on
Ubuntu/Windows/macOS × Python 3.9–3.13. A sister repo,
**echook-promo-video**, ships the 88-second 1080p product film built
entirely in code (Remotion + Claude Code + ElevenLabs + Suno).
Carved out as Chan's preserved IP in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter (May 2026).

**Impact:** Audio-feedback hook system for Claude Code / Cursor / Codex — 26 hook events, 2 themes, context-window status line with quota bars, 42 releases, 139 tests on triple-platform CI, plus a Remotion-built promo video shipped entirely in code.

**Problem:** Developers running long-form AI coding workflows on Claude Code,
Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI have no way to *hear* what their agent
is doing — they alt-tab to check progress, miss rate-limit
warnings, and only discover stuck sessions on visual inspection.
Bolt-on notification tools exist but they all carry the same
anti-pattern: **the human is downstream of the CLI**. The user
has to read docs, memorise flags, copy commands, and re-learn the
same surface every time it changes. That model collapses the
moment a serious user starts rotating between Claude Code, Cursor,
and Codex — three editors, three configuration languages, three
sets of muscle memory. The mismatch isn't audio; it's the
contract between humans, AI agents, and developer tooling.

**Audience:** Software engineers operating Claude Code / Cursor IDE / Codex CLI
at the "AI-first" level — comfortable telling their coding agent
to install, theme, snooze, webhook, or reconfigure infrastructure
in plain English rather than memorising subcommands. Also: power
users debugging long-running workflows where audio cues are faster
than visual polling; teams rolling out shared notification
defaults across multiple developers; researchers building on the
AI-first plugin contract as a reference implementation.

**Product logic:** echook inverts the relationship: **the human is upstream of
Claude Code, the AI agent learns the tool on the human's behalf.**
A one-line install prompt — *"install the audio-hooks plugin from
github.com/ChanMeng666/echook"* — kicks off the entire flow. Claude
Code runs `claude plugin marketplace add` and `claude plugin
install`, the user types `/reload-plugins` exactly once (the only
manual slash command in the entire lifetime of the tool, because
Claude Code's reload has no CLI equivalent), and from that moment
forward every interaction is conversation. *"Switch to chimes."*
*"Snooze for 8 hours."* *"Send alerts to my Slack at this webhook
URL and test it."* *"Disable everything except stop and
permission_request."* The `/audio-hooks` skill parses intent,
invokes the right `audio-hooks` CLI subcommand, reads its JSON
output, and narrates the result. The user memorises **nothing**.
Cross-editor parity is part of the same contract: every prompt
works identically on Cursor's agent and on Codex CLI because they
all drive the same Python binary against the same JSON API.

**Business logic:** Open source under MIT — no paywall, no premium tier, no telemetry.
The business value is **build-in-public credibility**: echook is
the demonstration artifact for Chan's AI-first product philosophy
and shows up in every Engram-cohort interview, every recruiter
conversation, and the public README of every downstream client
project. The 42-release cadence, the cross-platform CI matrix,
and the 139-test bridge-contract suite make it visible production
engineering — not a weekend script. The Remotion promo video
sister repo extends the same proof: it shows Chan can ship not
just the product but the marketing artifact for the product, end
to end, programmatically, with zero Adobe-suite licensing or
third-party video production overhead.

**Technical approach:** AI-first design at every layer. The `audio-hooks` CLI ships a
stable JSON-only API with **27 subcommands, no prompts, no
spinners**, versioned NDJSON event logging (schema
`audio-hooks.v1`, 5 MB rotation × 3 files), and a **stable error
code enum** (`AUDIO_FILE_MISSING`, `WEBHOOK_TIMEOUT`,
`DUAL_INSTALL_DETECTED`, etc.) where each error carries a
`suggested_command` field so an agent can recover autonomously
without screen-scraping. Hook coverage is **26 events** spanning
session start/end, tool execution, permission requests, file
changes, worktree creation, elicitation, and rate-limit warnings
(5-hour and 7-day quota windows with configurable 80%/95%
thresholds; each `(window, threshold, resets_at)` fires exactly
once via debounce markers). Two audio themes: ElevenLabs
**Jessica** voice or modern UI chimes; rebuilds are declarative
via `config/audio_manifest.json` + the ElevenLabs generator.
Webhooks fan out to Slack, Discord, Teams, and ntfy via
fire-and-forget subprocess with a versioned
`audio-hooks.webhook.v1` payload — they never block the hook.
The status line ships **10 customisable segments** including
color-coded context-window and API-quota bars with absolute
token counts (e.g. `Context: 83% (166K/200K)`) so users see the
"agent dumb zone" before they enter it. Cross-editor architecture
is the deepest piece: canonical sources live in `/hooks/`,
`/bin/`, `/audio/`, `/cursor-hooks/`, `/codex-hooks/`, and
`build-plugin.sh` syncs them into the Claude Code plugin layout
— **one source of truth, three editor targets**, with CI
enforcing in-sync via `build-plugin.sh --check`. The Cursor path
leverages Cursor 3.2.16+'s auto-bridge so 99% of users get
Cursor support for free; Cursor-only installs go through a
native `~/.cursor/hooks.json` registration with a
`DUPLICATE_BRIDGE` error guard that aborts if both paths would
be active. The Codex CLI path goes further: install handles
OpenAI's required `[features]\ncodex_hooks = true` feature flag
by **authoring a fresh `~/.codex/config.toml` when none exists**
and emitting machine-readable `next_steps` for the calling AI
agent to add the flag with its Edit tool when one does — we
never round-trip user TOML because that destroys comments and
formatting. **18 canonical events with no Codex equivalent
no-op cleanly under the codex invoker** with a debug NDJSON
event, never blocking the session. The whole bridge contract is
validated by 139 unittest cases across Ubuntu/Windows/macOS ×
Python 3.9/3.12/3.13 in GitHub Actions CI.

**Outcomes:** 63 GitHub stars, 6 forks, **42 releases** shipped, **139 unittest
cases**. The defining release sequence: v5.0 (Jan 2026) shipped
the AI-first redesign — JSON CLI, `/audio-hooks` skill, NDJSON
logging, plugin-native install; v5.2.0 (May 2026) added native
Codex CLI support with +33 bridge-contract tests; v5.2.1 (May
2026) executed the **echook rebrand** — a "door-only" GitHub
rename from `claude-code-audio-hooks` → `echook` (because leading
with "Claude Code" became misleading once native Cursor and
Codex paths shipped) that preserved every existing install via
GitHub's repo redirect, kept the `audio-hooks` CLI name, the
`chanmeng-audio-hooks` marketplace name, and all state
directories unchanged. The Engram Architect Cohort Engagement
Letter (May 9, 2026) explicitly names
*claude-code-audio-hooks* among Chan's preserved IP — formal
recognition by a commercial agentic infrastructure firm that this is
portfolio-grade engineering, not a hobby script. The sister
repo **echook-promo-video** then closed the loop on the
AI-first thesis: an 88.7-second 1920×1080 @ 30fps product film
composed of 9 sections (Intro → Problem → Solution → Features →
Advanced → Demo → Ecosystem → Cross-Platform → CTA) connected
by `@remotion/transitions` (wipe/fade/slide) with a layered
audio mix (Suno-generated BGM with auto volume ducking + 8
ElevenLabs SFX cues + 3 ElevenLabs voiceover tracks) — built
entirely in React + Remotion + AI-generated audio, **zero
Adobe Premiere, zero After Effects, zero human voice actors**.
The whole video pipeline is reproducible: one
`npm run generate-audio` regenerates every voice and effect from
the ElevenLabs API; one `npm run render` produces the MP4.

**My role:** Sole author of both repos. On echook proper: designed the
AI-first contract (JSON API + NDJSON log + stable error codes +
`suggested_command` recovery), wrote every hook event
integration, engineered the three-editor synchronisation
pipeline (canonical → plugin layout sync via
`build-plugin.sh`, with CI enforcement), authored the Codex
TOML-feature-flag bootstrap pattern, and executed the 5.2.1
rebrand without breaking compatibility. On echook-promo-video:
designed the 9-section narrative arc with Claude Code, built
the reusable visual primitives (TypewriterText, Terminal,
SoundWave, GreenGrid, CounterNumber, GlowText), orchestrated
the Remotion `TransitionSeries`, wrote the ElevenLabs
audio-generation script, and tuned the volume-ducking
envelopes so BGM ducks during VO + demo playback. Maintains
both repos today through the 5.x line.
### 2D Portfolio — chanmeng.org — Personal site at chanmeng.org powering an 11-stage autonomous AI newsletter pipeline + IBM watsonx.ai digital twin, reskinned to the Caldera brand and hardened for recruiter-LLM discovery (GEO)
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://chanmeng.org/
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 14 (App Router, Edge Runtime API routes), React 18, TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS (Caldera brand system, light-only), Framer Motion, Three.js + postprocessing (PixelBlast WebGL background), MDX blog (Shiki / rehype-pretty-code, remark + rehype pipeline), Lottie + rough-notation + react-icon-cloud + canvas-confetti (UI polish), IBM watsonx.ai chat API (digital-twin assistant — us-south, IAM-token auth), OpenAI (gpt-4o + gpt-4o-mini — newsletter curation pipeline), Resend, React Email, Vercel Blob, Product Hunt GraphQL API, Cloudflare Pages (@cloudflare/next-on-pages + wrangler), GitHub Actions cron

**Summary:** Personal website at **chanmeng.org** serving three production systems
from a single repository: (1) an animated portfolio + MDX blog
(Caldera brand, light-only, PixelBlast WebGL background);
(2) an **IBM watsonx.ai–powered AI digital twin** that converses with
visitors as Chan's virtual representative; (3) a fully autonomous
**11-stage AI newsletter pipeline** that fetches trending AI products
from Product Hunt every Sunday 20:00 UTC, runs them through a
multi-stage LLM curation chain (extract → adversarial counter-evidence
→ curate → reader critique → self-edit → quality scorecard → leak guard
→ persist memory), and delivers via Resend at Monday 8-9 AM NZ local
time. Deployed on Cloudflare Pages.

**Impact:** Three production systems in one repo: animated portfolio + IBM Watson digital twin + an 11-stage autonomous AI newsletter pipeline that ships every Monday 8 AM NZ time fully unattended since April 2026 — Lighthouse 95+ across all metrics.

**Problem:** A personal site is usually one of two things: a brochure (looks
nice, says nothing) or a CMS (constant maintenance, dies the
moment the author gets busy). Neither answers what an *AI
engineer's* personal site should look like in the era when
frontier models actively rank candidates by their verifiable
public footprint — the case Engram's founder demonstrated by
asking Claude Code to surface architects and being matched to
Chan. The site needs to *be* the proof, not merely list it:
ship live AI infrastructure visitors can interact with, run a
signal-grade publication on autopilot, and stay current without
Chan touching it most weeks.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. **Hiring decision-makers and
founders** (the Engram-style sourcing case): see the site, see
live AI engineering, decide. **Newsletter subscribers** at
chanmeng.org/#newsletter: AI practitioners and AI-curious
operators who want a weekly signal-grade Product Hunt digest
with an opinionated argumentative spine, not a feed. **Frontier
LLMs themselves**: the site is structured (via the sister
`claude-code-compressed-map` tier metadata system on Chan's
GitHub profile + GEO-conscious MDX content) so Claude / GPT /
Gemini can index and rank it accurately when answering "who
builds X?" — a deliberate Generative Engine Optimization
position.

**Product logic:** Visitors land on chanmeng.org, see an animated portfolio
showcase with smooth Framer Motion transitions, and can open
the **IBM Watson digital twin chat** to ask about Chan's 50+
projects or work history in natural language. They can read
the MDX blog, browse curated external signals (AI HOT feed +
Product Hunt feed tabbed UI), or subscribe to the weekly
newsletter via the embedded Resend form. Subscribers receive
an automated **8-9 AM NZ Monday** delivery: a single
opinionated AI digest with a clearly-stated thesis, ~3–5
product recommendations as evidence, a "spotlight" pick, a
deliberate *crack* (counter-evidence section that argues the
opposite of the thesis), and a closing question. Unsubscribe
is one-click via Resend's per-contact template variable.

**Business logic:** Theory of change: **build-in-public is no longer just for
human eyes — it's GEO for the foundation models that now do
the sourcing**. The Engram engagement (Claude Code recommended
Chan to Engram's founder based on her public AI footprint) is
the empirical instance. The personal site is therefore
engineered as the surface where that footprint compounds:
every weekly newsletter becomes a public artefact, every blog
post becomes a indexable position, every interaction with the
digital twin signals depth of project knowledge. The newsletter
specifically builds owned distribution (vs. rented social
reach) — a list of opt-in AI practitioners Chan can reach
independently of platform algorithms.

**Technical approach:** Three intertwined systems in one Next.js 14 repository.

**(1) Portfolio + blog.** Next.js 14 App Router on Cloudflare
Pages via `@cloudflare/next-on-pages` + wrangler. React 18 +
TypeScript 5 + Tailwind + Framer Motion for animations,
reskinned in June 2026 to the **Caldera personal-brand system
(light-only)** with a **PixelBlast WebGL background** (Three.js
+ postprocessing) replacing the earlier SVG grid. MDX blog with
Shiki / rehype-pretty-code for syntax highlighting. **GEO /
recruiter-LLM hardening**: server-rendered JSON-LD structured
data, an FAQ schema block, Open Graph images, and dedicated
**CV JSON-LD + `llms.txt` sidecars served for recruiter LLMs**,
so a sourcing agent can parse Chan's identity, roles, and
projects without scraping prose. Reported Lighthouse 95+ across
all metrics.

**(2) IBM watsonx.ai Digital Twin.** An Edge-runtime chat
interface (`/api/chat` + streaming `/api/chat-stream`) backed by
**IBM watsonx.ai's chat API** (us-south region, IAM-token auth
via `/api/ibm-token`) where the assistant embodies Chan's voice
and project knowledge through a system prompt kept in sync with
the curated `data/resume.tsx` source. Visitors ask about
specific projects or technical skills and receive contextually
accurate first-person responses.

**(3) 11-stage autonomous AI newsletter pipeline.**
Orchestrated by `scripts/ai-newsletter.ts` and triggered every
Sunday 20:00 UTC by a GitHub Actions cron job:

- **Parallel fetch**: Product Hunt GraphQL API (top 30
  trending products by votes, past 7 days) + cross-source
  signals + previous-issue memory (`newsletter-memory.ts`)
- **Bucket** products by vote distribution (top / mid /
  longTail) and warn if no longTail pick is selected
- **Cluster** products against related industry events
- **Stage 1 — extract candidate theses** from the signal
  pool (`gpt-4o-mini`)
- **Stage 2 — adversarial counter-evidence search** for each
  thesis (`gpt-4o-mini`)
- **Stage 3 — full newsletter assembly** (`gpt-4o`): picks
  the thesis with strongest product evidence, uses
  counter-events as the *crack* section, weaves prior-issue
  history into the intro for week-over-week continuity
- **Stage 4 — reader critique** (`gpt-4o`): adversarial
  argument review from a sceptical-reader perspective
- **Stage 5 — self-edit pass** (`gpt-4o`): sharpens the
  weakest sentences identified by the critique
- **Stage 6 — quality scorecard** (`gpt-4o-mini`): grades the
  final newsletter 1–10 across four dimensions
  (thesisSharpness, productEvidence, crackHonesty, proseVoice)
- **Leak guard**: scans output for source-attribution leaks
- **Persist history** for next week's continuity
- **Image upload** to Vercel Blob → **render** via
  @react-email/render → **send** via Resend Broadcasts API
  to the configured segment

Resend custom tracking domain enabled for click/open
analytics under chanmeng.org. Unsubscribe link uses Resend's
per-contact template variable `{{{RESEND_UNSUBSCRIBE_URL}}}`
in broadcast mode; preview sends rewrite it to a real
`/unsubscribe?email=` URL so admin can verify the flow.

**Outcomes:** Live at **chanmeng.org**, deployed on Cloudflare Pages with
full Lighthouse 95+ across metrics. Newsletter system in
production since **April 2026** (`feat: add automated AI
newsletter pipeline with ProductHunt + OpenAI`, 2026-04-03);
upgraded to the multi-stage curation pipeline on
**2026-05-09** (`feat(newsletter): add multi-stage curation
pipeline with cross-source signals`). Resend custom tracking
domain live since 2026-04-25. Weekly delivery cadence is
cron-enforced and operates fully unattended — no manual
intervention between Sunday cron fire and Monday morning
subscriber inbox. The newsletter is cited on Chan's profile
as **"What's Shipping in AI (Weekly AI Digest)"**, signup at
chanmeng.org/#newsletter.

A **June 2026 wave** reskinned the entire site to the Caldera
brand (light-only), swapped the background for a PixelBlast
WebGL shader (Three.js + postprocessing), rebranded the
newsletter emails to Caldera with copy rewritten for
non-technical readers, curated "Things I've built" to 12 with
progressive disclosure, converted site imagery to WebP, and
added the **GEO sidecar layer** (server-rendered structured
data, FAQ schema, CV JSON-LD + `llms.txt`) that operationalises
the recruiter-LLM sourcing thesis.

**My role:** Sole author. Designed the three-system architecture (portfolio
+ digital twin + newsletter) inside a single Next.js codebase,
wrote the entire 11-stage pipeline (`scripts/ai-newsletter.ts`
plus 14 lib modules: fetch-products, fetch-signals,
cluster-events, extract-trends, find-counterevidence,
curate-content, reader-critique, self-edit, quality-score,
leak-guard, product-buckets, newsletter-memory, image-manager,
setup-tracking-domain), built the React Email templates,
configured the GitHub Actions cron, set up Resend custom
tracking domain + Vercel Blob image hosting, and migrated the
whole site to Cloudflare Pages. Maintains the system as both
a publication and a continuous demonstration of agent-pipeline
engineering.
### AI Programming Education Platform (programming.chanmeng.org) — Bilingual Docusaurus teaching platform — three years of AI/programming cohorts running (four cohort versions side-by-side) with an in-course RAG AI assistant on Cloudflare Workers (Llama 3.1 8B + Vectorize + KV), Notion-backed Capstone voting, and Typst-generated PDF handouts; the active home for the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the clearest evidence of Chan as an AI educator/evangelist, not only a builder
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://programming.chanmeng.org/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/ai-programming-teaching-project
- **Tech stack:** Docusaurus 3.8.1, @docusaurus/faster, React 18, MDX, Cloudflare Workers, Workers AI (Llama 3.1 8B Instruct), Cloudflare Vectorize, BGE Base EN v1.5 (768-dim embeddings), Cloudflare KV, Cloudflare Pages, Notion API, Algolia DocSearch (contextual), Typst, Mermaid (dual-view), i18next (EN + 简体中文), Server-Sent Events

**Summary:** Production teaching platform at **programming.chanmeng.org** hosting
four cohort versions side-by-side (2024-winter, 2025-summer,
2026-her-waka, 2026-technest) through Docusaurus versioned docs.
Backed by an **in-course RAG AI assistant** running on Cloudflare
Workers AI (Llama 3.1 8B Instruct), with ~4,800 indexed document
chunks served from Cloudflare Vectorize via BGE Base EN v1.5
embeddings and conversation memory in Cloudflare KV. Bilingual
EN + 简体中文 throughout, with Typst-generated downloadable PDF
handouts, Notion-backed Capstone voting, Mermaid dual-view
diagrams, and Algolia contextual search. 199 of 208 commits solo
(95.7%). Actively maintained — new course recordings shipped
weekly through the 2026 TechNest cohort.

**Impact:** Public teaching platform running 4 coexisting AI-instructor cohort versions (Winter 2024 · Summer 2025 · HER WAKA 2026 · TechNest 2026) with an in-course RAG assistant on Cloudflare Workers indexing ~4,800 bilingual document chunks across the entire course corpus.

**Problem:** AI-programming cohort programmes typically die between cohorts:
the lecture deck is rebuilt from scratch each round, students
from the previous cohort lose access to their materials, and
the "AI tutor" experience — when it exists at all — is a
ChatGPT tab the student opens separately, with no awareness of
which cohort's curriculum they're studying. Meanwhile, the
instructor has no way to make older cohorts permanently
retrievable without disturbing the live one. The result: every
cohort feels like a one-shot, course content compounds nowhere,
and the AI-assisted-development experience is itself
unassisted by AI.

**Audience:** Three concentric layers. **Active cohort students** — currently
the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the 2026 academyEX Her Waka
programme — who consume video recordings, lab guides,
downloadable PDF handouts, and ask the in-site AI tutor
version-aware questions. **Past cohort alumni** (2024-winter,
2025-summer, Forward with Her 她行 cohorts) who retain stable
URLs to their original materials. **Public learners and
prospective employers** who hit programming.chanmeng.org from
the LinkedIn profile and use it as proof that Chan ships
production-grade EdTech with native AI integration, not just
slides.

**Product logic:** Visitors land on programming.chanmeng.org and the docs default
to the **2026 TechNest** version (the active cohort), but the
version switcher exposes 2026-her-waka, 2025-summer, and
2024-winter side-by-side — alumni of any cohort hit a stable
permalink. Each version ships its own Video Tutorials sidebar
section, weekly lab guides as both web docs and downloadable
Typst-generated PDFs, and a per-version Mermaid dual-view
diagram (the same diagram in two presentations so visual and
textual learners both succeed). A floating **AI Chat Assistant**
widget answers questions in natural language by retrieving the
current cohort's content first (version-aware retrieval), then
falling back to other cohorts and the blog. Capstone 2026 has
its own public showcase page where invited guests vote on
teams' demos via a Notion-backed voting flow.

**Business logic:** Theory of change: a single teaching platform that compounds
cohort-over-cohort instead of resetting each round. Once
2024-winter cohort materials are versioned in, they stay
accessible forever at the same URL; the next cohort's
materials sit alongside them without conflict. The AI tutor
means a student writing code at midnight gets context-aware
help against their own cohort's syllabus, not generic ChatGPT
answers. For Chan personally: the platform demonstrates
production-grade EdTech engineering (Cloudflare Workers AI +
Vectorize + i18n + versioned docs + PDF pipeline) while doing
actual paid teaching work — every commit is both portfolio
artefact and student value.

**Technical approach:** Built on **Docusaurus 3.8.1** with the experimental
`@docusaurus/faster` build pipeline. Four cohort versions
coexist through Docusaurus's versioned-docs feature — each
version is its own self-contained doc tree with its own
sidebar, blog feed, and tag taxonomy. Bilingual i18n is
end-to-end: EN primary, 简体中文 secondary, with Algolia
DocSearch configured for **contextual** locale + version
filtering so a Mandarin-speaking 2026 TechNest student gets
2026 TechNest results in Chinese by default.

**RAG AI assistant on Cloudflare Workers AI.** A separate
Cloudflare Worker (`worker/src/index.ts`) handles `POST /api/chat`:
- Worker calls **Cloudflare Vectorize** with the user query
  embedded via **BGE Base EN v1.5** (768-dim)
- Vectorize returns the top relevant chunks from ~4,800
  MDX-derived document embeddings (all four cohort versions
  + blog, indexed with **stable hash chunk IDs** so re-indexing
  is idempotent and version-aware retrieval works)
- Worker passes the context window to **Workers AI Llama 3.1
  8B Instruct** which generates the response
- Response streams back to the in-site ChatWidget via
  **Server-Sent Events** for incremental rendering
- Per-session conversation history persists in **Cloudflare KV**
- Secure seeding flow so the embedding index can be
  rebuilt on demand without exposing data
- Vector retrieval is **version-aware**: a 2026-technest
  student gets 2026-technest results by default, with
  older cohorts available on explicit ask

**Typst PDF handout pipeline.** The same Typst skill taught
in Week 8 of the curriculum is the build tool that produces
the printable curriculum outline, capstone spec, and
per-week lab guides — the pedagogical loop is intentional:
students see in production the artefact they're being
taught to build. **Mermaid dual-view component** renders
every diagram in two synchronised presentations
(rendered + source) so both visual and textual learners
succeed.

**Capstone classroom infrastructure.** Cloudflare Worker
backend proxies writes into Notion so the cohort owners
moderate inside the tool they already use. The Capstone 2026
public showcase page lists every team's demo with live
Notion-backed voting open to invited guests — also bilingual.

Deployed end-to-end on Cloudflare's edge: **Cloudflare Pages**
for the static Docusaurus build, **Cloudflare Workers** for
the chat backend, **Cloudflare Vectorize** for the vector
index, **Cloudflare KV** for sessions.

**Outcomes:** Live at **programming.chanmeng.org**, in active use as the
official platform for the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the 2026
academyEX Her Waka programme. **199 of 208 commits (95.7%)
solo across Chan's two GitHub identities**; remainder is
AI auto-review + Dependabot. Four cohort versions running
