simultaneously without interfering with each other. RAG
index covers all four cohort versions + the blog as of
2026-05-02; version-aware retrieval and stable hash chunk
IDs landed the same day. New course recordings continue to
ship weekly — through June 2026 the TechNest cohort added
Week 5 ("Add a Blog System"), Week 6 ("Build a Slack App" +
a Claude Code skills office hour), and Week 7 recordings,
plus a LinkedIn recommendation section and backfilled
简体中文 for Weeks 3-5. DeepWiki provides an external AI
doc-Q&A surface at
deepwiki.com/ChanMeng666/ai-programming-teaching-project.

**My role:** Sole engineer and sole author. Designed and built the entire
platform end-to-end: Docusaurus configuration, the versioned
docs taxonomy across four cohorts, every cohort's curriculum
content, the Cloudflare Worker RAG backend (worker entry,
chat handler, RAG module, Vectorize seeding, KV session
store), the BGE-Base embeddings ingestion pipeline, the
Typst PDF handout build, the Mermaid dual-view component,
the Notion-Worker proxy for Capstone voting, the Algolia
contextual-search configuration, and the bilingual i18n
across every surface (including Mermaid toolbar labels and
the localised `目录` TOC label). Migrated production from
Vercel to Cloudflare Pages in 2026-04. Continues to ship
the current cohort's lecture recordings and lab content
each week.
### Chinese Redefine 汉语新解 — AI Word Redefinition — 汉语新解 — a full-stack AI app that reinterprets everyday Chinese vocabulary with sharp, humorous, philosophically-loaded takes (productizing Li Jigang's 李继刚 viral prompt) via Google Gemini, exports shareable SVG cards, gated by a two-layer rate-limit quota system, deployed on Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext. Low on raw technical difficulty, high on cultural/philosophical depth
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://chinese-redefine.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/chinese-redefine
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15 (App Router), React 18, TypeScript 5, Tailwind, shadcn/ui, Framer Motion, Google Gemini API, Neon Auth, Neon Postgres (serverless), Drizzle ORM, Resend (OTP email), @opennextjs/cloudflare, Cloudflare Workers, Wrangler

**Summary:** Full-stack AI application that reinterprets traditional Chinese
vocabulary through Google Gemini and exports the results as
shareable, vermillion-seal-themed SVG cards. Production deployment
includes user authentication (Neon Auth, migrated from self-hosted
Better Auth), Neon Postgres + Drizzle ORM for cards and quota
persistence, a two-tier rate-limit system (per-user free/pro quotas
plus a global daily/monthly circuit breaker), public gallery,
"My Cards" private collection, Resend-powered OTP email
verification, and Cloudflare Workers deployment via
`@opennextjs/cloudflare`. Modern Chinese Ink aesthetic UI
redesigned March 2026.

**Impact:** Full-stack AI app reinterpreting traditional Chinese vocabulary through Google Gemini — exports shareable SVG cards, gated by a two-layer rate-limit quota system, ~95% solo across 60 commits, deployed on Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext.

**Problem:** Generic LLM chat windows are bad at *creative cultural
reinterpretation*: ask Gemini directly to "redefine 婚姻" and
you get a paragraph of generic prose with no shape, no
shareability, no persistence, and no protection against
adversarial users burning through API quota. The
Chinese-language ecosystem in particular needs a tool that
treats vocabulary as a *cultural object* — produces a result
worth screenshotting, saves it for the user to revisit, and
lets the curated best ones surface to a public gallery — all
while staying within a sustainable cost envelope for a solo
operator.

**Audience:** Three groups. **Chinese language learners and cultural
researchers** who want fresh perspectives on traditional
vocabulary — the core product use case. **Content creators
and designers** who want a one-tap path from word → AI
interpretation → exportable shareable visual card. **Engineering
portfolio audiences** (recruiters, founders, the Engram-style
Claude-Code-sourcing case) who use the project as proof that
Chan ships full-stack AI applications end-to-end — not just
prompts, but the auth + DB + quota + deployment scaffolding
around them.

**Product logic:** A user lands on the site, types a Chinese word in the input
form, and sees a real-time Gemini-generated reinterpretation
rendered as a stylised card. They can preview the SVG version,
download it as a high-resolution shareable image, or — once
signed in via OAuth or email-OTP — save it to their personal
"My Cards" collection. The public **Gallery** surfaces curated
community cards. A live **Quota Display** widget shows the
user's remaining daily and monthly allowance. Authentication
uses Neon Auth's pre-built flows (login, register, email
verification via Resend-sent OTP, forgot-password, OAuth
callback).

**Business logic:** Theory of design for a solo-operator AI product: build the
capacity-control layer *into the application* from the start.
The two-layer quota system — per-user tier (free: 1/min ·
3/day · 30/month; higher tier: 5/min · 30/day · 300/month)
plus a **global circuit breaker** (150 generations/day ·
1,500/month across all users) — means Gemini capacity is
bounded structurally, not by trust. The product itself is
free to play with so the gallery accumulates real content;
higher tiers exist as the capacity surface for power users;
the global cap prevents a single bad actor (or a viral moment)
from triggering runaway capacity drain. The architecture is
the access model.

**Technical approach:** **Next.js 15 App Router** with grouped routes:
`(auth)` — login, register, verify-email (OTP input),
forgot-password, debug; `(protected)` — account / cards (the
signed-in dashboard); top-level `gallery` (public). API routes
under `app/api/`: `generate` (Gemini call), `cards`,
`gallery`, `auth`, `debug`. UI built with **shadcn/ui** atop
Radix primitives + **Framer Motion** animations + a
deliberate **Modern Chinese Ink aesthetic** (vermillion seal
logo, ink-wash typography, redesigned end-to-end March 2026).

**Persistence layer**: **Drizzle ORM** over **Neon serverless
Postgres** with three core tables — `cards` (the saved AI
outputs), `userUsage` (per-user daily/monthly counters keyed
by date), `globalUsage` (the circuit-breaker counters).
Drizzle Kit for migrations. CUID2 (`@paralleldrive/cuid2`)
for ID generation — collision-resistant, sortable, shorter
than UUIDs.

**Auth layer**: **Neon Auth**, migrated from a self-hosted
Better Auth implementation in March 2026. The migration
rewrote middleware whitelists (callback URLs for OAuth,
verification-flow query parameters, static assets like
`icon.svg`), formalised the email-OTP flow with explicit
success guidance and resend capability, and switched social
OAuth to absolute callback URLs to work correctly under
Cloudflare Workers' edge-runtime constraints.

**AI layer**: Google **Gemini API** for generation, fronted by
the two-layer rate-limit module in `lib/rate-limit.ts`. Every
generation request: (1) checks user tier limits against
`userUsage`; (2) checks the **global circuit breaker** against
`globalUsage`; (3) only if both pass, calls Gemini; (4) writes
the result to `cards` and increments both counters atomically.

**Email**: **Resend** for transactional OTP delivery during
email verification and password reset.

**Deployment**: **Cloudflare Workers** via
**`@opennextjs/cloudflare`** + **Wrangler** — a non-trivial
migration from the original Vercel deployment, because Workers'
edge runtime requires absolute URLs for OAuth callbacks and
differs from Node-runtime assumptions in subtle ways (every
edge-runtime crash during the migration is in the commit log
as a separate fix).

**Outcomes:** Live at **chinese-redefine.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev**. Solo
development: **60 commits, ~95% Chan-authored** across two
GitHub identities. The Vercel → Cloudflare Workers migration
and the Better Auth → Neon Auth migration were both completed
in a focused March 30, 2026 sprint — the commit log captures
each edge-runtime crash and OAuth callback bug in sequence as
they were fixed. Modern Chinese Ink aesthetic redesign
(vermillion seal logo, ink-wash typography) shipped the same
day. The gallery serves as a living portfolio of AI-generated
Chinese-word reinterpretations.

**My role:** Sole engineer and sole designer. Architected the Next.js App
Router structure, wrote the two-layer rate-limit / quota
engine, designed the three-table Drizzle schema, executed the
Better Auth → Neon Auth migration (including middleware
whitelists, OAuth callback rewriting, OTP verification flow,
and email-passing via URL params to the verify-email page),
executed the Vercel → Cloudflare Workers migration via
`@opennextjs/cloudflare`, built every shadcn/ui component
composition (`CardGenerator`, `CardThumbnail`, `ResultCard`,
`SVGPreview`, `DownloadButton`, `GalleryGrid`, `AuthForm`,
`QuotaDisplay`, `InputForm`, `DeveloperSection`, `Navbar`),
designed and implemented the SVG generation system in
`lib/svgGenerator.ts`, and shipped the Modern Chinese Ink
aesthetic redesign end-to-end.
### Te Pa Tiaki — CSS 3D Tower Defense (Guardians of Aotearoa) — A 3D tower-defense game rendered 100% with CSS 3D transforms — zero Canvas, zero WebGL, zero engine (a hand-built CSS 3D rendering engine) — themed end-to-end on Māori mythology of Aotearoa and inspired by Waitangi Day, with a full Cloudflare Workers + Hono + Neon Postgres backend (auth, leaderboards, cloud saves, anti-cheat)
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-18]
- **URL:** https://towerdefense.chanmeng.org/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/css-tower-defense
- **Tech stack:** CSS 3D Transforms, Vanilla JavaScript (ES6), LESS preprocessor, Gulp 4 build pipeline, Cloudflare Workers, Hono, Neon Postgres, Web Audio API, Howler.js, DOM object pooling

**Summary:** A full **3D isometric tower-defense game** rendered entirely through
CSS 3D transforms on real DOM elements — no `<canvas>`, no WebGL,
no game engine. Every tower, enemy, projectile, particle, and
weather effect is a styled DOM node at a 45-degree isometric
perspective. Themed end-to-end on **Maori mythology and culture of
Aotearoa**: six towers named after Maori weapons and atua (Taiaha,
Mere, Tangaroa, Tohunga, Tawhiri, Mahuika), six enemies from
Maori legend (Kehua, Patupaiarehe, Toa, Wairua, Tipua, Taniwha),
three maps (Te Awa, Te Maunga, Te Moana), three taonga materials
(Pipi, Koiwi, Pounamu). Backed by Cloudflare Workers + Hono +
Neon Postgres for auth, leaderboards, cloud saves (3 slots),
daily challenges, and server-side anti-cheat. 84 commits over
~2 months (Feb–Mar 2026), executed in disciplined P0–P4 phases.

**Impact:** 3D tower-defense game rendered 100% with CSS 3D transforms — zero Canvas, zero WebGL, zero engine — themed end-to-end on Māori mythology of Aotearoa, with full Cloudflare Workers + Hono + Neon Postgres backend. 84 commits / ~99% solo over 2 months.

**Problem:** Two prevailing pictures of "a portfolio game project" are both
wrong: (a) a trivial Canvas + JS toy that lasts one weekend
and looks it; (b) a Unity-shaped engine project that's
impressive but says nothing about web-platform fluency. There
is a third position the portfolio market doesn't routinely
produce: a **production-shaped game shipped end-to-end on raw
web primitives** — DOM + CSS — that proves both deep browser
engineering and design discipline. There is also a fourth
gap: a NZ-based developer's portfolio should *show*
engagement with Aotearoa rather than just claim residence,
and Maori mythology deserves more interactive cultural
representation than it usually gets.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. **Game players** — anyone who
clicks Play Now on towerdefense.chanmeng.org and can play
without an account thanks to fully-supported guest mode.
**Engineering recruiters and architects** evaluating the
portfolio: the game is proof of CSS-engine fluency, vanilla
ES6 architecture discipline, full-stack ops (Workers + Hono +
Neon), and the kind of phased product execution
(P0 → P1 → P2 → P3 → P4) usually only seen in shipped
products. **The Aotearoa cultural audience**: locals, the
Maori community, NZ tech employers — anyone for whom
respectful engagement with te reo and Maori cosmology is a
meaningful signal alongside the technical work.

**Product logic:** Players load the game in-browser, optionally sign in for
cloud progression (or stay in guest mode), pick a difficulty
(Normal / Hard / Expert), choose a map (Te Awa, Te Maunga,
or Te Moana), and play through **10 progressive waves**
defending against six Maori-legend enemy archetypes plus
four variant tiers (Rangatira elite, Pakanga armored, Tere
swift, Mate deadly). Players place towers, gather **taonga**
(treasures: Pipi, Koiwi, Pounamu) dropped by enemies, craft
consumables from six recipes, build XP through a Tech Tree
of 10 upgrades, attempt **daily challenges** drawn from 20+
templates across six categories, and chase rank on
**leaderboards** (global and per-challenge). Endless mode
keeps wave 11+ available after victory. Server-side
anti-cheat validates submitted scores against expected
ranges. Cloud saves persist across 3 slots; guest mode keeps
progress in localStorage.

**Business logic:** Theory of differentiation for a non-commercial portfolio
artefact: be **specifically uncopyable**. A Canvas tower
defense is a five-page tutorial; a CSS-3D tower defense is
a multi-month engineering bet that signals *exactly* what
the candidate can do. Pair that with cultural engagement
most portfolios don't attempt — six towers and six enemies
whose names and roles draw on te reo Maori — and the
project becomes a single-sentence portfolio differentiator
in NZ-relevant interviews. The free, guest-playable web
deployment maximises the surface area: anyone reading the
portfolio can click Play and *experience* the engineering,
not just read about it.

**Technical approach:** **Rendering — 100% CSS 3D.** Every tower, enemy, projectile,
explosion, particle, weather effect is a real DOM element.
The world is a 45-degree isometric scene built with
`transform-style: preserve-3d` and 1200px CSS perspective.
Towers and enemies are constructed from `clip-path`,
`border-radius`, and pseudo-elements — *no emoji, no
images*. Visual style is comic / pop-art: halftone dot
patterns, hard-offset pop shadows, thick comic borders, a
warm `#FFF8F0` palette. **DOM object pooling** via
`Pool.acquire()` / `Pool.release()` recycles elements so
the scene stays at 60 FPS through dense wave combat.
**Dynamic theming** via CSS custom properties driven from
JS produces day/night, weather, and seasonal palette
transitions — same DOM, different CSS variables.

**Game logic — vanilla ES6.** No framework, no engine. The
codebase is organised as IIFE modules communicating
through an event-driven bus. Core systems include the
wave director, enemy AI, tower targeting, projectile
physics, the combo system (chain kills within 1.5s),
critical-hit probability, the mana ability system with
cooldowns, the Terraria-inspired **reforging** engine
(Common → Uncommon → Rare → Epic → Legendary prefixes),
and **tower synergies** that activate when adjacent towers
combine (Freeze Lightning, Fire Blast, Blessing, Array).

**Maori cultural integration is structural, not decorative.**
Towers carry the abilities their te reo names imply:
Tangaroa (ocean atua) slows enemies with ice, Mahuika (fire
goddess) deals damage-over-time, Tawhiri (storm god) chains
lightning. The Taniwha boss has three signature skills —
Karanga (call / summon minions), Kaitiaki (guardian /
shield), Te Riri (anger / enrage). Maps are place-words:
Te Awa (the river), Te Maunga (the mountain), Te Moana
(the ocean). Materials are taonga (treasures): Pipi
(shellfish), Koiwi (bone), Pounamu (greenstone). The
naming isn't surface skin — each mechanic is selected to
match the mythological meaning.

**Cloud backend.** Cloudflare Workers running the **Hono**
web framework, backed by **Neon serverless Postgres**.
Endpoints cover auth, leaderboards (global + per-challenge),
cloud saves (3 slots per user), stats tracking, and
daily-challenge rotation. **Server-side score validation**
rejects submissions outside expected ranges to defend the
leaderboard against cheating. **Guest mode is a
first-class path** — the entire game works without an
account; cloud sync activates the moment a player signs in.

**Audio.** **Web Audio API** synth generates SFX in real
time (firing, impact, death, level-up); **Howler.js**
handles music and longer samples. Music is *dynamic* — the
soundtrack adapts to game state (menu, playing, boss,
victory, defeat).

**Build pipeline.** **LESS** preprocessor for CSS modules,
**Gulp 4** for the build/watch pipeline, deployment via
Wrangler to Cloudflare Workers with the static assets
served from the same edge.

**Outcomes:** Live at **towerdefense.chanmeng.org**. **84 commits across
~2 months** (2026-02-06 → 2026-03-30), ~99% Chan-authored
across two GitHub identities. Execution followed an
explicit **P0 → P4 phase plan** visible in the commit log:
P0 (game feel — damage numbers, death bursts, firing
animations, wave transitions), P1 (onboarding & UX —
tutorial with spotlight cutout, hints, cooldown UI),
P2 (retention — expanded achievements, leaderboard filters,
celebrations), P3-P4 (deep systems — mana abilities, dynamic
music, endless mode). Plus the full content build:
synergies, new enemies, multi-map, daily-challenge overhaul,
cloud backend integration. Anti-cheat is in production and
actively rejecting out-of-range submissions. The 3D
isometric CSS rendering holds 60 FPS through dense
Wave-10 combat thanks to object pooling.

**My role:** Sole designer, sole engineer, sole audio architect. Designed
the CSS-3D rendering technique end-to-end (perspective,
preserve-3d, the comic/pop-art visual language, the DOM
object pool), wrote every game system (waves, AI,
physics, towers, enemies, synergies, reforging, combo,
mana, achievements, daily challenges, tech tree, crafting,
inventory), shipped the entire **Maori cultural design** —
choosing which te reo name maps to which mechanic so
meaning and gameplay reinforce each other — built the
Cloudflare Workers + Hono + Neon backend including
server-side anti-cheat validation, composed the dynamic
music state machine and the Web Audio synth effects,
wrote the Gulp + LESS build pipeline, and authored the
4-step interactive tutorial with spotlight overlays.
### Vitex — AI Career Agent (vitex.org.nz) — Agent-ready AI career agent (vitex.org.nz) — turns a job description + free-text background into a tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + cover letter in ~30s via an 8-step pipeline. The whole product is reachable over an authenticated public HTTP API, plus a vitex-cli and a stdio MCP server (Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor); billing is outcome-based (charged only when a resume is successfully produced). Vercel AI SDK + GPT-4o + Typst local compile (<100ms), on a DigitalOcean VPS via Docker + Traefik + GitHub Actions
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://www.vitex.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/easy-resume
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript 5, Vercel AI SDK v6, @ai-sdk/openai, OpenAI GPT-4o, Zod schemas, Typst (local binary), Neon Postgres, Drizzle ORM, Upstash Redis, Stack Auth, Stripe, Cloudinary, shadcn/ui + Neobrutalism, Framer Motion, Public v1 HTTP API (OpenAPI spec + curl playbook in skill.md), vitex-cli (thin API client, bin `vitex`), MCP server (`vitex mcp` via npx, stdio — Claude Desktop/Code, Cursor), Docker, Traefik, DigitalOcean VPS, GitHub Actions CI/CD

**Summary:** AI Career Agent live at **vitex.org.nz**. A user pastes a job
description, describes their background in free text, and an
**8-step AI pipeline** (JD parsing → background parsing → match
analysis → resume tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter →
document generation → server-side PDF compilation) streams the
result into a tailored, ATS-optimized resume PDF + matching
cover letter in ~30 seconds. Typst compiles PDFs locally in
under 100 ms across 7 AI-auto-selected professional templates —
no external document-generation API. The product is
**agent-ready**: every capability is reachable over an
authenticated **public v1 HTTP API** (the web UI and API share
one pipeline core), and a thin **vitex-cli** plus a stdio **MCP
server** (`vitex mcp`) let terminals and coding agents (Claude
Desktop/Code, Cursor) drive the whole product without a browser.
Billing is **outcome-based** — credits are charged only when a
resume is successfully produced (failures are free), idempotently.
Natural-language refinement applies targeted free edits instead
of full regenerations. Production runs as a Docker container on a
**DigitalOcean VPS** behind **Traefik** (reverse proxy + TLS),
shipped continuously by **GitHub Actions**.

**Impact:** Agent-ready AI career agent — an 8-step pipeline turns a JD + free-text background into a tailored ATS-optimised resume + cover letter in ~30s, usable over a public HTTP API, a CLI, and an MCP server; outcome-based billing (only on success), Typst PDF compile <100ms.

**Problem:** Most AI resume tools are one of two failure modes: (a) a
thin GPT wrapper that hands you a paragraph and calls it a
resume — no PDF, no ATS-aware structure, no template
intelligence; or (b) a SaaS that lets you fill out a form
and burns 90 seconds spinning a hosted Chromium to render
the same template every other user gets. Both miss the
actual job: the candidate needs the resume *tailored to the
specific JD they're applying to*, the document needs to
survive ATS parsing, the visual template needs to match the
target industry/role, and the round-trip needs to feel
instant. None of those are true of "paste your CV into
ChatGPT."

**Audience:** Three groups. **Job seekers in real applications** —
especially in NZ / Australia / Asia-Pacific markets where
Chan personally needed the tool and where vitex.org.nz lives
(the NZ domain is deliberate). **Career-change candidates**
who need every application tailored — the people for whom
re-writing a resume per JD is the actual bottleneck.
**AI-engineering portfolio reviewers** evaluating Chan's
ability to ship a production-grade multi-step AI agent with
real users, real billing, and real DevOps — not a demo.

**Product logic:** User flow is intentionally short: (1) **Homepage** — paste
a job description + describe your professional background
in free text. No CV upload, no form filling, no field-by-field
data entry. (2) **Generate** — click run; the 7-step AI
pipeline streams progress via SSE, the user watches each
stage complete (parsing JD → parsing background → matching
→ tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter → Typst compile),
and arrives at the **Editor** view with the PDF preview,
ATS score with actionable feedback, the matching cover
letter, and a **natural-language refinement box**
("make the leadership section stronger" / "shorten the
intro paragraph" — the AI regenerates that slice).
(3) **Refine & export** — download the PDF or generate a
public share link with a unique token. Persistent **resume
management dashboard** and **credit display** for active users.

**Business logic:** A Stripe-integrated credits ledger gates AI generation
capacity. Free credits on signup cover trial use; additional
credit packs scale further. The architecture aligns the
right incentive — each generation runs a 7-step OpenAI
pipeline plus a Typst compile, so per-event credit gating
keeps capacity predictable rather than all-you-can-eat.
Public **share links via unique tokens** give the product
organic distribution: a candidate who lands a job credits
Vitex on LinkedIn, the share link becomes social proof, the
referenced resume itself is a working landing page
demonstrating the product. The whole stack — Auth + DB +
Cache + Payments + AI + PDF rendering — is production-shaped
for a real user base, not a demo.

**Technical approach:** **AI agent layer.** The pipeline is **Vercel AI SDK v6**
with the OpenAI provider (`@ai-sdk/openai`) calling **GPT-4o**
across seven sequential stages, each producing a
**Zod-validated** structured output:
1. **JD Parsing** — extract role, seniority, must-have
   skills, nice-to-haves, company signals
2. **Background Parsing** — convert the user's free-text
   description into a structured skill / experience model
3. **Match Analysis** — compute the candidate↔JD fit profile
4. **Resume Tailoring** — reorder, rewrite, and emphasise
   the candidate's history toward the parsed JD
5. **ATS Scoring** — assess parse-ability against ATS rules,
   emit actionable feedback
6. **Cover Letter Generation** — produce a matching cover
   letter from the same parsed pair
7. **Document Generation** — render to Typst, compile to PDF

Each stage streams progress to the client via **Server-Sent
Events** so the user sees the agent thinking instead of a
spinner. The system disables OpenAI strict JSON schema
mode where Zod uses `.optional()` (commit log captures the
diagnosis), and reads JD + background from `sessionStorage`
instead of URL params so deep links don't leak input data.

**Document engine — Typst, not LaTeX.** PDF generation runs
a **local Typst binary** in the Docker image, compiling
each document in **under 100 ms** without calling any
external API. The project originally rendered with LaTeX
and migrated to Typst on 2026-04-06 — the same day as the
Cloudflare Workers → DigitalOcean VPS migration — because
Typst compiles in milliseconds where LaTeX needs seconds
and Workers couldn't run the binary at all. The Docker
image pre-populates the Typst package cache for FontAwesome
so cold container starts don't pay the package-fetch cost.
**14 professional templates** are AI-auto-selected based
on parsed industry / role signals; the renderer also ships
FontAwesome 6 contact icons (replacing the emoji icons of
an earlier iteration).

**Persistence + product stack.**
- **Neon serverless Postgres** with **Drizzle ORM** for
  user resumes, generation history, and credit ledgers
- **Upstash Redis** for short-lived cache + rate-control
  state
- **Stack Auth** for the authentication surface
- **Stripe** for the credits-ledger payment integration
- **Cloudinary** for user uploads and rendered-PDF storage
- **nanoid** for the shareable-token URL scheme

**UI.** Next.js 15 App Router + React 19 + TypeScript. The
**Neobrutalism design system** (hard shadows, thick borders,
high contrast) is the deliberate visual identity — chosen
and refined in the Phase 3 UI/UX overhaul branded
internally as *"sell results, not tools"*. shadcn/ui +
Tailwind for primitives; Framer Motion for transitions;
react-hook-form + Zod resolvers for input validation.

**Deployment — three migrations, one production.** Started
on **Railway**, migrated to **Cloudflare Workers**
(2026-02-22), then migrated again to a **DigitalOcean VPS**
(2026-04-06) because the Typst binary needs a real
filesystem and a long-lived container. Production now runs
as a single **Docker container** on the VPS behind
**Traefik** (reverse proxy + automatic TLS), continuously
deployed by **GitHub Actions** on every push to the
deployment branch. The Dockerfile uses `curl` (not `wget`)
and `xz-utils` for the Typst tarball — both fixes captured
in the commit log as real edge-case failures during the
VPS migration.

**Outcomes:** Live and operational at **vitex.org.nz**, serving real users
with the full free-credit + credit-pack flow. **168 commits over
~3 months** (2026-02-21 → 2026-05-09), **~95% Chan-authored**
across two GitHub identities (8 commits from Claude
co-author, 1 from Vercel deploy bot, rest solo). Three
deployment migrations and a LaTeX → Typst document-engine
swap shipped without service outage — every migration step
is in the commit log including the specific Docker
fixes (`xz-utils`, `curl`, FontAwesome package pre-cache),
the strict-JSON-schema regression diagnosis, and the
`sessionStorage` migration for sensitive input. Mobile
reliability + responsive UI pass shipped 2026-05-09 after
the production hardening phase.

**My role:** Sole engineer, sole designer, sole product owner. Designed
the 7-step agent pipeline with Zod-validated stages
(migrating the editor from CopilotKit to Vercel AI SDK
agent chat along the way), wrote every API route, built
the Stripe credit system, configured Stack Auth, designed
the Drizzle schema for resumes / credits / share tokens,
executed all three deployment migrations (Railway → CF
Workers → DigitalOcean VPS), executed the LaTeX → Typst
engine swap (including the Dockerfile fixes for `xz-utils`
and the Typst FontAwesome package pre-cache), designed the
Neobrutalism UI in the "sell results, not tools" Phase 3
overhaul, built the natural-language refinement loop in
the Editor, and continues to operate the production
service as recently as the May 2026 mobile reliability
pass.
### Aotearoa AI Hackathon 2026 — Event Q&A AI Assistant (She Sharp) — Industry-partnered AI event assistant for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched for 2026 (AUT City Campus) as a single-page voice-first agent: an audio-reactive Three.js orb driving a two-way OpenAI Realtime voice conversation (WebRTC + ephemeral keys) plus gpt-4o-mini text chat, over a Neon knowledge base + transcripts with KV rate-limiting, on Vercel. The 2025 edition (AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp) shipped a 3D sprite mascot with 6-tier affection gamification + a Notion-as-database FAQ on Cloudflare Workers; generalized into an open-source MIT template
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://hackathon.shesharp.org.nz/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/event-qa-ai-template
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 16.1 (App Router), React 18 + TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS v3 + Framer Motion v12, Vercel AI SDK v4.3, @ai-sdk/openai (GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18 streaming), Three.js 0.182 + @react-three/fiber 8.16, Custom Quantum Orb 3D mascot (CSS 3D transforms + Three.js particles), Sprite affection gamification engine (gesture detection, mood weighting, time-of-day bias), Notion REST API (raw fetch, CF Workers compatible — replaced @notionhq/client SDK), shadcn/ui + Radix primitives, @opennextjs/cloudflare adapter, Cloudflare Workers deployment (migrated from Vercel), wrangler v4 CI/CD, localStorage state persistence (50-msg chat history + sprite affection state), 5-min FAQ client cache + optimistic vote/view updates, AI rate limiting + per-session token budgets + prompt guardrails (2026-07 security hardening), OpenAI Realtime API over WebRTC — two-way voice (2026 relaunch), Ephemeral client secrets minted server-side (OPENAI_API_KEY never reaches the browser), Audio-reactive Three.js orb voice UI + live captions (2026), Vercel deployment (2026 edition — she-sharp1/aihackathon-2026)

**Summary:** Real industry-partnered AI event assistant built for the **AI
Hackathon Festival 2025** (Auckland, Aug 15–16, 2025), co-organised
by **Auckland University of Technology**, **AI Forum New Zealand**,
and **She Sharp**, with **UN Sustainable Development Goals** as
the innovation brief. The platform serves participants and mentors
through three interlocking systems: (1) an **interactive 3D sprite
mascot** with a 6-tier "Stranger → Soulmate" affection system,
7-state dynamic mood engine, 17 emotion categories, gesture
recognition (circle/pat/swipe), negative-behaviour penalties,
streak bonuses, and time-of-day emotion biases — a fully gamified
personality layer that responds to how users actually touch the
page; (2) a **Notion-as-database community FAQ** with up/downvote
ranking, view tracking, 5-minute client cache, and graceful static
fallback when Notion is unreachable; (3) an **OpenAI GPT-4o-mini
streaming chat** with markdown-rendered responses and one-click
FAQ-to-chat hand-off. After the live deployment, the codebase was
generalised into a reusable open-source template
(`event-qa-ai-template` on GitHub, MIT) so any future hackathon /
conference can fork-and-config the same stack. Chan was
subsequently recognised among the **14 expert mentors** at the
festival.

For the **2026 festival** (Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival 2026,
AUT City Campus) the project was **rebuilt from the ground up** as
a single-page **voice-first** agent: the whole site is a centered,
audio-reactive Three.js orb that starts a **two-way voice
conversation over the OpenAI Realtime API via WebRTC** (the client
exchanges SDP directly with OpenAI using a short-lived ephemeral
secret, so the real API key never reaches the browser), with a
text-input fallback answered by gpt-4o-mini via the Vercel AI SDK,
live captions, and a collapsible event-info panel. Agent
instructions build from a Neon `knowledge` table (graceful
fallback to config), transcripts persist best-effort, and Upstash
KV rate-limits token minting. Deployed on Vercel
(`she-sharp1/aihackathon-2026`, live at aihackathon-2026.vercel.app).

**Impact:** Voice-first AI event agent for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched 2026 (AUT) as an audio-reactive orb driving two-way OpenAI Realtime voice + gpt-4o-mini text chat; the 2025 edition served 11 teams / 80+ participants with a gamified 3D sprite mascot + Notion FAQ.

**Problem:** Hackathons and conferences generate the same Q&A surface every
year: participants ask the same 30 questions ("how do I form a
team", "what counts as AI", "when do I have to be on site",
"what's the prize structure", "where do I find a mentor"), and
organisers answer them via static PDFs that go stale the moment
the schedule shifts. Bolt-on chatbots exist but they treat
humans as passive consumers — a popup widget in the corner, a
knowledge base scraped at build time, no memory of what the
community itself finds helpful. Three structural gaps stack up:
(1) the FAQ is **authored once** rather than **curated by the
community**, so quality drifts; (2) the AI is a generic
wrapper around a chat API, with no event-specific personality
or theme tie-in; (3) organisers cannot update content without
a code redeploy, so the platform calcifies the moment the
original developer hands it off. The AI Hackathon Festival
2025 organisers needed a platform that solved all three on the
timeline of a real event with 80+ participants and three
co-organising institutions reviewing every word on screen.

**Audience:** Direct users were the **80+ participants and 14 expert mentors**
of AI Hackathon Festival 2025 — students from Auckland
universities, **Westlake Girls High School** secondary students,
and AI Forum NZ industry mentors all asking the same FAQ-style
questions across a 26-hour residential event. Secondary
audience: the **three co-organising institutions** (AUT, AI
Forum NZ, She Sharp) who use the platform as a public-facing
artefact of their joint programme. Tertiary audience: future
hackathon and conference organisers in NZ and abroad who can
fork the open-source template (`event-qa-ai-template`) and
deploy their own event Q&A AI in under an hour by editing four
configuration files — `site.config.ts`, `ai.config.ts`,
`content.config.ts`, `branding.config.ts` — without touching
component code.

**Product logic:** The product is structured as three layers that compose
independently. Layer one is the **3D welcoming surface**: the
landing page renders a particle sphere with a "WELCOME TO AI
HACKATHON!" greeting set against a `CosmicBackground` Three.js
scene, with `Calendar` / `MapPin` event-info widgets, sponsor
logos for the three co-organisers, and a glassmorphism Google
Maps modal showing AUT WG Building. Layer two is the **chat
page** — a 70/30 desktop split between a full-screen OpenAI
chat (with `react-markdown` + `rehype-highlight` for
code-block syntax highlighting) and a community FAQ sidebar.
Layer three — the differentiator — is the **interactive sprite
mascot** that lives alongside the chat: a CSS-3D **Quantum
Singularity Orb** (accretion disk + photon ring + event
horizon + energy jets, all built from layered divs with
`transform-style: preserve-3d` and `rotateX(60deg)`) that
becomes a fully gamified personality. The user can **circle**,
**swipe vertically/horizontally**, **pat**, or **click** the
orb; the gesture detection engine tracks each interaction and
feeds it through an affection/mood scoring system. Five
gestures grant affection (circle x5 = +10 affection / "Wheee~!
So dizzy but fun!"), five negative behaviours penalise it
(`roughMovement`, `spamClick`, `suddenLeave`, `longIgnore`,
`excessiveCircle`), and the resulting state drives which of
17 emotions the sprite expresses — including three **rare**
emotions (`devoted`, `starry`, `mischievous`) only unlocked
at the top affection tiers. The result: users do not just chat
with an AI, they **build a relationship with a tiny digital
creature** while doing so — a memorable, shareable layer that
turns a Q&A bot into a piece of event identity.

**Business logic:** Three intertwined value propositions. For the **organising
institutions**, the platform is a public-facing demonstration
artefact of the joint AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp programme,
running on Cloudflare Workers (free tier covers an event-week
traffic spike with zero cost) with a Notion database that
**non-developer event staff can edit live** during the event —
adding new FAQ entries through the Notion UI, watching them
appear within the 5-minute client cache window. For **future
event organisers**, after the live event, the codebase was
deliberately generalised into a **reusable MIT template**
(`event-qa-ai-template`) with all event-specific content
extracted into `config/*.ts` files and full setup
documentation, so the same stack ships any hackathon /
conference / community event Q&A platform in under an hour of
configuration. For **Chan**, the project established a
verifiable, AI-Forum-NZ-endorsed track record of shipping
commercial-grade AI infrastructure for an industry partner —
a credential cited in the public LinkedIn announcement of the
14-mentor cohort and in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter narrative.

**Technical approach:** **AI layer.** OpenAI GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18 served through
Vercel AI SDK v4.3's `streamText` with `convertToCoreMessages`,
returning `toDataStreamResponse()` for character-by-character
SSE streaming. The system prompt is **generated from
siteConfig at runtime** so any organiser fork inherits an
event-specific prompt without code edits — it embeds event
name, dates, venue, theme (UN SDGs), team-formation rules
(3-6 members, Hacker/Hipster/Hustler composition), schedule
(Friday 8 PM venue close, Discord for mentor requests), and
prize tiers ($250 venue, $1,000 national, $5,000 national
Agentic AI winner) plus Markdown formatting guidelines. The
codebase pivoted from Google Gemini 2.5 Flash to OpenAI
GPT-4o-mini mid-build (commit `5eb3e70`) for cost + Cloudflare
Workers compatibility reasons; the system prompt generator
remained provider-agnostic. **3D sprite gamification.** A
~960-line `sprite-affection.config.ts` declaratively encodes
the entire gameplay model: 5 gestures, 5 negative behaviours,
7 mood states (`ecstatic` 80–100 → `upset` -100 to -80),
17 emotions with per-emotion ear-colour RGB, glow-colour,
particle-colour, animation type (`bounce` / `shake` / `pulse`
/ `spin` / `wobble` / `tremble` / `droop`) and eye shape
(`sphere` / `arc` / `star` / `heart` / `crescent` / `spiral`
/ `teardrop`), 6 affection tiers (Stranger 0–9 → Acquaintance
10–29 → Friend 30–59 → Good Friend 60–99 → Best Friend
100–149 → Soulmate 150+), 7 time-of-day biases (late-night
boosts `sleepy` x3, evening boosts `love` x1.3), and 5 streak
bonus levels (1/3/7/14/30 days, 30-day streak unlocks the
`devoted` rare emotion). Two relational systems link mood
and affection: a **mood multiplier** scales affection gains
from 0.5x (depressed) to 1.5x (euphoric), and **affection
mood protection** sets a floor that rises with tier (Soulmate
gets a -10 floor + 5 mood/min recovery toward +20). All state
persists to `localStorage` under `sprite-affection-data`.
The Quantum Orb itself is rendered with **CSS-only 3D**
(preserve-3d transforms + rotateX) + Three.js particle
accents — no GLB / heavy 3D pipeline — so the bundle stays
small enough for Cloudflare Workers. **Notion backend.** A
non-obvious engineering call: the project initially used
`@notionhq/client`, but migrating to Cloudflare Workers
(commit `5b1dc7e`) broke the Node-dependent SDK; the fix
(commit `20eaf2e`) replaced it with **direct REST calls
against `api.notion.com/v1` using native `fetch`** — version
header `Notion-Version: 2022-06-28`, filter by
`IsActive` checkbox, parse `Title` / `RichText` / `Select` /
`Number` properties via small typed getters. Four API routes
— `/api/chat`, `/api/faq/questions`, `/api/faq/vote`,
`/api/faq/view` — wrap the same client. **Deployment.**
Migrated from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers using
`@opennextjs/cloudflare` v1.3 + wrangler v4; the entire stack
now ships on Cloudflare's free tier including the AI streaming
endpoint. The build command `opennextjs-cloudflare build &&
opennextjs-cloudflare deploy` handles the Next.js → Workers
adapter step.

**Outcomes:** Delivered live for the **AI Hackathon Festival 2025** at AUT
City Campus WG Building, **15–16 August 2025**. Used by **11
teams and 80+ participants** during the residential event —
AI Forum NZ's public LinkedIn cohort announcement (with full
Westlake Girls High School student attendance) cites Chan as
**1 of 14 expert mentors**, recognised at the event. The
teams shipped solutions across the UN SDG brief — food
insecurity, digital accessibility, workforce upskilling, and
indigenous environmental stewardship — with national-tier
prizes flowing through the AI Forum NZ pipeline (AI Summit
entry worth $1,499, $1,000 national winner, $5,000 national
Agentic AI winner, ArcGIS licences). Post-event, the codebase
was **refactored into a reusable MIT template** (commit
`948bdf0` "transform project into reusable event Q&A
template"), documented in `docs/TEMPLATE-SETUP.md`,
`docs/NOTION-INTEGRATION.md`, and `docs/UI-DESIGN-SYSTEM.md`,
with the live deployment retained as the canonical worked
example. Listed alongside Chan's other commercial-grade AI
platforms (echook, vitex, chinese-redefine,
ai-programming-teaching-project) as portfolio evidence of
end-to-end industry-partner delivery — design, AI integration,
gameplay engineering, database, deployment, and post-delivery
productisation as a reusable open-source artefact.

**My role:** Sole developer end-to-end. Owned the discovery phase with the
three co-organising institutions, designed the dual-layer
product (chat surface + gamified mascot + Notion FAQ), built
every component, authored both the Gemini and the OpenAI
provider implementations, designed the entire ~960-line
affection / mood / emotion gameplay schema, engineered the
CSS-3D Quantum Orb visualisation, migrated the deployment
from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers when free-tier economics
and Notion-SDK incompatibility forced the move, refactored
the codebase into a reusable open-source template after the
live event, and authored the three setup-documentation files.
On the mentorship side: served as one of the 14 expert
mentors during the 26-hour event itself, guiding teams on
prompt design, SDG framing, and product narrative —
simultaneously building the platform and using it.
### SendJoy — Visual Email Platform — No-code visual abstraction layer over the Resend API — drag-and-drop block editor, 5-step send wizard, Resend Audiences sync, and onboarding designed explicitly for non-technical users. Born as a Christmas-greeting CLI; pivoted into a full visual platform in 4 days.
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/send-joy
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15.1.2 (App Router), React 19.0, TypeScript 5.7, Tailwind CSS 3.4 + custom Neobrutalism shadow utilities, Resend SDK 4.0, React Email 0.0.31 (rendering) + @react-email/render 1.0.3, @dnd-kit/core 6.1 + @dnd-kit/sortable 8.0 (drag-and-drop editor), Zustand 5.0 (state + 50-state undo/redo history), shadcn/ui + 10 Radix primitives, react-colorful (theme color pickers), Lucide React (icon library), @opennextjs/cloudflare adapter, Cloudflare Workers deployment (migrated from Vercel, Feb 2026), GitHub Actions CI/CD auto-deploy, wrangler v4, localStorage state persistence (all user data lives in the browser)

**Summary:** A **no-code visual abstraction layer over the Resend API**, built
so that **non-technical users never have to read Resend's docs,
open Resend's dashboard, or learn merge-field syntax** to send
beautiful personalised email campaigns. Started life on Christmas
Eve 2025 as a one-shot CLI for sending Christmas greetings; four
days later, on Dec 27 2025, the codebase pivoted in a single
commit (`fe16fdd`: *"transform CLI tool into visual web
application platform"*) into a full Next.js 15 web app with a
drag-and-drop block-based template editor (7 block types,
`@dnd-kit`), a 5-step sending wizard, a Resend Audiences sync,
and a deliberately authored **onboarding + context-aware help
system "for non-technical users"** (commit `782731c`). Wraps four
Resend API surfaces — `/emails`, `/templates`, `/audiences`, and
`/audiences/.../contacts` — behind a unified UI so the user
never sees a request body. Ships **6 Neobrutalism preset
templates**, real-time desktop/mobile preview, undo/redo with
50-state history, and CSV-import contact management.
Privacy-first: all user data (templates, contacts, settings)
lives in browser localStorage, and the Resend API key is passed
per-request rather than persisted on any server. Live at
[sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/),
migrated from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers in Feb 2026 with
GitHub Actions auto-deploy.

**Impact:** No-code visual abstraction layer over the Resend API — drag-and-drop block editor, 5-step send wizard, Audiences sync, onboarding designed for non-technical users. Born as a Christmas-greeting CLI, pivoted to a full visual platform in 4 days across 43 commits.

**Problem:** Resend is the modern transactional-email API of choice for
developers — clean SDK, fast onboarding, MX-record sanity — but
the gap it leaves wide open is **non-technical users**.
Marketers, community managers, school teachers, holiday-card
senders, small-business owners, and event organisers want
exactly what Resend provides (high deliverability, real
sender domains, real bounce handling) but **cannot get
through the API on-ramp**. To send one campaign through
vanilla Resend, a non-technical user has to (1) read SDK docs,
(2) write Node code with the `Resend` client, (3) hand-author
HTML or React Email JSX, (4) understand merge-field syntax
differences between Resend's native templates and their own
templating, (5) manage `Audiences` and `Contacts` through the
REST API directly, and (6) deploy a serverless function or
run scripts locally. Every one of these steps is a wall.
Tools that **do** cater to non-technical users — Mailchimp,
ConvertKit, Substack — are either expensive at small list
sizes, lock the user into their templating, or don't expose
the underlying mail infrastructure (custom domains, API key
rotation, deliverability metrics) that Resend gives away
cheaply.

**Audience:** Explicitly **non-technical or semi-technical Resend users**.
The persona stack: (a) marketers and community managers who
want to send a polished campaign through Resend's
deliverability stack but don't write Next.js; (b) holiday
senders who want to send Christmas / Lunar New Year / Birthday
greetings to dozens of friends with personalised names baked
in; (c) small-business owners running newsletters out of
their own domain who don't want Mailchimp branding or pricing;
(d) developers who *can* code but want a visual editor for
speed because they're authoring one-off campaigns rather than
building a templated system. Secondary audience: other
developers who want to study a worked example of wrapping a
commercial API into a no-code platform — the codebase ships
MIT-licensed as a study artefact.

**Product logic:** The product replaces six pieces of Resend's developer
workflow with visual surfaces. **(1) HTML authoring** becomes
a drag-and-drop block editor with 7 block types — Header,
Text, Image, Button, Wishes List, Divider, Footer — each with
a focused property panel; React Email renders to real
email-client-safe HTML on the server. **(2) Templating** is
the visual canvas plus a theme panel with 9+ colour pickers
(`react-colorful`), 600px/375px device preview toggle, and
50-state undo/redo. **(3) Recipient management** becomes a
Contacts page with three import modes — manual entry, CSV
bulk import (Email/FirstName/LastName columns), and a
one-click sync from a Resend Audience (`audienceId` selected
from a dropdown). **(4) Merge fields** become four named
variables — `{{recipientName}}`, `{{senderName}}`,
`{{currentYear}}`, `{{nextYear}}` — with the server-side
renderer normalising between local camelCase syntax and
Resend native UPPER_SNAKE_CASE so a template can use either.
**(5) Sending** becomes a 5-step wizard: pick template -> pick
recipients -> customise variables -> preview the actual
rendered HTML (not a mockup) -> send-and-track with a real
per-recipient success/failure result table; the server hits
Resend per recipient with progress streamed back. **(6) API
infrastructure** is replaced by a Settings page where the
user pastes their Resend API key and verified sender email
once; the key never leaves the browser except as a
per-request header. A welcome-modal **onboarding flow**
(commit `782731c`, *"comprehensive onboarding and help
system for non-technical users"*) walks first-time visitors
through these six surfaces step by step, and every screen
has a context-aware help modal pinned to it.

**Business logic:** Open source under MIT. The product is **free to self-host**
(deploy to Cloudflare Workers in under a minute via the
`npm run deploy` script and `@opennextjs/cloudflare`) and
free to use on the public instance at
sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev (the user supplies their own
Resend API key, so Chan bears no email-delivery cost). The
commercial premise is asymmetric: Resend already monetises
through their own free tier (3,000 emails/month) plus paid
plans for higher volume; SendJoy makes their funnel reachable
to a customer segment Resend can't service directly, which
makes it a complementary product rather than a competing
one. The build-in-public value for Chan: a portfolio-grade
worked example of **"abstract a developer API into a no-code
product"** — a pattern the agentic-AI economy will need
repeatedly as more APIs ship without UX layers. The codebase
is deliberately reusable as a template for wrapping any
other Resend-shaped API (transactional SMS, push
notifications, file delivery).

**Technical approach:** Next.js 15.1.2 App Router with React 19 and TypeScript 5.7,
deployed on **Cloudflare Workers via
`@opennextjs/cloudflare`** (migrated from Vercel on
2026-02-04, commit `e9ace8a`, with a follow-up GitHub
Actions auto-deploy workflow in commit `a970c1a`). Four
Resend-wrapping API routes form the entire backend surface:
**`POST /api/send`** takes `{apiKey, from, to, subject,
html}` and forwards to the Resend SDK, returning
`{success, data: {id}, error?}`; **`POST /api/preview`**
takes `{blocks, theme, variables}` and runs
`@react-email/render` to produce email-client-safe HTML
with merge fields substituted; **`GET/POST/DELETE
/api/contacts`** wraps the Resend Audiences contact CRUD;
**`GET /api/resend-templates`** plus its `[id]/duplicate`
and `[id]/publish` sub-routes wrap Resend's cloud
templates. **State management** uses Zustand 5.0 with a
50-state undo/redo history stack — every block move, edit,
delete, or theme change is reversible. **Templates and
contacts persist to browser localStorage** under six
namespaced keys (`email-platform-templates`,
`email-platform-contacts`, `email-platform-settings`,
`email-platform-onboarding`, `email-platform-emails-sent`,
`email-platform-sidebar-collapsed`) — there is no
server-side database, no auth layer, and no telemetry. The
editor itself uses **`@dnd-kit/core` + `@dnd-kit/sortable`**
for accessible drag-and-drop (commit `1dadb76`, *"implement
drag-and-drop block reordering"*). The visual language is a
custom **Neobrutalism design system** — 4px solid black
borders, 8px hard-offset shadows (no blur), three brand
colours (`#DC2626` red, `#16A34A` green, `#F59E0B` gold),
custom Tailwind `shadow-neo-*` utilities — that signals
"approachable / playful" while still feeling polished.
Performance: 95+ Lighthouse, <1s TTFB, <100ms API. **GEO
optimisation** (commit `04fc131`, *"add Generative Engine
Optimization for AI discoverability"*) ships
machine-readable metadata for LLM-driven discovery, same
pattern as Chan's other public projects.

**Outcomes:** Live and functional at
[sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/)
since end of December 2025; 43 solo commits across roughly
6 weeks of active development with subsequent maintenance
through April 2026. The defining engineering moment is the
**4-day arc from CLI to visual platform**: Dec 23 ships
commit `16c7026` *"initial release of Christmas greeting
email sender"* (a CLI script using `tsx` and `dotenv` to
send one templated greeting via Resend), Dec 27 ships
commit `fe16fdd` *"transform CLI tool into visual web
application platform"*, and Dec 27–28 layers the visual
editor, the 5-step wizard, the Resend cloud-templates
integration, the Resend Audiences sync, and the onboarding
flow for non-technical users on top — all before the
product gets its final name `send-joy` (commit `dba12bc`,
Dec 29) and brand identity (`0798540`). The Feb 2026
Cloudflare Workers migration cuts hosting cost to zero and
adds GitHub Actions auto-deploy. The codebase serves as
Chan's worked example of the "wrap a developer API into a
no-code product for non-technical users" pattern — directly
cited in conversations with founders and recruiters as a
demonstration of full-stack product judgement (not just
engineering): identifying the non-technical-user gap in a
developer-first API, then closing it in a week.

**My role:** Sole product designer, sole developer, sole maintainer. Made
the call to pivot from a one-off Christmas CLI into a
general-purpose visual platform after the CLI's 6th commit;
designed the Neobrutalism visual language; designed the
7-block schema; designed the 5-step wizard; designed the
onboarding flow specifically for non-technical users (the
explicit framing in commit `782731c`); implemented every
Resend API wrapper route; engineered the localStorage
persistence model with zero server-side state; executed the
Vercel -> Cloudflare Workers migration with GitHub Actions
auto-deploy; authored the README, TUTORIAL.md, and GEO
metadata. Maintains the live deployment today.
### Seismophone — The independent observatory for AI music — Seismophone — commercial analytics layer built on a private Suno API probe (suno-research-private) — closed a gap Suno's official platform couldn't fill by discovering that Suno's serializer leaks unredacted parent→child lineage UUIDs only on /api/trending + persona sub-trees, then productizing that into the industry's first public Suno lineage explorer. Three free lenses + three single-purchase deep-dive reports. Trilingual EN/简中/日本語. Live on a Docker+Traefik VPS.
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-07-04]
- **URL:** https://seismophone.chanmeng.org/
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 16.2 (App Router, Webpack pin), React 19.2, TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS 4 + GSAP 3.15 scroll motion, Neon Postgres (serverless HTTP driver), Drizzle ORM 0.45 + Drizzle Kit 0.31, NextAuth v5 beta + Neon Auth (Better Auth), Google OAuth (no password flow), Stripe 22 (webhook-verified credit ledger, auto test/live switch), Resend 6 (insight email delivery), @suno-cards/parser workspace package (Valibot validators + typed Suno error hierarchy), WITH RECURSIVE Postgres CTEs (lineage graph traversal), Biome 2.4 (lint + format), Docker multi-stage + Node 20 slim runner, GitHub Container Registry (GHCR), VPS deployment behind Traefik + Let's Encrypt TLS, GitHub Actions CI/CD (typecheck + lint + build + deploy), Linux crontab (collect-trending 04:00 UTC, sync-all 04:05 UTC), next-themes (light/dark), i18n: EN / 简体中文 / 日本語 (cookie-driven, server-resolved)

**Summary:** Production third-party analytics layer for **Suno creators** —
built on a private API-probe codebase (**suno-research-private**)
that Chan developed first to map Suno's undocumented endpoints,
then productized and commercialized as SunoStats. The opportunity
was identified by **technical research** rather than market survey.
The Suno UI shows a total
play count and a like count, and that's it: no time series, no
tag-level breakdowns, no view of which tracks are climbing or
stalling, no exposure of the parent->child remix lineage that
Suno's own data model encodes. Reverse-engineering the public
Suno API surface revealed two specific gaps: (1) Suno's
serializer **leaks unredacted lineage UUIDs only on
`/api/trending` and persona-clip sub-trees** — nowhere else,
not even on the page that lets you remix a clip — making it
possible (and industry-unique) to build a public lineage
explorer by ingesting Trending daily and walking the graph
server-side via `WITH RECURSIVE` CTEs; (2) Suno **advertises
its public-read API surface via W3C oEmbed** (the same
protocol Discord and Slack use for link previews), which is
anonymous + CORS-enabled by design — making a compliant
analytics product possible without scraping, headless
browsers, or asking users for their Suno password. SunoStats
ships **6 lenses on a Suno career** — 3 free (portfolio at
`/@handle`, the lineage explorer, watched playlists with
daily pulse view) + 3 premium deep-dive analytics modes
(Tag Optimization, Performance Snapshot, Method Mix Analysis)
plus a weekly Trend Brief. **Single-purchase, no subscription**,
no card stored unless the user chooses to, **3 free credits
on signup**. Stack:
Next.js 16.2 + React 19.2 + Drizzle + Neon Postgres + Neon
Auth + Stripe webhook-verified credit ledger + Resend
insight delivery, deployed as a Docker container on a VPS
behind Traefik with GHCR + GitHub Actions CI/CD. Trilingual
EN / 简体中文 / 日本語. Live at
[sunostats.chanmeng.org](https://sunostats.chanmeng.org).

**Impact:** Industry's first Suno music lineage explorer — closed a gap in Suno's own platform that creators couldn't fix themselves by discovering an unredacted parent→child UUID leak; 46 solo commits, same-day scaffold-to-live, free + premium-lens analytics model.

**Problem:** Suno is one of the most prolific music platforms onthe
internet — millions of generated tracks, an Explore chart
that turns over daily — but its creators operate **almost
completely blind**. Suno's UI shows one total play count
per song, one total like count, and absolutely nothing
else: no play-history time series, no breakdown by tag, no
signal on which tracks are climbing or stalling, no
comparison against what's trending on Explore, no view of
which other creators have built on (remixed, concatenated,
covered, or edited) your clips. Adjacent platforms
(Spotify for Artists, YouTube Studio, SoundCloud Pro)
have shipped this baseline for a decade; Suno hasn't.
Worse: Suno's own data model **encodes the remix lineage
between clips** (every clip has a `parent_clip_id`, every
generation method has a `metadata.type` tag, every
upsample / concat / edit produces a typed edge in a
directed graph) — but none of that surfaces in the UI.
A creator has no idea their concat clips outperform their
gen clips 2.4x; no idea which of their tracks have been
remixed by others; no idea their work just hit a milestone
threshold. The gap is at the platform level, and Suno
cannot or will not close it.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. (1) **Suno creators** —
bedroom musicians, label A&R staff, indie producers, and
creator-economy professionals who release on Suno and
need actual signal to make tag, format, and timing
decisions. (2) **Suno-adjacent industry observers** — AI
music trend-watchers, journalists, content marketers who
cite Suno data, and other AI-music platforms benchmarking
against Suno's Explore chart. (3) **Engineers studying
the project as a worked example** of (a) reverse-engineering
a partial public API surface into a usable third-party
product, (b) compliance-first data sourcing via W3C
oEmbed, (c) a credits-ledger access model with no
recurring-subscription wall.

**Product logic:** Six lenses, deliberately tiered into three free + three
paid so a new user can value the product before paying.
**Free lens 1 — Portfolio at `/@handle`**: a shareable,
public, account-free page with an auto-generated OG
image, a grayscale-to-colour-on-hover cover grid,
tag-filterable and sortable by plays/likes/created date.
`middleware.ts` rewrites `/@handle` -> `/profile/[handle]`
so creators can share the prettier short form. **Free
lens 2 — Lineage Explorer at `/lineage/[clipId]`**: the
industry-unique core, built on the lineage-UUID research
finding. Renders the parent->descendant graph of any
Suno clip that has ever appeared on Explore, as a
clickable SVG. Private clips appear anonymised (no
title, no handle) so listing-level visibility is
respected. **Free lens 3 — Watched playlists with daily
pulse view (up to 10 per user)**: each cycle classifies
a playlist's entries into **new entries / risers /
fallers / drop-outs / holders** — useful for tracking
contest playlists, label-curated channels, or genre
playlists creators want their tracks placed in.
**Premium lens 4 — Tag Optimization Report**: compares
a creator's library tag distribution against the tags
winning on Explore right now, returns three priority
buckets (Add / Increase / Maintain). **Premium lens 5 —
Performance Snapshot**: time-series analysis of
the creator's own catalog with **milestone tracking
(100/500/1K/5K/10K plays)**, **movers / stalls /
decliners** by play velocity, and **per-Suno-model-version
breakdown** (v3 / v4 / v4.5+ average plays per song).
**Premium lens 5b — Method Mix Analysis**: distribution
across the **10-value `metadata.type` enum**
(`gen`/`concat`/`studio_export`/`edit_v3_export`/
`edit_crop`/`edit_speed`/`edit_fade`/`upsample`/
`concat_infilling`/`upload`) vs. the trending corpus,
plus per-method average plays, surfacing claims like
"your concat clips outperform gen clips by 2.4x".
**Premium lens 6 — Weekly Trend Brief**: cross-week
delta analysis on Explore, emailed via Resend with the
full writeup. Sign-up grants **3 free credits**, no
card required. Each insight is **inline-debited from
the `creditTransactions` ledger** with automatic refund
+ status `failed` if the generator throws.

**Business logic:** **Single-purchase credits ledger, no recurring
subscription**, no card stored unless the user chooses
to. The model is structurally important: SunoStats serves
a creator population whose willingness-to-engage is
non-uniform across the year (release cycles, contest
pushes), and a recurring-subscription wall would push out
the long tail of dabblers. A per-insight credits model
makes the access surface legible: a user runs one Tag
Report and instantly knows the value of that one report,
rather than committing to a tier they'd use sparingly.
Stripe Checkout is one-click; live and test modes
**auto-switch based on the `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` prefix**
so demos can run without touching production data. The
webhook signs every transaction into the
`creditTransactions` ledger before the credit is usable.
Three credit packs (Starter / Pro / Bulk) scale the
ledger top-up. Portfolios and the Lineage Explorer are
permanently free — they're the network-effect surface
(creators share their `/@handle` URL on socials, lineage
URLs link out from any Suno discussion) that pulls new
signups into the funnel. The product's competitive moat
is the lineage research itself: no other Suno analytics
product surfaces parent->child remix graphs, because
nobody else has discovered the data leaks through
`/api/trending` and persona-clip sub-trees.

**Technical approach:** **The discovery and the engineering it enables.** Chan
reverse-engineered Suno's public JSON surface and found
that Suno's response serializer **leaks unredacted
`parent_clip_id` fields only when a clip is included in
the `/api/trending` snapshot or when it appears as a
descendant of a persona clip** — never on the standard
clip-detail endpoint, never on the profile listing
endpoint, never in the remix UI itself. The
`collect-trending` cron job (04:00 UTC daily) walks
every clip in Trending, ingests the leaked lineage
UUIDs, deduplicates them into a `clipLineageEdges`
table, and serves the resulting graph via Postgres
`WITH RECURSIVE` CTEs through `/api/lineage/[clipId]`
(public, no auth, configurable `hopsUp`/`hopsDown`
query params capped at 5/3). Private clips appear as
anonymised nodes so listing-level visibility is
respected. **Compliance posture**: every Suno read goes
through endpoints **Suno itself advertises via the W3C
oEmbed protocol** (the same
`<link rel="alternate" type="application/json+oembed">`
any reader software follows), making the data plane
anonymous, CORS-enabled, and public-by-design. No HTML
scraping, no headless browsers, no authenticated
requests, no proxying user sessions. The full
posture lives at
`/legal/data-sources`.
**Suno parser package**: `packages/parser`
(`@suno-cards/parser`) is a workspace-local Valibot
validator + typed-error hierarchy (`SunoNotFoundError`,
`SunoNetworkError`, `SunoSchemaError`, …) consumed by
`src/lib/suno/*` — every external Suno call returns
typed errors mapped to user-facing API codes. Small
reverse-engineered details get their own utils: e.g.
Suno's CDN `cdn2.suno.ai/image_*.jpeg?width=N` only
accepts widths `{100, 256, 360, 720}`, so
`resizeSunoCover` snaps automatically. **Insight
generation pipeline**: each report is an idempotent
per-user-per-day pure async function (`tag-optimization`,
`performance-snapshot`, `method-mix`) in
`src/lib/insights/*`; an i18n-aware `headline()`
function returns `{eyebrow, headline, subtitle}` for
the report card; the React Server Component dispatcher
at `/dashboard/insights/[id]` selects the right
renderer by `kind`. Adding a new insight type requires
exactly **8 touchpoints** (engine + payload type +
headline + 3-locale translations + report component +
registration in both the on-demand and cron-batch
routes + dispatcher case + dashboard button) —
deliberately friction'd so each concern stays separated.
**Deployment**: Docker multi-stage build (Node 20 slim
runner with non-root `nextjs` user); GitHub Actions
pushes images to `ghcr.io/chanmeng666/sunostats/web`,
SSHes to the VPS, restarts the container with Traefik
labels for `sunostats.chanmeng.org` (Let's Encrypt TLS),
and rewrites `/etc/cron.d/sunostats` to re-schedule
the 4 cron endpoints with the fresh `CRON_SECRET`.
Cron-protected internal routes 401 anything without
the `x-cron-secret` header. **i18n**: trilingual
EN/简体中文/日本語 with cookie-driven server-resolved
locale plus Accept-Language fallback. The translation
dictionary is a TypeScript type, so adding a key fails
the build if any locale is missing — guaranteeing
coverage without runtime drift.

**Outcomes:** Built and shipped April 2026, with a same-day
scaffold-to-live trajectory: the initial commit
`f2f9f80` (2026-04-12) is *"scaffold SunoStats monolith
— Next.js 15 + Drizzle + Tailwind 4"*, followed within
hours by Neon Auth (`2c243dd`), Google OAuth
(`2ff764f`), the data collection pipeline (`40707ec`),
and the portfolio + landing + OG image generator
(`b5d1e1d`). Total of **46 solo commits across the first
month** taking the project from blank repo to the
production system live at sunostats.chanmeng.org —
with auth, Stripe-integrated payments, Resend email, 4 cron
pipelines, the lineage explorer with `WITH RECURSIVE`
graph queries, the 3 paid insight engines, the
watched-playlist pulse view, the Docker + Traefik VPS
deploy, and trilingual i18n all wired end-to-end.
Most recent commit `d36ddfb` (2026-05-10) tunes
production container env handling. The lineage
research finding remains the project's competitive
moat — no other Suno analytics product surfaces the
parent->child remix graph, because the discovery
depends on noticing which API endpoints leak the
UUIDs versus which redact them. Demonstrated to
founders and recruiters as Chan's worked example of
**identifying a gap by technical research, then
closing it with a fully production-shape full-stack
product in 4–5 weeks**.

**My role:** Sole product designer, sole engineer, sole maintainer.
Did the reverse-engineering research that found the
lineage-UUID leak surface; designed the
oEmbed-discovered compliance posture and the public
data-sources legal page; designed the 3-free + 3-premium
lens taxonomy; designed the
credit-ledger-with-auto-refund Stripe-integrated payment
model; designed the 8-touchpoint extension pattern for new
insight types; built every Suno parser in
`@suno-cards/parser`; engineered the 4 cron pipelines
with `x-cron-secret`-gated internal routes; built the
lineage `WITH RECURSIVE` CTE graph traversal; built
the Docker+Traefik+GHCR deployment pipeline with
crontab auto-install via GitHub Actions; authored the
trilingual EN/简中/日本語 i18n dictionary; wrote the
public-facing README + the internal AGENTS.md and
architecture/data-model/i18n docs. Maintains the live
deployment today.
### AI Image Generator — Full-Stack Text-to-Image SaaS — Full-stack text-to-image SaaS pivoted onto Cloudflare Workers AI's FLUX.1 Schnell free tier (100k req/day) — auth + credits ledger + Stripe-integrated payments + image storage + gallery, deployed on Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext. Two architectural pivots (Vercel->CF Workers free-AI 2026-04-09, then full SaaS layer one day later) turned a 2024 demo into a production product.
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/image-generator
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15.5 (App Router with (auth) + (protected) route groups), React 19.2 + TypeScript 5, Tailwind CSS 3.3 + tailwindcss-animate, Cloudflare Workers AI (env.AI binding, no API key), FLUX.1 Schnell 12B-parameter model (@cf/black-forest-labs/flux-1-schnell, steps=4), Cloudflare Workers (edge runtime, 300+ POPs), @opennextjs/cloudflare 1.18 adapter, wrangler 4.81 CI/CD, Neon Postgres (serverless HTTP driver), Drizzle ORM 0.45 + Drizzle Kit 0.31, Neon Auth (Better Auth, 0.2.0-beta with better-auth-migration.sql), Stripe 22 (webhook-verified credit ledger, auto test/live mode switch), @paralleldrive/cuid2 (collision-resistant IDs), @opentelemetry/api (observability), react-hot-toast (notifications), Radix UI primitives (Label, Slot), Mondrian-inspired UI (geometric background + minimalist grayscale aesthetic), Apache-2.0 license (with patent grant)

**Summary:** Production **full-stack text-to-image SaaS** — image generation
powered by **Cloudflare Workers AI's FLUX.1 Schnell** 12B-parameter
model accessed through the in-platform `env.AI` binding (no
external API key, 100k requests/day on the free tier), wrapped in
a complete SaaS surface: **Neon Auth + Stripe credits ledger +
Neon Postgres image storage + gallery + (auth) and (protected)
Next.js route groups**. The interesting story is the **two
architectural pivots** the project went through to get here:
it started 2024-11-27 as a Vercel-hosted Next.js demo using a
paid AI provider, ran in that shape for ~16 months gathering
iteration ideas, then on **2026-04-09 pivoted to Cloudflare
Workers with free-tier FLUX.1 Schnell** (commit `3aec6ce`,
*"migrate from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers with free AI image
generation"*), and on **2026-04-10** — one day later —
transformed from "free demo" to **full SaaS in a single commit**
`8626be4` *"add authentication, credits, payments, and image
storage"*. UI is a deliberate **Mondrian-inspired** geometric
composition (later refined to minimalist grayscale on
2026-04-20). Stack: Next.js 15.5 + React 19.2 + Drizzle ORM +
Neon Postgres + Better Auth + Stripe 22 + OpenTelemetry +
`@opennextjs/cloudflare` adapter. Live at
[image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/).
Apache-2.0 with patent grant. 24 solo commits across ~17 months
of sustained iteration.

**Impact:** Full-stack text-to-image SaaS pivoted onto Cloudflare Workers AI's FLUX.1 Schnell free tier (100k req/day) — auth, credits ledger, Stripe-integrated payments, image storage, gallery; 17 months of sustained development from Vercel demo to free-tier production SaaS.

**Problem:** Text-to-image AI tools sit in an awkward economic position:
the model inference (DALL-E, Stable Diffusion XL, Midjourney
API) costs real money per image, so every demo on the
internet is either rate-limited to single-digit images per
user, hidden behind a sign-up wall, or quietly burning
someone's API budget on every public generation. Building a
consumer-facing image generator that's **actually free at
meaningful volume** requires either subsidising the inference
cost out of pocket or finding a provider whose free tier is
large enough to underwrite a public deployment. At the same
time, the few "free" public image generators that do exist
cap at a few hundred generations per day and ship as
single-page demos with no persistence — you can't see your
history, can't save generations to an account, can't share
a gallery, can't upgrade to higher volume even if you
wanted to pay. The product gap is **a full-stack image SaaS
whose unit economics work because the inference layer is
actually free at the volumes hobbyists need, with an upgrade
path for users who want persistence + paid credits**.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. (1) **Casual creators**
(designers, content makers, hobbyists) who want to generate
images for blog headers, social posts, mood boards, or
decoration without committing to a paid Midjourney subscription
or wrestling with self-hosted Stable Diffusion — the free tier
covers them. (2) **Power users** who hit the free tier ceiling
and want a credit-based top-up path with persistent gallery
and account-tied image history — Stripe credits ledger
covers them. (3) **Engineers studying the project as a worked
example** of (a) discovering a free-AI free-tier large enough
to underwrite a public product, (b) layering a full SaaS on
top in 48 hours, (c) the @opennextjs/cloudflare adapter
pattern for Next.js 15 App Router on Cloudflare Workers.

**Product logic:** The product surface is intentionally split between **free
anonymous mode** and **authenticated SaaS mode** through
Next.js 15 route groups. The default `/` route is
unauthenticated: a Mondrian-inspired geometric background
with a single text-area prompt input, generate button, and
image display. Anyone can generate images via the
`/api/generate` POST endpoint which routes to the
Cloudflare Workers AI binding (`env.AI.run('@cf/black-
forest-labs/flux-1-schnell', { prompt, steps: 4 })`), returns
a base64-encoded PNG, and the client renders it via a
`data:image/png;base64,…` URI. **Free tier ceiling**:
100,000 image-generation requests per day, enforced by
Cloudflare's Workers AI quota — that's enough to underwrite
a public deployment indefinitely for hobbyist traffic. Sign
in (via the `(auth)/` route group) and the same prompt
input now lives in the `(protected)/` route group with
added persistence: every generation goes through the
`/api/images` endpoint to write metadata + the base64 blob
to Neon Postgres via Drizzle ORM, indexed by a `cuid2`
collision-resistant ID, and shows up in the `/gallery`
view. Credits work as a usage cap on the authenticated
path — `/api/credits` reads the user's ledger, `/api/stripe/
checkout` creates a Stripe Checkout session for the chosen
credit pack, and the `/api/stripe/webhook` route receives
the signed event from Stripe (using the `stripe:listen`
and `stripe:trigger` dev scripts for local testing) and
atomically debits/credits the ledger. **The auto test/live
Stripe mode switch** (commit `00791c0`) detects the
`STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` prefix and routes traffic accordingly
so demos can run against test keys without environment
re-config.

**Business logic:** The architectural insight that makes the project viable:
**the Cloudflare Workers AI free tier underwrites the
inference path on public traffic**. 100,000 generations/day
is the load-bearing constraint that lets the surface stay
open while a credits-ledger upgrade path scales power-user
capacity. Without that free-tier ceiling the choice would
have been: cap everyone, lose money on every public
generation, or rate-limit so aggressively the demo isn't
useful. The pivot to Cloudflare Workers AI (commit
`3aec6ce`) was the structural decision that made the
whole SaaS shape workable. Above the free tier:
Stripe-integrated credit packs (test/live mode
auto-switching, webhook-verified ledger) provide the
upgrade path. Storage on Neon's serverless Postgres keeps
persistence light at typical hobbyist volume. Net: the
product is **open for the long-tail majority of users** and
scales gracefully for the small minority who exhaust their
free credits. Apache-2.0 with patent grant — explicitly
licensed for commercial use, fork, and re-deployment.

**Technical approach:** **AI layer.** Cloudflare Workers AI is accessed through
the **in-platform `env.AI` binding** declared in
`wrangler.jsonc` (`{"ai": {"binding": "AI"}}`) — no
external API key required, no network hop to a third-party
provider, all inference runs inside Cloudflare's edge.
The model is **FLUX.1 Schnell** (12B parameters,
`@cf/black-forest-labs/flux-1-schnell`) called with
`steps: 4` (1-8 range — fewer steps = faster, more
steps = higher quality). Response is base64-encoded PNG
returned as a JSON string, rendered client-side via a
`data:image/png;base64,…` URI for zero-additional-request
display. **Full-stack SaaS layer.** Next.js 15.5 App
Router with `(auth)/` and `(protected)/` **route groups**
for clean separation of unauth/auth surfaces;
`/api/generate` (free public path), `/api/auth/[...path]`
(Neon Auth handler), `/api/credits` (ledger reads),
`/api/images` (gallery persistence), `/api/gallery`
(public gallery), `/api/stripe/checkout` + `/api/stripe/
webhook` (billing). **Database**: Neon Postgres via the
`@neondatabase/serverless` HTTP driver (better fit for
Workers' stateless runtime than connection-pooled drivers
— no pool exhaustion, no pgbouncer hop), schema in
Drizzle ORM. **Auth**: Neon Auth (Better Auth beta) with
a committed `better-auth-migration.sql` for schema setup.
**IDs**: `@paralleldrive/cuid2` for collision-resistant
URL-safe identifiers (better than UUIDs for public-facing
image gallery URLs). **Observability**: `@opentelemetry/
api` instrumentation hooks. **Deployment**: standard
`@opennextjs/cloudflare` 1.18 adapter — `npm run deploy`
runs `opennextjs-cloudflare build && opennextjs-cloudflare
deploy`, building the Next.js app and bundling it through
the OpenNext compiler into a Workers-compatible
single-file bundle. Same deployment pattern Chan uses on
sunostats, send-joy, chinese-redefine, and the event-qa
template — the pattern is now a portable skill. **UI**:
deliberate **Mondrian-inspired geometric composition** in
the initial design phase (`MondrianBackground.tsx` is a
first-class component), later refined to a **minimalist
grayscale aesthetic** on 2026-04-20 (commit `10c5a0d`).
