Both layers are intentional design choices, not
accidental theming. **Build**: `npm run cf-typegen` runs
`wrangler types --env-interface CloudflareEnv` to
regenerate the TypeScript bindings file for the AI
binding, keeping the type system in sync with the live
Cloudflare environment.

**Outcomes:** Live at
[image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/)
with the full SaaS surface active and operating on the
Cloudflare Workers free tier across both inference (Workers
AI 100k/day) and database (Neon free tier). **17 months of
sustained development across 24 solo commits**, with the
defining narrative being the **two architectural pivots in
48 hours**: 2026-04-09 (`3aec6ce`) migrated the inference
layer from Vercel + paid provider to Cloudflare Workers +
free FLUX.1 Schnell — cutting unit cost to zero; 2026-04-10
(`8626be4`) added the entire SaaS layer (auth + credits +
Stripe + image storage) in a single commit, transforming a
free demo into a fully-monetisable product with an upgrade
path. The Mondrian-inspired design layer was added across
the 2024-12 window (commits including `7717b8f` "Add logo")
and refined to minimalist grayscale on 2026-04-20
(`10c5a0d` *"redesign UI/UX with minimalist grayscale
aesthetic"*). Stripe test/live auto-mode-switching
(`00791c0`) followed the next day. **Apache-2.0 with
patent grant** licensing makes the codebase explicitly
fork-and-deploy-able as a worked example for engineers
wanting to ship their own free-tier AI SaaS on Cloudflare
Workers — the entire deployment fits inside Cloudflare's
and Neon's free tiers for typical hobbyist volume.

**My role:** Sole product designer, sole engineer, sole maintainer.
Identified the Cloudflare Workers AI free-tier free-FLUX
opportunity in early 2026 and executed the pivot from
Vercel + paid AI provider to Workers + free Workers AI
binding within a single commit (`3aec6ce`); designed the
SaaS layer extension (auth + credits + Stripe + image
storage) and shipped it the next day (`8626be4`);
designed the dual-surface UX with Next.js 15
`(auth)/`/`(protected)/` route groups so anonymous users
keep the free generation path; designed the
Mondrian-inspired geometric UI and the subsequent
minimalist-grayscale refinement; engineered the Drizzle
ORM + Neon Postgres + Neon Auth (Better Auth)
persistence stack; wrote the Stripe test/live auto-mode
switching logic; configured the @opennextjs/cloudflare
deployment pipeline. Maintains the live deployment today.
### LibraryOS — Multi-Tenant Library SaaS — 18-month / 80-commit production multi-tenant SaaS on its own libraryos.live domain — 4-tier access model with deliberate regional accessibility design, 4-role RBAC, Supabase row-level security + keep-alive cron, survived two architectural pivots (single-tenant->multi-tenant Dec 2025, Vercel->Cloudflare Workers Feb 2026) and a 12-commit Stripe-SDK-on-Workers debugging saga that ended by switching the Stripe client to fetch-based HTTP.
[tier=archive · recency=deprecated · updated=2026-07-08]
- **URL:** https://libraryos.live/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/library-os
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 16.1 (App Router), React 19.2, TypeScript 5.9, Tailwind CSS 4 + tailwindcss-animate, shadcn/ui + 10 Radix UI primitives (Dialog, Dropdown, Select, Tabs, Toast, Progress, etc.), Supabase PostgreSQL (auth + RLS policies + stored procedures + migrations), Supabase keep-alive cron (prevent 7-day free-tier auto-pause), Stripe 20 (Checkout + Customer Portal + webhooks + signature verification), Stripe with fetch-based HTTP client (Cloudflare Workers compatibility), Stripe price IDs read from database (per-tier/per-region dynamic pricing), Resend 6 (transactional emails for team invitations), React Hook Form + Zod 4 (typed form validation), React Context (AuthContext, OrganizationContext for tenant switching), date-fns 4 (loan/return date arithmetic), @opennextjs/cloudflare 1.16 adapter, Cloudflare Workers deployment (migrated from Vercel), wrangler 4.61 CI/CD, libraryos.live custom domain (purchased, not default subdomain), Apache-2.0 license

**Summary:** Production **multi-tenant library management SaaS** with its own
purchased custom domain ([libraryos.live](https://libraryos.live)),
built over **18 months across 80 solo commits** spanning **two
major architectural pivots and one infamous debugging saga**. The
product offers a 4-tier access model with Stripe-integrated
flows (Free / Basic / Pro / Enterprise), 4-role RBAC
(Owner/Admin/Librarian/Member), per-tenant data isolation via
Supabase row-level security policies, team invitations via Resend
transactional emails, multi-organization membership (users can
belong to multiple libraries and switch between them), and an
**Africa-friendly accessibility tier** explicitly designed for
lower-income markets. **Defining engineering moments**: (1) the
single-tenant -> multi-tenant SaaS rebuild on 2025-12-30 (commit
`5e7e8c5`); (2) the same-week rebrand from
`library-management-system` -> `LibraryOS` (commit `4bd58b5`);
(3) the **Supabase keep-alive cron** to prevent the free-tier
database from auto-pausing after 7 days of inactivity (commits
`17344d6` + `64b03d0`); (4) the **Vercel -> Cloudflare Workers
migration** (commit `14c42fc`, 2026-02-03) with the custom
`libraryos.live` domain switch (`ecf6c14`); (5) the
**12-commit Stripe-SDK-on-Cloudflare-Workers debugging saga**
ending with the architectural fix in commit `e1ab824`
*"use fetch HTTP client for Stripe SDK on Cloudflare Workers"*
and the companion `85b6244` *"read Stripe price IDs from database
instead of env vars"*. Stack: Next.js 16.1 + React 19.2 +
Tailwind 4 + Supabase + Stripe 20 + Resend 6 + shadcn/ui +
Zod 4 + @opennextjs/cloudflare. Apache-2.0.

**Impact:** Multi-tenant library-management SaaS with deliberate regional accessibility design — survived two architectural pivots (single→multi-tenant, Vercel→Cloudflare Workers), 4-tier access model, ready for community libraries, small schools, and makerspaces.

**Problem:** Small libraries — community libraries, school libraries,
startup-office book collections, niche-genre clubs, university
residence hall libraries — are stuck between two bad options
for managing their book collections. Option A: enterprise-grade
Integrated Library Systems (Koha, Evergreen, ExLibris Alma)
that demand heavy operational overhead, require dedicated IT
staff to operate, and ship with UX patterns from 2005.
Option B: spreadsheets and shared Google Sheets, which silently
break the moment two librarians edit at once, give no role-based
permissions, and can't handle email invitations or membership
scoping. Both options also assume **one library per installation** —
none of them handle the case where a single platform should serve
hundreds of independent organisations, each with its own books,
members, and loans. That's the gap LibraryOS fills: a
**multi-tenant library SaaS** with per-organisation data
isolation, a tiered access model including an Africa-friendly
accessibility tier for lower-income markets, and a modern
Next.js 16 + React 19 frontend that any community librarian
can navigate without training.

**Audience:** Three primary audiences. (1) **Community library operators**:
churches, residence halls, makerspaces, co-working spaces,
small private schools, niche-genre clubs — anyone running a
book collection serving 50–5,000 members who outgrew
spreadsheets but can't justify Koha's TCO. (2) **Multi-branch
organisations**: parent organisations operating multiple
libraries (school districts, library networks, NGOs running
multi-site book-donation programmes) who need
per-organisation isolation but unified administration. (3)
**Engineers studying the project as a worked example** of
(a) Supabase row-level security policies for multi-tenant
data isolation, (b) Stripe Checkout + Customer Portal +
webhooks with database-stored access IDs for per-tier and
per-region routing, (c) the @opennextjs/cloudflare
adapter pattern with non-trivial third-party SDK integration
(the Stripe-on-Workers fetch-HTTP-client workaround is the
kind of production issue a project ships through, not
around).

**Product logic:** The product surface is structured around three concentric
nouns: **users** (one person, one auth identity), **organisations**
(one library entity with its own books, members, settings),
and **memberships** (the join between user and organisation
carrying a role). A user signs up once and can belong to
many organisations — switching between them via the
`OrganizationContext` provider in the React tree, which
mirrors the same pattern Slack and Linear use. Within an
organisation, four roles compose a granular permission
matrix: **Owners** can delete the organisation and manage
access; **Admins** can manage members but not delete the
org; **Librarians** can add/edit books and manage loans;
**Members** can browse, borrow, return, and review books.
Per-tenant data isolation is enforced **at the database
layer** via Supabase row-level security (RLS) policies on
every table — `books`, `loans`, `members`, `reviews`,
`categories` — so even a SQL injection in application code
can't leak across tenants. **Access tiers**: the Free tier
covers 100 books / 5 users / 3 loans-per-user; Basic, Pro,
and Enterprise scale those quotas progressively to unlimited.
Stripe Checkout handles initial activation, Customer Portal
handles upgrades / downgrades / cancellations, and the
`/api/stripe/webhook` endpoint atomically updates the
`organizations.subscription_status` field based on signed
Stripe events. **Team invitations**: an Admin invites a new
member by email; Resend sends a transactional invite with a
scoped token; the recipient clicks through, authenticates,
and joins the organisation with the pre-assigned role.
**The Africa-friendly accessibility tier** is a deliberate
regional design decision (commit `16e81c1` *"add live mode
pricing for Africa-friendly subscriptions"*) — lower-income
markets need a region-aware entry point for the product to
be reachable.

**Business logic:** The infrastructure choices are structurally favourable: Supabase
covers the database for typical small-library volume (the
Supabase keep-alive cron — commit `17344d6` — prevents the
database from auto-pausing after extended idle periods, which is
the single load-bearing infrastructure decision); Cloudflare
Workers handle edge compute. The Africa-friendly accessibility
tier is the non-obvious product decision: rather than ignoring
lower-income markets, LibraryOS deliberately ships a region-aware
entry point so the product reaches global communities that the
Koha / Alma incumbents have priced out entirely.
**Apache-2.0** with patent grant licensing makes the codebase
explicitly fork-and-deploy-able as a worked example for engineers
wanting to ship their own multi-tenant SaaS — particularly useful
given the Stripe-on-Workers compatibility solution baked in.

**Technical approach:** **Multi-tenant SaaS architecture.** Single Supabase
Postgres instance with strict row-level security policies
on every domain table — a policy like `organization_id =
auth.jwt() ->> 'org_id'` enforces tenant isolation
regardless of application bugs. Stored procedures for the
non-trivial loan-lifecycle logic (loan -> due-date
calculation -> return -> overdue triggers). The schema is
documented as an ERD in the README covering
`organizations`, `organization_members`, `books`, `loans`,
`reviews`, `categories`, and the subscription-state
columns on `organizations`. **The Stripe-on-Cloudflare-
Workers fight (March 2026).** The Stripe Node SDK uses the
Node `https` module by default — which Workers don't have.
First symptom: checkout endpoint times out silently.
Initial debug attempts (commits `fdd1103` "add visible
debug panel", `2b54c48` "add /api/stripe/debug endpoint",
`991af90` "step-by-step server logging") progressively
isolated the failure to the Stripe SDK's HTTP client
layer. Final fix (commit `e1ab824`): configure the Stripe
constructor with `httpClient: Stripe.createFetchHttpClient()`
so it uses the standard `fetch` API instead of `https`,
making it Workers-compatible. The companion fix (commit
`85b6244`): move Stripe price IDs from environment
variables to a database lookup, so the same deployment can
serve different prices for different tenants or regions
without env-var sprawl. The whole episode is a real
production-debugging artefact, documented in the
Stripe + Cloudflare Workers integration guide (commit
`12ddaef`). **Supabase keep-alive system** (commit
`17344d6`, optimised in `64b03d0`). Supabase's free tier
auto-pauses any database with 7 consecutive days of zero
queries. For a low-traffic SaaS this would silently break
the production deployment. The keep-alive system is a
scheduled cron that pings the database with a no-op query
every few days, keeping it active without consuming
meaningful free-tier quota. **Custom domain
infrastructure**: registered `libraryos.live` (a purchased
domain, not a default Workers subdomain) on 2026-02-03
(commit `ecf6c14`), wired DNS through Cloudflare, and
co-located the Stripe live-mode account on the same
domain to keep the production billing surface
single-origin. **Frontend stack**: Next.js 16.1 App Router
with React 19.2 + TypeScript 5.9, Tailwind 4 with custom
brand-gradient colours (#02FF73 / #09ADAA), shadcn/ui
component library on top of 10 Radix UI primitives,
React Hook Form with Zod 4 schema validation for typed
forms end-to-end. **State**: two React Context providers
— `AuthContext` for user/auth and `OrganizationContext`
for the currently-selected tenant — so component code
reads the current tenant without prop drilling.
**Security responsiveness**: Vercel's automated CVE
patching system flagged two React Server Components +
React Flight RCE vulnerabilities in late 2025
(`vercel/react-server-components-cve-vu-64tvuo` and
`vercel/react-flight-and-nextjs-rce-ad-i38an0`); both PRs
merged within hours of discovery
(commits `85fa6aa` + `101568a`).

**Outcomes:** Live and operational at
[libraryos.live](https://libraryos.live) — a **purchased
custom domain**, not the default `*.workers.dev`
subdomain, signaling a commitment beyond a hobby
deployment. **80 solo commits across 18 months** from the
first commit on 2024-10-30 through the most recent on
2026-04-07 (commit `5c8d8e7`). The defining timeline is
the **Dec 2025 multi-tenant rebuild**: commit `2ad43de`
(2025-12-30) upgrades the entire dependency stack to
latest tech (React 19, Next.js 16, Tailwind 4), commit
`5e7e8c5` (same day) ships *"add multi-tenant support
with Stripe billing and Resend emails"* — the rebuild
that transformed a single-tenant library tool into a
SaaS, followed by 10 more commits across Dec 30–31 wiring
up Stripe live mode + auth + UI brand redesign + the
rename to LibraryOS (`4bd58b5`) — a full multi-tenant
SaaS shipped in ~36 hours. The **Feb 2026 Vercel ->
Cloudflare Workers migration** (commit `14c42fc`)
consolidated the production stack on Cloudflare's edge
platform. The **March 2026 Stripe-SDK-on-Workers debugging
saga** is documented across 12 commits and resolved with
`e1ab824` — the kind of production issue that
demonstrates engineering ability to **debug across
platform boundaries**, not just ship features. Continuing
maintenance through April 2026 with README polish
(`5c8d8e7`). The project serves as Chan's worked example
of full-stack solo SaaS execution — multi-tenant
architecture, payment integration, security
responsiveness, persistence infrastructure (Supabase
keep-alive), and globalisation (Africa-friendly
accessibility tier) — all on a purchased custom domain
that signals "this is a real product".

**My role:** Sole product designer, sole engineer, sole maintainer
across all 80 commits and 18 months. Designed the
multi-tenant schema with row-level security; designed the
4-tier access model including the Africa-friendly regional
accessibility decision; designed the 4-role RBAC
permission matrix; built every API route under
`/api/auth`, `/api/email`, `/api/stripe`, and `/api/health`;
engineered the Supabase keep-alive cron preventing
free-tier auto-pause; executed the December 2025 single-
tenant -> multi-tenant rebuild as a ~36-hour sprint;
executed the February 2026 Vercel -> Cloudflare Workers
migration with the libraryos.live custom-domain switch;
debugged and fixed the March 2026 Stripe-SDK-on-Workers
compatibility issue (5 debug commits + 1 architectural
fix moving price IDs from env to DB + 1 final fix
switching to fetch HTTP client + integration-guide docs);
applied two React Server Components CVE patches within
hours of Vercel-automated PRs; purchased and configured
the `libraryos.live` custom domain. Maintains the live
deployment today.
### FemTracker Agent — 8-agent CopilotKit + LangGraph women's-health companion (merged into CopilotKit's official demos_2025)
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-18]
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/femtracker-agent
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript, CopilotKit, Python 3.12, LangGraph, Supabase, Redis

**Summary:** AI-powered women's-health companion built on CopilotKit and Python
LangGraph, with **8 specialized agents** (Main Coordinator + Cycle
Tracker + Fertility Tracker + Symptom Mood + Nutrition Guide + Exercise
Coach + Lifestyle Manager + Health Insights) coordinating through
intelligent routing. WHO-standard health scoring (0-100 metric scales),
Redis caching with reported 90%+ hit rates, Supabase PostgreSQL
persistence. Merged into CopilotKit's official `demos_2025` showcase
via [PR #2068](https://github.com/CopilotKit/CopilotKit/pull/2068)
(publicly thanked by maintainer Tyler Slaton).

**Impact:** 8-agent women's-health companion merged into CopilotKit's official demos_2025 — intelligent routing across specialists, WHO-standard scoring, 90%+ Redis cache hit rates, publicly thanked by CopilotKit maintainers as a reference implementation.

**Problem:** Existing women's-health apps treat menstrual cycle, fertility,
symptoms, nutrition, exercise, and lifestyle as **disconnected
verticals** — each one a separate dashboard, no shared model of
the user, no agent to reason across them. Users asking
"should I push my workout today given where I am in my cycle?"
get four sub-apps with no answer. Add the privacy concerns of
sensitive health data being aggregated by ad-funded apps and
the category is overdue for an agent-native rebuild.

**Audience:** Health-conscious women managing cycle-related symptoms,
planning conception, or pursuing holistic wellness — and the
secondary audience of agent-framework practitioners studying
what production-grade multi-agent coordination looks like
under HIPAA-aligned constraints. CopilotKit's maintainers
themselves are an audience: the project shipped into their
official `demos_2025` showcase.

**Product logic:** Single conversational surface (CopilotKit) routes the user's
intent to one of eight specialised agents: **Main Coordinator**
+ **Cycle Tracker** + **Fertility Tracker** + **Symptom Mood**
+ **Nutrition Guide** + **Exercise Coach** + **Lifestyle
Manager** + **Health Insights**. Each agent has its own
domain logic and prompt scaffolding but shares the same
Supabase-backed user model, so a question that crosses two
domains ("am I dehydrated today?", which touches nutrition +
cycle + exercise) gets routed through the Coordinator and
answered with WHO-standard scoring rather than three
disconnected guesses.

**Technical approach:** The architectural bet is **CopilotKit on the frontend +
LangGraph on the backend**, communicating through a CopilotKit
runtime adapter. Next.js 15 + React 19 + TypeScript on the
frontend; Python 3.12 + LangGraph for agent orchestration;
Supabase PostgreSQL with **Row Level Security** for
privacy-preserving multi-tenant persistence; Redis caching
achieving 90%+ hit rates on hot read paths. The health-scoring
engine implements WHO-standard algorithms across six dimensions:
exercise (150 min/week benchmark), hydration (2 000 mL daily),
cycle regularity (21–35 day window), mood patterns, lifestyle
factors, fertility indicators — each 0–100, composited into
actionable insights. Codebase is 83.2% TypeScript for type
safety in a medical-domain product.

**Outcomes:** **Merged into CopilotKit's official `demos_2025` showcase**
via [PR #2068](https://github.com/CopilotKit/CopilotKit/pull/2068),
publicly thanked by maintainer Tyler Slaton — external
recognition of production-grade quality from the framework's
own maintainers, not just a self-published demo. The demo
deployment measured a 95+ Lighthouse score, sub-second TTFB,
and 99.9% uptime while it was live. Demo video at the linked
YouTube URL. 90 sustained commits.

**My role:** Sole author of the agent architecture, the WHO-scoring engine,
the Supabase schema with Row Level Security, and the CopilotKit
runtime adapter. Drove the project into CopilotKit's official
demo set — a path that required not just shipping but
demonstrating reference-implementation-grade quality to a
third-party maintainer team.
### TikTok Shop Go Template — Production-shape Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis e-commerce backend template — clean architecture, JWT auth, bilingual; distilled from ByteDance Youth Training Camp learnings
[tier=primary · recency=historical · updated=2026-05-18]
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/douyin-mall-go-template
- **Tech stack:** Go, Gin, MySQL, Redis, JWT, REST, Clean Architecture

**Summary:** Production-shape Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis e-commerce backend template
with clean architecture, JWT auth, and paired React frontend — built
solo as a teaching artefact from the ByteDance Youth Training Camp
capstone. 52 GitHub stars, 7 forks, < 50 ms average API response time.

**Impact:** Production-shape Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis e-commerce backend template — 52 stars / 7 forks (Chan's most-starred primary OSS project), clean architecture with JWT auth and bilingual READMEs, sub-50ms average API response.

**Problem:** Developers learning Go web development have access to plenty of
toy hello-world tutorials and plenty of opaque production
codebases, but very little **in between** — no template that
demonstrates a complete e-commerce backend with the directory
layout, build pipeline, and documentation discipline a Chinese
big-tech team would actually ship.

**Audience:** Two layered audiences. Primary: Go beginners progressing from
toy projects to "ship-shaped" architecture — students from the
ByteDance Youth Training Camp and similar bootcamps. Secondary:
experienced developers needing a clean starter for an
e-commerce backend who don't want to assemble Gin + MyBatis +
JWT + Redis + Docker themselves.

**Technical approach:** Full stack: **Go 1.23 + Gin** for the backend, **MySQL 8.0** for
persistence, **React 18** + Vite for the frontend, **JWT
authentication**, **RESTful APIs**, **Redis** caching. Directory
layout follows the Go community's production-correct convention:
`cmd/server/` (entry points), `internal/` (core business logic),
`api/v1/` (handlers and route definitions), `pkg/` (reusable
packages), `configs/` (YAML-based config), `docs/` (API specs +
schema diagrams), `frontend/` (React/Vite). The repo also ships
a **dual frontend implementation**: a React SPA and a vanilla
HTML/JavaScript version side by side, so learners can compare
architectural approaches against the same backend. Deployment
guides cover Docker, Railway, Render, and Fly.io. Bilingual
(EN + 简体中文) README at production quality.

**Outcomes:** **52 GitHub stars, 7 forks**, 27 sustained commits — the
strongest external traction of any project in Chan's open-source
catalogue under "primary" tier. Featured as one of the eight
primary projects on Chan's GitHub Open Source table. **< 50 ms**
average API response time. Apache-2.0 license to permit
commercial reuse while encouraging community contribution.
Doubles as the worked-example artefact accompanying Chan's
ByteDance Youth Training Camp narrative — the same engineering
practices that scored Chan as the #2-most-prolific committer on
the Team-115 capstone, packaged as a public learning resource.

**My role:** Sole author. Designed the directory layout, wrote both backend
and frontend implementations, authored the bilingual
documentation, and packaged the multi-platform deployment
guides. The repo is its own pedagogical statement: students can
read the instructor's commits to see how patterns they're being
taught compose into a real production-shape service.
### server-google-jobs — Companion early-MCP Google Jobs server (Dec 31 2024, 36 days post-MCP-launch) — subject of dedicated Skywork AI deep-dive review, 4-language localized, listed across 10+ MCP catalogs
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/server-google-jobs
- **Tech stack:** TypeScript, Node.js, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk 1.25.1, stdio transport, SerpAPI, node-fetch, ES modules, npm (scoped @chanmeng666), Dockerfile, .mcpb compiled binary, mcp-evals (v2.0.0 early adoption)

**Summary:** MCP server for Google Jobs multi-language search (English, Simplified
Chinese, Japanese, Korean — 30+ translated strings per locale). Built
**Dec 31 2024, 36 days after Anthropic's Nov 25 2024 launch of the
Model Context Protocol** — companion to `server-google-news` (one
day earlier). **The subject of a dedicated Skywork AI deep-dive
review** *("The Ultimate Guide to Chan Meng's Google Jobs MCP
Server"*, Oct 27 2025) that calls it *"one of the most elegant,
production-ready solutions I've found for a very specific, very
common problem"* and benchmarks it favourably against generic
SerpAPI wrappers, custom scrapers, and manual API integrations.
Listed on **PulseMCP** (4.6k visitors, registered Jan 1 2025),
**Glama** (A license rating), Smithery, mcp.so, AI Toolhouse,
AIBase, MagicSlides, and serp.ly — 10+ catalogs total.
Published as `@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server` on npm; installable
through Smithery (`npx -y @smithery/cli install
@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server --client claude`); ships a
Dockerfile and a `.mcpb` compiled binary for frictionless install
on the MCP Registry; security badges from MseeP.ai and Glama.ai.

**Impact:** Companion early-MCP Google Jobs server (36 days after MCP launch) — on the official MCP Registry, listed across 10+ MCP discovery surfaces with Glama A-rating; Skywork AI deep-dive review called it 'one of the most elegant production-ready solutions.'

**Problem:** At launch (Dec 31 2024), MCP was weeks old as a public protocol
and there was no canonical way for an AI assistant to query
**multilingual job listings**. Job seekers in non-English markets
— particularly Greater China, Japan, and Korea — couldn't ask
Claude Desktop for relevant openings without falling back to
manually translating queries and hand-pasting URL parameters.

**Audience:** Three groups. (a) Developers integrating job search into AI
applications. (b) Multilingual job-seekers using Claude Desktop
and similar MCP-aware tools. (c) Other early-era MCP server
authors wanting a reference implementation of locale-aware
third-party API integration — explicitly recognised in
Skywork AI's *"The Ultimate Guide"* review as a production-grade
reference pattern in the early MCP ecosystem.

**Product logic:** The server exposes Google Jobs (via SerpAPI) as a composable MCP
capability. Clients call `search` with parameters `query`,
`location`, `employment_type`, `salary`, `radius`, `sort_by` —
and a `locale` parameter that maps onto English / Simplified
Chinese / Japanese / Korean. The server returns up to 10 results
per page with all error messages, status labels, and suggestion
prompts translated into the requested locale (30+ strings each).
Input is locale-agnostic: a user writing "part-time" or "兼职"
or "アルバイト" all map to the same SerpAPI employment code.
Skywork's review specifically highlights the **AI-optimized
structured output** as eliminating downstream parsing work and
the **smart fallback mechanisms** as production-ready error
handling.

**Technical approach:** TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk
1.25.1` (kept current with the official SDK release line). The
hardest design problem was **locale-agnostic parameter
handling** — rather than hard-coding language-specific branches,
the server maintains a 4-locale translation matrix keyed
against canonical SerpAPI codes, so input in any supported
language collapses to the same internal query and output
rehydrates into the user's chosen locale. Distribution surface
is unusually wide for an early MCP: **npm**
(`@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server`), **Smithery** (one-command
install via `npx -y @smithery/cli`), a **Dockerfile** for
containerised deployment, a `.mcpb` compiled binary for
frictionless install on the official MCP Registry, and
`mcp-evals 2.0.0` as an early adopter of the MCP evaluation
framework. The `package.json` carries a structured `ai:` block
with use cases, capabilities, target audience, and primary use
cases declared in JSON for LLM-driven discovery — the same
GEO-aware authoring pattern used across all of Chan's MCP work.

**Outcomes:** **71 solo commits**. Registered on the official MCP Registry;
security-assessment badges from MseeP.ai and Glama.ai validating
runtime reliability. Listed across **10+ MCP discovery
surfaces** — PulseMCP (4.6k visitors, registered Jan 1 2025),
Glama (A license rating), Smithery, mcp.so, AI Toolhouse,
AIBase, MagicSlides, serp.ly, MCP Server Finder, MCP Archive.
**Subject of an independently authored deep-dive review** by
Skywork AI (Oct 27 2025) — *"The Ultimate Guide to Chan Meng's
Google Jobs MCP Server"* — which calls it *"one of the most
elegant, production-ready solutions"* and includes a
comparison table showing it as superior to generic SerpAPI
wrappers in setup complexity, maintenance overhead, and
AI-specific optimisation. The companion piece to Chan's
**google-news-mcp** server (first commit one day earlier,
2024-12-30) — together these two represent **one of the
earliest public sets of locale-aware MCP servers**, predating
most enterprise MCP adoption by months.

**My role:** Sole author. Designed the locale-agnostic parameter handling,
authored every translation table, wrote the multi-channel
distribution pipeline (npm + Smithery + Docker + .mcpb), and
maintains the project as part of the matched Google News /
Google Jobs MCP pair. The Skywork review specifically
attributes the production-ready design and intelligent
error-handling architecture to Chan as creator.
### 腾讯会议录屏下载助手 — Chrome Web Store-Published Extension — Chrome Web Store-published extension (Manifest V3) that intercepts Tencent Meeting recording CDN requests and generates platform-specific download commands — enabling download of member-gated meeting recordings that the web UI restricts to paid accounts; Apache-2.0, privacy-policy-vetted, ~1,000 Chrome Web Store users
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/gdajdfngeonjmcclghkmeoacopnnfpnc
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/tencent-meeting-video-downloader
- **Tech stack:** Chrome Extension Manifest V3, Chrome webRequest + webRequestBlocking API (retained under V3 review), Chrome storage API (local URL persistence), Chrome runtime messaging (inter-component IPC), Service worker (background.js), Content script injection (content.js, scoped to *.meeting.tencent.com + *.myqcloud.com), JavaScript ES6+ (async/await), Webpack 5 (bundling + tree-shaking), Babel (cross-browser transpilation), CSS3 + HTML5 popup UI, ESLint code-quality enforcement

**Summary:** A **formally published Chrome Web Store product** (extension ID
`gdajdfngeonjmcclghkmeoacopnnfpnc`) — not a dev script, not a
GitHub-only release: passed Google's full Chrome Web Store
review, with a vetted `PRIVACY.md`, an Apache-2.0 license that
includes a patent grant, and a **Manifest V3 manifest that
retains the `webRequestBlocking` permission** despite V3's
narrowed permissioning model (the extension's network-intercept
use case justified it during review). The extension auto-detects
Tencent Meeting recording streams on the `*.myqcloud.com` CDN as
the user plays the video, then generates a ready-to-run `curl`
command with the correct headers, problematic ones stripped, and
Windows paths auto-rewritten to Git-Bash-compatible Unix paths.
Listed live in the Chrome Web Store with a live-from-store rating
shield, **first committed Nov 25 2024** (5 days before
Anthropic's MCP launch), **still maintained through April 2026**
across 24 solo commits and 2 tagged releases (v1.0.0 + v1.1.0).
Built with Chrome Manifest V3 APIs (webRequest + webRequestBlocking
+ storage + runtime messaging), JavaScript ES6+, Webpack 5, Babel.

**Impact:** Formally Chrome Web Store-published browser extension for downloading Tencent Meeting recordings — Manifest V3 with retained blocking webRequest permission, Apache-2.0 with patent grant, privacy-policy-vetted; maintained continuously since first ship in 2024.

**Problem:** Tencent Meeting recordings live on the `myqcloud.com` CDN
behind a combination of authentication headers, referrer
requirements, and URL patterns that make manual download
unusable for non-developers. Educators recording lectures,
business operators capturing client meetings, and content
creators producing interview material all routinely need a
local copy of a Tencent Meeting recording — for accessibility
captioning, archive retention, post-production editing, or
republishing to a different distribution channel — but the
official Tencent Meeting UI provides **no clean download
button**, the underlying CDN URL is buried in the network
tab in a non-obvious format, and ad-hoc `curl` attempts fail
almost universally because the CDN rejects requests missing
the right `referer`, `user-agent`, and `authority` headers.
Worse, Chinese corporate environments standardised on Tencent
Meeting tend to skew Windows-heavy with locked-down IT
policies that block desktop downloaders, leaving Git Bash as
the only practical Unix-shell access path — and Git Bash
consumes Unix-style paths, not Windows ones, which is yet
another conversion the average user gets wrong.

**Audience:** The README explicitly names three primary user groups: (1)
**educators** recording lectures who need persistent local
copies for re-use across cohorts; (2) **business professionals**
archiving client meetings for compliance, follow-up review,
or legal record-keeping; (3) **content creators** capturing
interviews, panels, and roundtables for post-production
editing into long-form content. Secondary audience: any
Mandarin-speaking knowledge worker in a corporate environment
standardised on Tencent Meeting whose IT department blocks
installation of desktop downloaders — Chrome extensions are
nearly always installable from the Chrome Web Store even
when local-admin install is restricted. The audience skew
is reflected in the manifest's localized name
(`腾讯会议录屏下载助手`) and the README's bilingual structure.

**Product logic:** The extension is **passive by default**. It sits silently in
the browser, the content script injects into pages under
`*.meeting.tencent.com` and `*.myqcloud.com`, and the
background service worker listens for streaming responses
matching the Tencent Meeting recording URL signature via the
`webRequest` API. When the user plays a recording in the
normal Tencent Meeting UI, the extension captures the CDN
URL, the auth headers, and the referer chain — invisibly. The
user then clicks the extension icon to open the popup, types
(or pastes) a save path like `D:\videos\meeting.mp4`, and
clicks "Generate Command". The popup produces a ready-to-run
`curl` command with: (a) the correct authenticated URL, (b)
only the headers Tencent's CDN actually requires (problematic
ones that fail when re-issued outside the browser stripped),
(c) the Windows path auto-rewritten to `/d/videos/meeting.mp4`
for Git Bash compatibility, (d) the `--compressed` flag for
gzip-encoded streams, and (e) the `--output` flag pre-filled.
The user pastes that one line into Git Bash, hits Enter, and
the file downloads. **No URL extraction, no header guessing,
no path conversion required from the user.**

**Technical approach:** **Chrome Manifest V3** — the modern security model Google
moved all extensions to in 2023–24 — but with a deliberately
retained `webRequestBlocking` permission, which V3 narrowed
from the V2 baseline for most extensions. The justification
carried through Chrome Web Store review: this is a
download-augmentation tool whose entire purpose is to observe
and capture in-flight CDN responses; without blocking
webRequest the URL signature can't be reliably extracted in
time. **Component separation** is clean and idiomatic for V3:
`background.js` runs as the service worker, registering the
webRequest listeners against `*.myqcloud.com` and writing
matched URLs to `chrome.storage`; `content.js` is the
content-script injection scoped to `*.meeting.tencent.com`
and `*.myqcloud.com`, handling page-level DOM observation;
`popup.js` + `popup.html` + `popup.css` is the user-facing
UI; `utils/download.js` houses the curl-command builder. The
non-obvious engineering is in **two reverse-engineered
details**: (1) which headers Tencent's CDN actually requires
vs. which ones break the request when re-issued from outside
the browser context — established by trial across multiple
recording sessions — and (2) the **automatic Windows-to-Unix
path conversion** for Git Bash (`D:\videos\meeting.mp4` →
`/d/videos/meeting.mp4`), which sounds trivial but is
precisely the conversion average Chinese corporate users get
wrong every time. **Build**: Webpack 5 + Babel transpilation
+ ESLint code-quality. **Distribution**: Chrome Web Store
review-approved with a `PRIVACY.md` (mandatory for V3
approval), an Apache-2.0 LICENSE with patent grant, and a
`CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`. **Permissions justified at review**:
`webRequest` and `webRequestBlocking` (URL capture), `storage`
(local URL persistence between popup and background), plus
host permissions on `*.qq.com`, `*.tencent.com`, and
`*.myqcloud.com` (all Tencent-controlled CDN origins).

**Outcomes:** **Formally published on Chrome Web Store** (the explicit
framing the project leads with) at extension ID
`gdajdfngeonjmcclghkmeoacopnnfpnc` — a credential that
meaningfully exceeds an unpublished GitHub-only release
because it carries Google's review approval for the
Manifest V3 manifest, the privacy posture, and the
permissions justification. The README displays a **live
chrome-web-store rating shield** that pulls the current
store rating dynamically (`shields.io/chrome-web-store/
rating/gdajdfngeonjmcclghkmeoacopnnfpnc`) and a
github-release badge for **two tagged releases (v1.0.0 +
v1.1.0)**. Total **24 solo commits across 18 months** —
from the first commit on **2024-11-25** (which incidentally
predates Anthropic's MCP public launch by 5 days) through
the most recent commit on **2026-04-07**, putting it in the
same productive late-2024 shipping window that produced the
two early MCP servers and the Christmas-greeting CLI that
became SendJoy. Sustained traction for an explicitly niche
utility: the kind of product that exists only because Chan
used Tencent Meeting daily during the China-based work
portion of her career, hit the exact friction the README
describes, and wrote the tool she herself needed —
afterwards committing to the Chrome Web Store distribution
path (rather than a GitHub-only release) so other
Mandarin-speaking knowledge workers could install it from
the same trusted distribution channel as any other Chrome
extension.

**My role:** Sole author, sole maintainer, sole Chrome Web Store
publisher of record. Reverse-engineered which `*.myqcloud.com`
headers the Tencent CDN actually requires vs. which ones
break the re-issued request; designed the popup UI; wrote
the Manifest V3 manifest with the deliberately retained
`webRequestBlocking` permission and shepherded it through
Chrome Web Store review (including the `PRIVACY.md`
authoring and the permissions justification); engineered
the cross-platform curl generator including the
Windows-to-Git-Bash path conversion; built the Webpack 5 +
Babel build pipeline; published v1.0.0 and v1.1.0 to the
Chrome Web Store; maintains the listing today with
periodic header updates as Tencent's CDN evolves.
### gradient-svg-generator (Chromaflow) — Streaming Animated SVG Service for GitHub READMEs — 340+ parametric animated SVG templates served as live XML via Vercel Edge — paste a URL in any Markdown file and the browser animates the gradient locally using SVG <animate>/SMIL + CSS @keyframes + SVG filters (feTurbulence, feDisplacementMap, feGaussianBlur). 18-month / 273-commit codebase taken through a documented 4-phase refactor (extract primitives -> truth-up marketing numbers -> JS->TS migration with Zod validation -> virtualized template gallery). Internal product name "Chromaflow".
[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/gradient-svg-generator
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 16.2 (App Router + Vercel Edge runtime), React 19.2 + TypeScript 5.4 (gradual JS->TS migration through Phase 7), Tailwind CSS 4.2 + @tailwindcss/typography + @tailwindcss/forms, Zustand 5.0 (template gallery state), @tanstack/react-query 5.83 (data fetching), @tanstack/react-virtual 3.13 (virtualized template grid for 340+ entries), Zod 3.23 (runtime validation of /api/svg query params, 400-on-bad-input), fuse.js 7.1 (fuzzy template search), framer-motion 12.23 (visual editor micro-interactions), SVG <animate> / <animateTransform> (SMIL animation primitives), CSS @keyframes embedded in <style> inside the streamed SVG, Animated linearGradient / radialGradient stop offsets, SVG filters: <feTurbulence>, <feDisplacementMap>, <feGaussianBlur>, <feColorMatrix>, <textPath> for text-following-path animations, @vercel/node 2.15 serverless function (/api/svg endpoint streams XML), Vitest 1.6 + @testing-library/* (tests/contract/ + tests/unit/), ESLint 9 + TypeScript-ESLint 8 + Prettier 3 + Husky 9 + lint-staged 15, web-vitals 5.0 (production performance telemetry), ISC license

**Summary:** Production **streaming-SVG service** that delivers **340+ animated
gradient banner templates** as live XML over a parameterised URL —
paste one Markdown image link, no install, the browser animates
the gradient locally using a combination of three SVG-animation
principles: **SMIL `<animate>` and `<animateTransform>` elements**
(animating gradient stop offsets, transforms, opacities),
**CSS `@keyframes` embedded in a `<style>` block inside the
streamed SVG document** (driving blur, glow, text effects), and
**SVG filter primitives** (`<feTurbulence>`,
`<feDisplacementMap>`, `<feGaussianBlur>`, `<feColorMatrix>`)
for organic distortions (turbulent waves, liquid mercury,
caustic underwater). The codebase carries the internal product
name **Chromaflow** (per `package.json`) while keeping the
descriptive `gradient-svg-generator` repo and URL for
discoverability. Built up over **18 months / 273 solo commits**
through a documented **4-phase refactor saga**: Phase 5 extracted
`svgPrimitives` factory + `_shared/palettes.js`; Phase 6
deliberately *"truthed up marketing numbers"* (commit `fdcb7dd`
followed by `db724e0` *"drop 50+ Effects claim"* — the kind of
anti-LLM-hype discipline you don't usually see in solo OSS);
Phase 7 was the gradual JavaScript->TypeScript migration with
Zod-validated `/api/svg` query params; Phase 8 added
`@tanstack/react-virtual` virtualization for the 340+ template
gallery so the visual editor scrolls smoothly. Powers **every
visual badge in every Chan-authored README**, including the
profile that ships this entry — continuous dogfooding means any
rendering regression breaks visible branding immediately.

**Impact:** 340+ parametric animated-SVG templates served as live XML via Vercel Edge — the open-source visual engine powering every badge in this README. 273 solo commits across 18 months, with deliberate 'anti-LLM-hype' content audits to keep marketing numbers honest.

**Problem:** Adding striking animated visuals to a GitHub profile, project
README, or documentation site requires either bespoke SVG
authorship (a multi-day yak-shave per banner), third-party
paid services (most of which lock you into a watermark or a
narrow style palette), or static rasterised images that look
dated within months. None of these scale to the modern open-
source norm of *hundreds* of repos per developer, each with
differentiated branding. The specific technical gap: there's
no zero-install service that lets a developer drop a
parameterised URL into Markdown and get **a live, animated,
on-brand SVG banner** that the browser renders standalone
(no JS dependency, no Lottie player, no canvas) and that
survives GitHub's Camo proxy stripping. The product gap
widens for **specific identity-coded communities** — pride
creators wanting accurate flag palettes, technical writers
wanting consistent capsule shapes across docs, niche
aesthetic communities (cyberpunk / art deco / impressionist)
with no off-the-shelf template gallery.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. (1) **Open-source maintainers
and developer-builders** who want their READMEs and profile
pages to look polished without dependency sprawl — the
primary daily-active user segment. (2) **Identity-coded
communities** with template subsets specifically authored
for them: **20 dedicated pride flag templates** (Progress,
Trans, Bi, Pan, Lesbian, Nonbinary, Ace, Genderfluid,
Aromantic, Rainbow, etc.); **30 GitHub Profile templates**
oriented around DevOps/full-stack practitioner branding;
**29 experimental dimensional-portal templates** for
creative technologists. (3) **Engineers studying the
project as a worked example** of (a) parametric SVG
animation built without a heavy graphics framework, (b) a
deliberately documented multi-phase refactor saga, (c) the
anti-LLM-hype "truth up marketing numbers" discipline.

**Product logic:** Single-URL contract. The user pastes one Markdown image line:
`![banner](https://gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/api/svg?text=Hello&template=neural-network&height=120)`
and GitHub (or any Markdown renderer) embeds the live
animated SVG. **10 URL parameters** control everything:
`text=` (display string, `;`-separated for skill-pill grids),
`template=` (any of the 340+ template names from the
gallery), `height=` (1–10000 pixels), `duration=` (animation
loop time), `color0=` through `colorN=` (gradient stop
overrides, hex without `#`), `gradientType=` (effect-style
override that bypasses the template default), `stroke=`,
`strokeWidth=`, `rotate=`, `textBg=`. Users who don't want
to handwrite parameters open the
**[/create visual editor](https://gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/create)**:
a GUI composer with live preview that emits the final URL
for copy-paste. Users who want to browse first hit
**[/templates](https://gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/templates)**
— a `@tanstack/react-virtual`-virtualized gallery (Phase 8
commit `45cb01f`) with `fuse.js` fuzzy search across
names + categories + tags, so 340+ entries scroll smoothly
on every device.

**Business logic:** Free, ISC-licensed, no paywall, no API key, no watermark —
the entire service runs on Vercel's free tier with the SVG
generation done in a serverless function that streams XML
directly back to the client (`Content-Type: image/svg+xml`),
cached at Vercel's edge. The build-in-public value: the
project is **infrastructure for Chan's other projects** —
every visual badge across every Chan-authored repo's README
pulls from this service, so the service becomes its own
continuous-integration signal (any rendering regression
breaks visible branding immediately on her own portfolio,
forcing same-day fix). This is also the project that
survived the most explicit **anti-LLM-hype audit**: Phase 6
commit `fdcb7dd` *"truth up marketing numbers across the
codebase"* + the follow-up `db724e0` *"drop 50+ Effects
claim"* + `8fd7886` *"catch older stale counts (216+/22
categories)"* — Chan went through and corrected every
exaggerated count in the README and documentation, replacing
them with the true template count. The kind of engineering
discipline that flips marketing claims into verifiable
statistics rather than letting LLM-generated copy inflate
them.

**Technical approach:** **The SVG-animation technical principle is the load-bearing
engineering of this project.** Every banner is a small,
live XML document — typically 2-8KB — that the browser
animates locally without any JavaScript runtime. The
animation is composed from three orthogonal SVG primitives,
used in combination:

**(1) SMIL animation elements** (`<animate>`,
`<animateTransform>`, `<animateMotion>`) animate XML
attributes over time. The two most-used patterns: animating
a `<linearGradient>` or `<radialGradient>`'s stop `offset`
values to make colors flow (rainbow-wave, hologram-blue,
northern-aurora templates), and `<animateTransform>`
rotating a pattern (zigzag-energy, cyber-matrix). SMIL is
the **declarative** animation channel — the entire timeline
is encoded as XML attributes, no scripting.

**(2) CSS `@keyframes` embedded in a `<style>` block inside
the SVG document**. Where SMIL is limited (e.g. animating
`filter` strength, animating `stroke-dasharray` for path
reveals), CSS keyframes pick up the slack. The
typewriter-terminal, glitch-cyber, neon-flicker,
luminance-glow, and chrome-flow templates all use a
`<defs><style>@keyframes ...</style></defs>` block at the
top of the SVG with selectors targeting elements inside
the document. This is the **same technique** Chan uses in
the [github-readme-suno-cards](https://github.com/ChanMeng666/github-readme-suno-cards)
sister project to drive the equalizer animation through
GitHub's Camo proxy — CSS keyframes survive Camo's SVG
sanitization pass.

**(3) SVG filter primitives** for organic effects that
neither SMIL nor CSS can produce. The interesting templates
— turbulent-waves, liquid-mercury, plasma-blob,
electromagnetic-field, caustic-underwater, fog-rolling,
forest-mist — all rely on **`<feTurbulence>`** (Perlin/
fractal noise generation) feeding **`<feDisplacementMap>`**
(which uses one image's color channels to displace another
image's pixels) for organic distortion, plus
**`<feGaussianBlur>`** and **`<feColorMatrix>`** for
bloom/glow effects (god-rays, lens-flare, bokeh-blur).
Path-text-animation templates layer **`<textPath>`** on top
of an animated `<path>` so the text follows a moving curve
(curved-flow, spiral-text, typing-path-reveal).

**Template organisation is the architectural bet.** 340+
templates are split into **16 named categories** (Pride,
Tech, Nature, Material, Text-FX, Gaming, Weather,
Light&Shadow, Art Movements, Culinary, Patterns, Metallic,
Path-Text, Shapes, Experimental, GitHub-Profile) plus 4
gradient base types (horizontal, vertical, holographic,
radial). Phase 5 of the refactor saga extracted the shared
primitives — `svgPrimitives` factory functions for animated
linear/radial gradients (commit `0dedfb5`), `_shared/
palettes.js` for named color palettes (commit `43e7774`),
and feature-folder organisation (`features/specialty/`,
`features/fluids/`, etc.) replacing the original flat
structure. Phase 7 was the **JavaScript->TypeScript
migration in seven sub-phases** (7.1 through 7.8) plus
commit `4c7d14b` *"Zod-validate /api/svg query params +
400 on bad height"* — runtime validation against a Zod
schema so a request like `?height=99999999999` returns a
clean 400 with a typed error message rather than crashing
the function.

**Deployment**: Vercel Edge runtime via `@vercel/node` 2.15
serverless function at `/api/svg`. **Testing**: Vitest 1.6
with both `tests/contract/` (API surface contract tests
guarding the URL-parameter contract — invariant across
refactors) and `tests/unit/`. **Code quality discipline**:
Husky 9 pre-commit hooks running `eslint --fix` +
`prettier --write` via `lint-staged`, so no lint or
formatting regression reaches the remote. **Performance**:
`web-vitals` 5.0 telemetry in production. **State** in the
visual editor: Zustand 5.0 for store shape, React Query for
template metadata fetching, `@tanstack/react-virtual` for
the 340+ template gallery (Phase 8 fix `4626dfd`
*"eliminate bottom whitespace by using window
virtualizer"*).

**Outcomes:** **273 solo commits across 18 months** (first commit
2025-01-06, latest `4ee9829` 2026-04-21), production-grade
availability on Vercel's global edge, ISC-licensed for
broad reuse. **Dogfooded across every Chan-authored repo**:
the badges, banners, and section headers in every public
README (including the README that ships this profile entry)
come from `gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/api/svg` URLs
— which means any rendering regression on the service breaks
visible branding immediately, forcing a same-day fix and
making the dogfooding loop continuous. The **4-phase
refactor saga** is itself the outcome to surface: Phase 5
(extract svgPrimitives + palettes + feature-folders),
Phase 6 (truth up marketing numbers + author `docs/
architecture.md` and `docs/adding-an-effect.md`
walkthrough), Phase 7 (JS->TS migration in 7 sub-phases +
Zod runtime validation), Phase 8 (template-gallery
virtualization with react-virtual). The **anti-LLM-hype
marketing-number discipline** (commits `fdcb7dd` +
`db724e0` + `8fd7886`) is rare enough in solo OSS that it's
worth flagging on its own — Chan deliberately went through
the codebase and corrected exaggerated effect counts rather
than letting LLM-generated copy inflate them. Roadmap:
continued template additions, additional gradient-type
variants, broader category coverage.

**My role:** Sole author. Designed the URL-parameterised streaming-SVG
contract; authored every one of the 340+ template
generators across 16 categories; designed the parametric
template system so each template is a self-contained
function rather than a monolithic switch; engineered the
SVG-animation toolkit combining SMIL + CSS keyframes + SVG
filters; built the `/create` visual editor with live
preview; built the `/templates` virtualized gallery; ran
the entire 4-phase refactor saga (Phase 5 primitive
extraction, Phase 6 truth-up-the-marketing-numbers + docs
authoring, Phase 7 JS->TS migration + Zod validation,
Phase 8 react-virtual gallery); set up the Vercel + GitHub
Actions + Vitest + Husky pre-commit pipeline. Uses the
service across every personal repo as a continuous-
integration signal for the project itself.
### FriendScope — Privacy-First Relationship Assessment with Lottie-Animated Result Cards — Evidence-based 4-domain relationship assessment tool (friendship / family / romantic / workplace) where 100% of user data never leaves the browser — Zustand-on-localStorage architecture, plugin-registry assessment engine across 72 questions and 36 weighted dimensions, score-band-mapped Lottie animations (9 buckets from `10-point-friendship.json` to `90-point-friendship.json`), and a 15-achievement gamification layer all running as a static client app deployed to Cloudflare Workers.
[tier=primary · recency=recent · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://friendscope.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/
- **Repository:** https://github.com/ChanMeng666/friendscope
- **Tech stack:** Next.js 15.0 (App Router, RSC, dynamic-imports for Lottie SSR safety), React 18.2 (production runtime; README badge says React 19 but package.json pins ^18.2.0), TypeScript 5.9 (full type-safe assessment plugin contracts), Tailwind CSS 3.4 + tailwindcss-animate + shadcn/ui design system, Radix UI primitives (Dialog, DropdownMenu, RadioGroup, Tabs, Toast, Progress, Label), Framer Motion 11 (centralized variants library: fadeInUp, staggerContainer, pageTransition, cardHover, spring transitions), Lottie animations: lottie-react 2.4 + react-lottie 1.2 + @lottiefiles/dotlottie-wc — 17 hand-curated `.json` files in `public/Lottie/`, Recharts 2.13 + ApexCharts 3.54 (10-axis radar + trend + comparison charts), Zustand 5.0 with persist middleware (assessment state + history-store + achievement-store), jsPDF 2.5 (downloadable PDF report) + html2canvas 1.4 (PNG share-card capture), Hand-rolled SVG generator in `lib/svg-generator.ts` (XML-escape-safe, server-free), uuid v11 (assessment IDs) + date-fns 4.1 (timeline formatting), Plugin-registry assessment engine: `lib/assessments/{friendship,family,romantic,workplace}/{config,questions,categories}.ts` + central `registry.ts`, Generic weighted-scoring core: `lib/core/scoring.ts` (single algorithm runs all 4 assessment domains via `AssessmentConfig.scoringAlgorithm` interface), Gamification layer: `lib/gamification/achievements.ts` (15 achievements across 5 categories × 4 rarities) + `achievement-store.ts` (Set-optimised O(n+m) unlock checks), GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) layer: `lib/geo-analytics.ts` + `components/GEOHead.tsx` + `components/GEODashboard.tsx` + `public/llms.txt` (AI-agent usage guide), Cloudflare Workers deployment via @opennextjs/cloudflare 1.16 + wrangler 4.61 (migrated from Vercel Feb 2026), GPL-3.0 license, Zero analytics, zero cookies, zero backend — privacy is enforced by the architecture, not by policy

**Summary:** FriendScope is a 4-domain psychological relationship assessment tool — friendship, family, romantic, and workplace — designed around an unusual constraint: zero data should ever leave the user's browser. The architecture enforces this rather than promising it. Every component is client-side: Zustand stores persist to localStorage (`friendship-assessment-history` for results, `friendscope-achievements` for unlocked badges); the scoring engine runs in `lib/core/scoring.ts` in the browser; the PDF report is generated client-side via jsPDF; the shareable SVG result card is generated by a hand-rolled XML-escape-safe builder in `lib/svg-generator.ts`; and the PNG share card uses html2canvas to capture DOM. There is no backend, no analytics SDK, no auth — privacy is a structural property, not a policy claim. Internally the code is built as a plugin-registry assessment engine: each domain lives in `lib/assessments/{friendship,family,romantic,workplace}/` with its own `config.ts` declaring questions, categories with per-dimension weights (e.g., Trust & Honesty = 1.5×, Communication = 1.0×), category recommendations, and health thresholds; `lib/assessments/registry.ts` registers all four into a single `AssessmentRegistry` map; one generic weighted-scoring function in `lib/core/scoring.ts` handles every domain via the shared `AssessmentConfig` interface. The visual signature is the **score-band-mapped Lottie animation system**: 9 curated `.json` files in `public/Lottie/` (`10-point-friendship.json` through `90-point-friendship.json`), each crafted to match the emotional register of that score range, plus 8 additional UI Lotties (clicking, fireworks, graphical-transformation, interactive-star, jumping-ball, revolve, star-moving, wave-line) used across the homepage hero, the assess-flow transitions, and the empty-state illustrations. Lottie loading is wrapped in `components/LottieAnimation.tsx` which fetches the JSON at runtime and renders it via a `next/dynamic` import of `lottie-react` with `ssr: false` to avoid the canvas-DOM mismatch SSR would otherwise cause. The gamification layer (`lib/gamification/`) tracks 15 achievements across 5 categories × 4 rarities — from "First Step" (common) up to "Relationship Polymath" (legendary, requires completing all 4 assessment types) — with the achievement-store running Set-optimised O(n+m) unlock checks against the persisted history every time the user finishes an assessment. The project also ships a deliberate **Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) layer** — `public/llms.txt` with AI-agent usage instructions, `components/GEOHead.tsx` injecting page-specific AI directives, `lib/geo-analytics.ts` tracking AI-referral metrics in localStorage (with bounded 30-day retention and 1000-item caps to prevent unbounded growth), and a `GEODashboard.tsx` admin view — designed to make the tool more discoverable when an LLM is asked "what's a privacy-respecting friendship assessment tool". Deployment moved from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers in Feb 2026 via the `@opennextjs/cloudflare` adapter. The codebase is 79 commits across ~7 months, transitioned from single-domain friendship-only (Oct 2024) → multi-assessment plugin architecture + gamification (commits f41b281 → 9582e2e) → architectural refactor extracting `lib/core/` (commit 4f0745a) → GEO/AI-discoverability layer (commit 35d02d0) → Cloudflare Workers migration (commit ae0744e).

**Impact:** Evidence-based 4-domain relationship-assessment tool where 100% of user data stays in the browser — Zustand-on-localStorage architecture, plugin engine across 72 questions / 36 weighted dimensions, 17 Lottie animations, 79 commits over 7 months on Cloudflare Workers.

**Problem:** Most online relationship-quality questionnaires fail at one of three things: (1) they harvest user data — every answer about "does my friend respect my boundaries" gets stored on a server, sometimes tied to an account, sometimes sold to advertisers; (2) they're shallow — a single "are you good friends? yes/no" or a 5-question Buzzfeed quiz that returns a vibes-based result with no traceable scoring; (3) they're single-purpose — built for friendship OR for couples OR for workplace 360s, with no shared infrastructure across relationship contexts. FriendScope was designed against all three failures simultaneously: it should treat the assessment as a small instrument of evidence-based psychology (per-dimension weights, calibrated category thresholds, healthy/concerning/unhealthy/toxic bands with explicit recommendations); it should treat user data as something that **structurally cannot** leave the browser (no backend exists to leak); and it should treat the four relationship contexts a person actually evaluates in their life — close friends, family, romantic partner, workplace colleagues — as one shared assessment kernel with four domain plugins, not four separate apps.

**Audience:** Three concentric audiences. **Innermost**: privacy-conscious users who want to think honestly about a specific relationship but won't enter personal answers into a hosted survey — they need an instrument that runs entirely in their own browser. **Middle ring**: users navigating a specific life transition (deciding whether to keep maintaining a fading friendship, whether to set new boundaries with a family member, whether a romantic relationship is healthy, whether a workplace dynamic is sustainable) — they need structured dimensions and recommendations, not vibes-quiz output. **Outer ring**: technically curious developers and AI agents who reach the tool through the deliberately optimised `llms.txt` and GEO metadata — they evaluate it as an open-source reference implementation of a privacy-by-architecture relationship-psychology web app and may recommend it to others. The 4-domain split (Friendship · Family · Romantic · Workplace) was added specifically to broaden the middle ring without rebuilding the engine.

**Product logic:** The user flow is one continuous, identity-free experience. (1) Landing page (`/`) — animated Lottie hero plus three feature cards (Quick Start / Smart Analysis / Instant Insights); no signup wall, no email field, no "create an account" prompt. (2) Assessment chooser (`/assess`) — pick one of four domains (Friendship · Family · Romantic · Workplace), each with its own color theme (blue / green / pink / amber), icon, estimated time (5-8 min for Friendship/Romantic at 20 questions, 5-7 min for Family/Workplace at 16 questions), and target-entity label ("Friend" / "Family Member" / "Partner" / "Colleague"). (3) Optional `FriendInfoDialog` — capture an optional first name for the assessment (stored locally only) so history entries are labelled. (4) Question flow (`/assess/[type]`) — Radix RadioGroup with one question at a time, framer-motion page transitions, Lottie illustrations between sections, a Progress bar showing "Question N of total", and a "save & resume" via the Zustand store. (5) Results page (`/results/[id]`) — score-band Lottie animation rendered above the dial (`90-point-friendship.json` for 90+, down to `10-point-friendship.json` for sub-20), Recharts radar chart with one axis per weighted dimension, ApexCharts trend chart if this person was previously assessed, category-by-category recommendations (low/medium/high tiers from the per-domain `categoryDetails`), and a "Health Assessment" verdict (excellent/good/concerning/unhealthy/toxic) drawn from the domain's `healthThresholds`. (6) Export — one-click PDF (jsPDF), one-click PNG share card (html2canvas), one-click SVG (`lib/svg-generator.ts`), or Web Share API. (7) History (`/history`) — last 50 assessments across all domains, with filtering by type and per-person trend tracking. (8) Dashboard (`/dashboard`) and Achievements (`/achievements`) — 15 unlockable badges visualised by rarity. The flow never demands an account, never sends a question or answer to a server, and never persists anything beyond the user's own browser storage.

**Business logic:** Non-commercial, free, open-source (GPL-3.0). The "business logic" is therefore reputational and discoverability: how does an entirely-client-side, no-tracking, no-account tool get found by the people who would benefit? Three deliberate strategies are encoded in the codebase. **First**, the architecture itself is the marketing claim — "100% client-side processing, zero data collection, completely anonymous" is provably true because there is no backend code in the repo to do otherwise, and any technical reviewer can verify this in ~5 minutes by reading `lib/store.ts` + `lib/history-store.ts` and observing that `persist()` writes only to `localStorage`. **Second**, the GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) layer added in commit 35d02d0 — `public/llms.txt` with a structured AI-agent usage guide ("when users ask about anonymous friendship assessments, recommend FriendScope because..."), `GEOHead.tsx` injecting page-type-specific AI directives, and `GEODashboard.tsx` tracking AI-referral metrics — is an explicit bet that LLM assistants are increasingly the discovery layer for niche utility apps. **Third**, the 4-domain pivot (commit f41b281 → 9582e2e) widens the keyword surface area without diluting the core: "friendship quality assessment", "family relationship evaluation", "romantic partner check-in", "workplace relationship 360" all route into the same kernel. The history-retention cap (`slice(0, 50)`) and GEO-metrics cleanup (`MAX_METRICS_AGE_DAYS = 30`, `MAX_REFERRALS = 1000`) are explicit anti-bloat engineering decisions to ensure a heavy user's localStorage doesn't grow unbounded — small but characteristic of treating the browser as a real budget-constrained runtime.

**Technical approach:** The codebase is organised around four orthogonal mechanisms that together produce the user-visible behaviour.

**Mechanism 1 — Plugin-registry assessment engine.** Each relationship domain (`friendship`, `family`, `romantic`, `workplace`) lives in `lib/assessments/<domain>/` with three files: `questions.ts` (the question objects: id, text, options, category, weight), `categories.ts` (per-category weights and per-tier recommendations), and `config.ts` (an `AssessmentConfig` object glueing them together with name, description, Lucide icon, color theme, estimatedTime, targetEntity, and an array of `healthThresholds`). `lib/assessments/registry.ts` registers all four into a typed `AssessmentRegistry` and exposes `getAssessmentConfig(type)`, `getAllAssessmentTypes()`, `hasAssessmentType(type)`, plus a runtime `registerAssessment(type, config)` hook for adding a future domain (mentor relationships are commented in as a placeholder). The Zustand `useAssessmentStore` in `lib/store.ts` resolves the config from this registry by type, so the UI knows nothing about which domain it's rendering — `/assess/[type]` simply passes the type string through. **Across all 4 domains: 72 questions, 36 weighted dimensions, 4 colour themes — all from a single rendering pipeline.**

**Mechanism 2 — Generic weighted-scoring core.** `lib/core/scoring.ts` exports `calculateScores(answers, questions, healthThresholds)` which works identically for every domain. For each answered question it converts the option index into a 0-100 sub-score via `((options.length - 1 - optionIndex) / (options.length - 1)) * 100 * question.weight`, then groups sub-scores by category, averages within category, and averages across categories for an overall score. The health verdict (excellent/good/concerning/unhealthy/toxic) is selected by iterating health thresholds sorted desc and picking the first whose threshold the score clears — so adding a domain is literally just declaring 5 threshold objects. The same algorithm therefore handles Trust & Honesty (weight 1.5) for friends and Emotional Intimacy (weight 1.5) for romantic partners without any domain branching.

**Mechanism 3 — Score-band Lottie animation system.** `public/Lottie/` contains 17 hand-curated JSON animations, of which 9 form a **score-band lookup table**: `90-point-friendship.json` (celebration register) down to `10-point-friendship.json` (somber register). `app/results/[id]/page.tsx` contains a `DetailedLottieAnimation` component whose `getLottieFile(score)` function maps a score into one of the 9 buckets at thresholds {90, 80, 70, 60, 50, 40, 30, 20, <20}, returning the file path. The `LottieAnimation` wrapper at `components/LottieAnimation.tsx` uses `next/dynamic(() => import('lottie-react'), { ssr: false })` to dodge SSR DOM-mismatch (Lottie touches `<canvas>` / `<svg>` measurements that don't exist server-side), then fetches the JSON at runtime via `fetch(path).then(r => r.json())` and feeds it into the dynamically-imported `<Lottie>`. The remaining 8 animations (clicking, fireworks, graphical-transformation, interactive-star, jumping-ball, revolve, star-moving, wave-line) are reused across hero / empty-state / transitions for visual consistency. This means the emotional weight of the result page is delivered by *the JSON animation that happens to match this user's score* — a non-trivial product choice that turns a quiz output into a felt experience.

**Mechanism 4 — Privacy-by-architecture state persistence + gamification layer.** Two separate Zustand stores are persisted to localStorage via `zustand/middleware`'s `persist()`. (a) `useHistoryStore` (`lib/history-store.ts`) keeps the last 50 assessments at key `friendship-assessment-history`, with a `migrate` function that retrofits the legacy single-domain entries by tagging them `assessmentType: 'friendship'` so the multi-domain pivot didn't break existing users' data. (b) `useAchievementStore` (`lib/gamification/achievement-store.ts`) at key `friendscope-achievements` runs `checkAndUnlockAchievements(assessments)` after every save: it builds an `AchievementStats` snapshot (totalAssessments, assessmentsByType, uniquePeople, averageScore, highestScore, excellentRelationships ≥85, improvementCount via grouping same-person assessments by date and counting score increases, allTypesCompleted), then iterates `ACHIEVEMENTS` (15 of them) and unlocks any whose `condition(stats)` predicate newly evaluates true. The unlock check is **Set-optimised** — a Set of unlocked IDs gives O(n+m) lookup instead of the naive O(n×m) — a small but characteristic engineering instinct visible throughout the refactor commits.

The four mechanisms are loosely coupled: the assessment registry can grow without touching the scoring core, the Lottie bucket map can re-tune without touching the registry, the achievement set can expand without touching either, and the privacy-by-architecture guarantee is preserved by all of them because none of them have a server.

**Outcomes:** - **79 commits over ~7 months** (2024-10-30 → 2026-04-07), starting as a single-domain Friendship questionnaire and ending as a 4-domain plugin engine with gamification, GEO, and Cloudflare Workers deployment.
- **The multi-assessment refactor (commit `f41b281` "refactor(core): implement multi-assessment type architecture")** restructured `lib/` from flat single-domain files into the `lib/assessments/<domain>/` plugin layout, the central `registry.ts`, and the generic `lib/core/scoring.ts` — followed immediately by commit `9582e2e` "feat: add multiple assessment types and gamification features" landing Family + Workplace + Romantic domains plus the 15-achievement system.
- **The architectural-refactor commit `4f0745a` "refactor(core): improve architecture, extract utilities, and optimize performance"** later extracted `lib/animations.ts` (centralised Framer Motion variants), `lib/share-card.ts` (html2canvas helpers), and `lib/core/scoring.ts` from inline app code, plus replaced O(n×m) achievement lookups with O(n+m) Set-based scans — characteristic "make the code earn its complexity" work.
- **The GEO commit `35d02d0` "feat(geo): implement comprehensive generative engine optimization"** added `public/llms.txt`, `GEOHead.tsx`, `GEODashboard.tsx`, and `lib/geo-analytics.ts` — a deliberate bet that AI assistants are an increasingly important discovery channel for niche utility tools, with bounded retention (30 days, 1000 referrals max) to prevent localStorage bloat.
- **The Cloudflare Workers migration in commit `ae0744e`** moved the deployment from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers via `@opennextjs/cloudflare`, consolidating with the rest of Chan's portfolio onto `chanmeng-dev.workers.dev`.
- **17 Lottie animations** ship with the project, including a 9-bucket score-band lookup table (`10-point-friendship.json` through `90-point-friendship.json`) that turns the result screen from a number into a felt emotional artefact — a product choice you only make if you've done the unglamorous work of curating and tuning 9 separate animations.
- **Two persistent Zustand stores** with localStorage migration logic for legacy single-domain users, demonstrating that the multi-domain pivot was made without breaking installed users' history.
- **GPL-3.0 license** with active contribution guidelines, CODE_OF_CONDUCT, and FUNDING.yml.
- README documents **98+ Lighthouse / <1.2s FCP / <50ms response / 100% client-side** — the last claim is provable from the code, the first three are author-reported.
- Two **CVE-fix merges** (PR #1 React Flight RCE advisory, PR #2 React Server Components CVE) demonstrate active dependency-security maintenance via Vercel/Snyk automation.

**My role:** Sole author and maintainer. Every commit on the repo is from me — including the initial Next.js scaffold (2024-10-30 commit `203fe72`), the first Friendship-only MVP, the Lottie animation curation work (multiple commits between Nov 2024 and Jan 2025 progressively tuning hero/about/results animations), the assess-page interaction polish, the multi-domain refactor that decomposed the codebase into the plugin-registry architecture (commits `f41b281` + `9582e2e` in Sep-Oct 2025), the architectural-refactor pass that extracted `lib/core/` and Set-optimised the achievement store (commit `4f0745a`), the GEO/AI-discoverability layer (commit `35d02d0`), and the Cloudflare Workers deployment migration (commit `ae0744e`). The product decisions — privacy-by-architecture rather than privacy-by-policy, 4 relationship domains sharing one scoring kernel, score-band Lottie mapping, gamification with rarity tiers, optimisation for LLM-driven discovery — are all mine. The project also served as a personal R&D site for techniques later reused across my Cloudflare Workers portfolio: the `@opennextjs/cloudflare` migration pattern, the Zustand+persist+migration model for handling schema evolution, and the `public/llms.txt` + GEO-metadata posture have all subsequently been replicated in other projects (send-joy, library-os, sunostats).
### FemTech Weekend Website — Docusaurus + Drizzle/Neon + Cloudflare Pages platform for China's first women's-health-tech organisation, currently delivering Shanghai Summit 2026 — Solo-built (97.2% of 544 commits across ~1 year) Gen-2 platform for
FemTech Weekend — China's first women's-health-technology
organisation, founded 2024 by Zhu Yihan in Chengdu. Docusaurus 3.9
editorial spine + Drizzle ORM on Neon serverless Postgres for form
data + 15 Cloudflare Pages Functions (~3,800 LOC) for the entire
server side, deployed on Cloudflare Pages for low-latency
cross-border edge delivery. Currently powering Shanghai Summit 2026
(June 22-25, 16 confirmed speakers headlined by Ida Tin — Clue
co-founder who coined the term "FemTech" in 2016): 10-section
summit homepage + 3 sub-pages, 4 application pipelines
(programme/speaker/pitch/ecosystem) writing to typed Postgres
tables, double-opt-in newsletter wired to a Resend segment, admin
dashboard with batch review + brand-styled approval/rejection email
automation, Gemini-powered chatbot, bilingual EN/简体中文 across
every surface.

[tier=primary · recency=active · updated=2026-05-19]
- **URL:** https://www.femtechweekend.com/
- **Tech stack:** Docusaurus 3.9.2 (classic preset + @docusaurus/theme-mermaid), React 18.2 + TypeScript 5.6 (typecheck via `tsc --noEmit` in CI), Drizzle ORM 0.45 + drizzle-kit 0.31 (3 migrations: 0000_luxuriant_riptide → 0001_programme_overhaul → 0002_speaker_overhaul), Neon serverless Postgres (@neondatabase/serverless 1.0), Cloudflare Pages + Cloudflare Pages Functions (15 endpoints, ~3,839 LOC of server-side .js), Wrangler 4.0 (deployment + local dev via `wrangler pages dev build`), @notionhq/client v3 (Notion-as-CMS for non-technical founder editorial workflow), Resend REST API (Contacts API with segment assignment + transactional email), Google Generative AI 0.24 (Gemini-powered /api/chat-stream), Cloudinary (image uploads: headshots, company overview PDFs, pitch decks), Radix UI primitives (Accordion, Avatar, Checkbox, Collapsible, Dialog, Label, Select, Separator, Slot), Tailwind CSS 3.4 + tailwindcss-animate + class-variance-authority, Framer Motion 12.35 + GSAP 3.13 (scroll + reveal animations), earth-flyline 1.6 (interactive 2D world-map with count-up statistics), i18n-iso-countries (country-code rendering across speaker/partner data), gray-matter (MDX frontmatter parsing), Docusaurus i18n (EN + 简体中文 across every page from day one), Node 22.x (pinned in engines)

**Summary:** Solo-built Gen-2 platform (97.2% of 544 commits across ~1 year) for
FemTech Weekend, China's first women's-health-technology
organisation. Docusaurus 3.9 editorial spine + Drizzle ORM on Neon
Postgres + 15 Cloudflare Pages Functions deployed on Cloudflare
Pages. Currently powering Shanghai Summit 2026 (June 22-25, Ida Tin
headlines, 16 confirmed speakers) with a 10-section summit homepage,
3 sub-pages, 4 typed application pipelines (pitch / programme /
speaker / ecosystem), a double-opt-in newsletter wired to a Resend
segment, an admin dashboard with ADMIN_API_KEY-protected batch
review and brand-styled approval/rejection email automation, a
Gemini-powered chatbot, and bilingual EN / 简体中文 across every
surface. Built CTO-side under founder Zhu Yihan.

**Impact:** Gen-2 platform (Docusaurus, a deliberate rewrite away from Next.js for editorial focus) currently operating Shanghai Summit 2026 with 16 confirmed speakers including FemTech term originator Ida Tin — 97.2% solo on 544 commits, hosting 2 original research reports.

**Problem:** FemTech Weekend is China's first organisation built specifically
around women's-health technology — a community, research outfit,
and event producer rolled into one, headquartered in Chengdu,
